topics.title: World Class Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** World Class Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is defined as the systematic, integrated, and results-oriented approach to manufacturing that emphasizes the continuous pursuit of excellence across all functions of an enterprise. It represents the **survival strategy** for organizations operating in a global, highly competitive market, where every process is benchmarked against the best-in-class standards.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (OEMET835), World Class Manufacturing is an **umbrella concept** that integrates principles of Total Quality Management (TQM), Just-In-Time (JIT), Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, Total Productive Maintenance (TPM), and Agile Manufacturing into a unified strategic model. The goal is to deliver products and services of the highest quality at the **lowest total cost**, with the **shortest lead time**, and with the **highest customer value**.

Mathematically, the philosophy can be expressed conceptually as:

$$\text{WCM Performance} = f(\text{Quality}, \text{Cost}, \text{Delivery}, \text{Flexibility}, \text{Innovation})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Five-Star Restaurant vs. a Roadside Diner**
> Imagine two food outlets. The roadside diner serves food that is *okay* — inconsistent taste, slow service, sometimes dirty tables. The five-star restaurant, however, runs like a Swiss watch: every chef knows their station, ingredients are pre-portioned to the gram (JIT), the kitchen is spotless (5S/TPM), wait time is a science, and customer feedback drives the next day's menu (Continuous Improvement).
> 
> The five-star restaurant is the **"World Class"** manufacturer. The diner represents a traditional factory. WCM is the operating manual that turns the diner into the five-star establishment — not by changing the menu, but by transforming **how** the food is prepared and delivered.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of a World Class Company

A true WCM enterprise exhibits the following measurable traits:

- **Customer-Centric Operations**: Voice of Customer (VoC) drives every design and process decision.
- **Defect-Free Output**: Targets a **Six Sigma** quality level (3.4 defects per million opportunities, **DPMO**).
- **Zero Downtime**: Equipment availability targets **>90%** (Overall Equipment Effectiveness, OEE).
- **Continuous Flow**: Lead time reduced to near-single-piece flow.
- **Empowered Workforce**: Operators are decision-makers on the shop floor (autonomation / *Jidoka*).
- **Agile Response**: New product introduction (NPI) cycles measured in **weeks, not months**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in WCM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are the **gold standard benchmarks** for any KTU-level WCM analysis:
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance × Quality
> - **Takt Time** = Available Production Time / Customer Demand
> - **First Pass Yield (FPY)** > 99%
> - **Inventory Turns** > 12 per year
> - **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** > 98%
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolution of Manufacturing Paradigms

To understand WCM, one must understand where it came from. The manufacturing landscape has evolved through distinct eras:

1. **Craft Production (Pre-1900)**: Skilled artisans, low volume, high customization, high cost.
2. **Mass Production (1900s–1970s)**: Henry Ford's assembly line. High volume, low customization, standardized products. The trade-off was **rigidity**.
3. **Lean Production (1970s–1990s)**: Toyota Production System (TPS). Eliminated waste (*Muda*), achieved flexibility with low inventory.
4. **Agile Manufacturing (1990s–2000s)**: Responded to volatile markets with mass customization.
5. **World Class Manufacturing (2000s–Present)**: The convergence point — integrates Lean, Six Sigma, TPM, digitalization (Industry 4.0), and sustainability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** WCM is **not a replacement** for Lean or TQM. It is the **strategic umbrella** under which Lean (waste reduction), Six Sigma (variation reduction), and TPM (equipment reliability) are deployed as tactical tools.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of WCM

WCM rests on **three foundational pillars** (a high-frequency KTU 2024 question):

| Pillar | Core Focus | Key Tool / Philosophy | Primary Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1: Cost & Quality** | Eliminate defects and reduce variation | Six Sigma, TQM, Kaizen | DPMO, Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) |
| **Pillar 2: Delivery & Speed** | Compress lead time, ensure flow | Just-In-Time (JIT), Lean | Takt Time, Lead Time, OTD |
| **Pillar 3: Safety & Morale** | Zero accidents, engaged workforce | TPM, 5S, Autonomation | LTIR (Lost Time Injury Rate), OEE |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for OEMET835 Module 1**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | % (target $\geq 85\%$) | Measures equipment effectiveness |
| Availability (A) | $A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | % | Captures downtime losses |
| Performance (P) | $P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | % | Captures speed losses |
| Quality (Q) | $Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | % | Captures defect losses |
| Takt Time | $T_t = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | seconds/unit | Sets production pace |
| Cycle Time | $C_t = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Number of Units}}$ | seconds/unit | Actual process speed |
| Inventory Turns | $IT = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | turns/year | Capital efficiency |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma = f(\text{DPMO})$ | $\sigma$ | Defect rate inverse |
| Productivity | $\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | ratio | Resource utilization |
| Value-Added Ratio | $\text{VAR} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100$ | % | Lean efficiency indicator |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **plant audits, ISO certifications, and TPM scorecards**. Industries ranging from automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki's WCM journey) to semiconductor fabs (Intel, TSMC) rely on these metrics to measure their "World Class" status.

## 2.4 Why WCM? The Survival Imperative

A simple cost-quality-speed triangle governs global competition:

- If **Quality** falls → customers leave.
- If **Cost** rises → margins vanish.
- If **Delivery** is slow → competitors win.
- WCM is the **only** strategy that simultaneously attacks all three corners.

## 2.5 The WCM Roadmap (Logical Steps)

1. **Step 1 — Top Management Commitment**: Without C-level sponsorship, WCM fails.
2. **Step 2 — Assessment & Benchmarking**: Measure current OEE, DPMO, OTD.
3. **Step 3 — Pillar Deployment**: Launch cost, delivery, and safety pillars.
4. **Step 4 — Tool Implementation**: Roll out 5S, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, SMED.
5. **Step 5 — Skill Development**: Train multi-skilled operators.
6. **Step 6 — Continuous Improvement (CI)**: PDCA cycles forever.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: OEE Derivation for a CNC Machining Cell

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A CNC cell operates for **480 minutes** per shift. There are **2 breakdowns** lasting 30 minutes each. The ideal cycle time is **1.5 minutes per part**. During the shift, **280 parts** are produced, of which **15** are rejected for quality. Calculate OEE and classify the plant as per world-class standards.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters
- Planned Production Time (PPT) = 480 min
- Downtime = 2 × 30 = 60 min
- Ideal Cycle Time = 1.5 min/part
- Total Parts Produced = 280
- Rejected Parts = 15
- Good Parts = 280 − 15 = 265

### Step 2: Calculate Operating Time
$$\text{Operating Time} = \text{PPT} - \text{Downtime} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Availability (A)
$$A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{PPT}} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$

### Step 4: Calculate Performance (P)
$$\text{Expected Output at Ideal Speed} = \frac{420}{1.5} = 280 \text{ parts}$$

$$P = \frac{\text{Total Parts}}{\text{Expected Parts}} = \frac{280}{280} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

### Step 5: Calculate Quality (Q)
$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}} = \frac{265}{280} = 0.9464 = 94.64\%$$

### Step 6: Calculate OEE
$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 1.0 \times 0.9464$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.8281 = 82.81\%$$

### Step 7: Classify Against World-Class Standard

| OEE Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| $\geq 85\%$ | **World Class** |
| 60% – 84% | Typical / Average |
| 40% – 59% | Needs Improvement |
| $< 40\%$ | In Crisis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The cell scores **82.81%**, placing it in the "Typical" band — **not yet world class**. The primary loss is **Availability (downtime)** and **Quality (rejection rate)**. The plant must launch a TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) program to eliminate breakdowns and a Six Sigma project to reduce defects.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Manufacturing Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-frequency KTU 2024 Part A question.**

| Dimension | Mass Production | Lean Production | Six Sigma | World Class Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | High volume, low unit cost | Eliminate waste (*Muda*) | Reduce variation (defects) | Holistic excellence |
| **Origin** | Henry Ford (1913) | Toyota (1950s) | Motorola (1986) | Schonberger / Hayes (2000s) |
| **Inventory** | High (buffer stock) | Minimal (JIT) | Not primary focus | Optimized, demand-driven |
| **Quality Focus** | Inspection at end | Built-in (Poka-Yoke) | Statistical (DMAIC) | Zero-defect culture |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Short | Variable | Shortest possible |
| **Flexibility** | Low | Medium | Low | High (Mass Customization) |
| **Key Metric** | Output volume | Takt Time, Lead Time | DPMO, $\sigma$ level | OEE, OTD, VOR (Value-Over-Ratio) |
| **Worker Role** | Task specialist | Multi-skilled | Project-based | Empowered decision-maker |
| **Supplier Linkage** | Adversarial | Partnership | Limited | Strategic alliance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The WCM Conceptual Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tool fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff
    classDef metric fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef goal fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    WCM["WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING"]:::goal

    P1["PILLAR 1: COST AND QUALITY"]:::pillar
    P2["PILLAR 2: DELIVERY AND SPEED"]:::pillar
    P3["PILLAR 3: SAFETY AND MORALE"]:::pillar

    T1["Six Sigma"]:::tool
    T2["TQM"]:::tool
    T3["Kaizen"]:::tool
    T4["JIT"]:::tool
    T5["Lean Tools"]:::tool
    T6["TPM"]:::tool
    T7["5S Methodology"]:::tool
    T8["Autonomation"]:::tool

    M1["DPMO Target: 3.4"]:::metric
    M2["OEE Target: Greater than 85 percent"]:::metric
    M3["LTIR Target: Zero Accidents"]:::metric

    WCM --> P1
    WCM --> P2
    WCM --> P3

    P1 --> T1
    P1 --> T2
    P1 --> T3
    P2 --> T4
    P2 --> T5
    P3 --> T6
    P3 --> T7
    P3 --> T8

    T1 --> M1
    T4 --> M2
    T6 --> M3
```

## 4.2 The WCM Implementation Roadmap (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stage fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A["STAGE 1: TOP MANAGEMENT COMMITMENT"]:::stage
    B["STAGE 2: BASELINE AUDIT AND BENCHMARKING"]:::stage
    C{"GAP ANALYSIS: ARE WE WORLD CLASS?"}:::decision
    D["STAGE 3: PILLAR DEPLOYMENT"]:::stage
    E["STAGE 4: TOOL IMPLEMENTATION"]:::stage
    F["STAGE 5: SKILL MULTIPLICATION"]:::stage
    G["STAGE 6: CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT LOOP"]:::stage

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- NO --> D
    C -- YES --> G
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -.FEEDBACK.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** from Stage 6 back to Stage 2. WCM is **never finished** — it is a continuous journey. Even world-class plants re-audit annually to maintain their status.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define World Class Manufacturing. List any four characteristics of a WCM company.**

**Model Answer:**
World Class Manufacturing (WCM) is an integrated manufacturing philosophy aimed at achieving the highest standards of quality, cost, delivery, safety, and morale by continuously improving all processes and benchmarking against the best-in-class globally.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Customer-Centricity**: Every process is designed around Voice of Customer (VoC) requirements.
2. **Zero-Defect Quality**: Operations target Six Sigma performance (3.4 DPMO).
3. **Shortest Lead Time**: Production operates on single-piece flow / JIT principles.
4. **Empowered Workforce**: Frontline employees have the authority to stop the line (*Jidoka*).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is OEE? Write the formula and state the world-class benchmark value.**

**Model Answer:**
OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) is a composite metric that measures the percentage of manufacturing time that is truly productive.

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

- **World-Class Benchmark:** $\text{OEE} \geq 85\%$
- Availability captures **downtime** losses.
- Performance captures **speed** losses.
- Quality captures **defect** losses.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for benchmark value)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 1 typically carries internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive WCM Analysis**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the evolution of manufacturing systems from craft production to World Class Manufacturing. Highlight the key limitations of mass production that necessitated the shift to WCM.**

**Model Solution:**

The evolution of manufacturing is a story of adapting to changing market realities — from low-volume customization to high-volume standardization, and finally to high-volume **plus** high-mix customization.

**1. Craft Production (Pre-1900)**
- Skilled artisans, low volume, high customization.
- *Limitation:* Extremely high cost, slow, not scalable.

**2. Mass Production (1900s)**
- Pioneered by Henry Ford's moving assembly line.
- Key principle: interchangeability of parts + division of labor.
- Achieved: dramatic cost reduction, high output.
- *Limitations:*
  - **Rigidity**: Designed for single product variants.
  - **High inventory**: Required buffer stock to absorb variability.
  - **Low worker morale**: Repetitive tasks, no empowerment.
  - **Long changeover times**: SMED was unknown.
  - **Quality issues**: Defects discovered at end-of-line inspection.

**3. Lean Production (1970s — Toyota)**
- Ohno's Toyota Production System eliminated *Muda* (waste).
- Introduced JIT, Kanban, 5S, Poka-Yoke.
- *Advantage over Mass:* Lower inventory, higher flexibility, better quality.
- *Limitation:* Primarily focused on waste, not statistical quality.

**4. Six Sigma (1986 — Motorola)**
- Introduced DMAIC to drive variation to near zero.
- *Limitation:* Strong on quality, weak on flow/lead time.

**5. World Class Manufacturing (2000s — Present)**
- Converges Lean + Six Sigma + TPM + Industry 4.0.
- Addresses **all four competitive dimensions** simultaneously: Quality, Cost, Delivery, Flexibility.
- Adds digital layer: IoT, AI-driven predictive maintenance, smart factories.

**Conclusion:** The mass production model's inability to handle customization, coupled with rising global competition, forced manufacturers to adopt WCM as a survival strategy.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, describe the three pillars of WCM. List two tools deployed under each pillar.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual representation.)*

WCM rests on **three interdependent pillars**:

**Pillar 1: Cost & Quality**
- *Objective:* Deliver defect-free products at the lowest total cost.
- *Tools:*
  1. **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** — statistical process control.
  2. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — organization-wide quality culture.

**Pillar 2: Delivery & Speed**
- *Objective:* Shortest lead time, perfect order fulfillment.
- *Tools:*
  1. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** — produce only what is needed, when needed.
  2. **Lean Tools (Kanban, Value Stream Mapping)** — eliminate non-value-added steps.

**Pillar 3: Safety & Morale**
- *Objective:* Zero accidents, zero breakdowns, engaged workforce.
- *Tools:*
  1. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — operator-owned equipment care.
  2. **5S Methodology** — Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.

**Integration:** The pillars are not silos. A defect (Pillar 1) stops the line (Pillar 2), and a breakdown (Pillar 3) creates a quality issue (Pillar 1). WCM succeeds only when all three pillars are advanced together.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — OEE Numerical + WCM Characteristics**

**(a) [7 Marks] A bottling plant operates two shifts of 8 hours each. In a day, total downtime is 90 minutes. The ideal filling rate is 1200 bottles/minute. Actual output is 1,10,000 bottles, of which 1,500 are rejected. Calculate OEE and comment on the world-class status of the plant.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Planned Production Time = 2 × 8 × 60 = 960 minutes
- Downtime = 90 minutes
- Ideal Rate = 1200 bottles/min
- Total Output = 1,10,000 bottles
- Rejected = 1,500 bottles
- Good Output = 1,10,000 − 1,500 = 1,08,500 bottles

**Step 2: Operating Time**
$$\text{Operating Time} = 960 - 90 = 870 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Availability**
$$A = \frac{870}{960} = 0.90625 = 90.625\%$$

**Step 4: Performance**
$$\text{Expected Output} = 870 \times 1200 = 10,44,000 \text{ bottles}$$

$$P = \frac{1,10,000}{10,44,000} = 0.1054 = 10.54\%$$

**Step 5: Quality**
$$Q = \frac{1,08,500}{1,10,000} = 0.9864 = 98.64\%$$

**Step 6: OEE Calculation**
$$\text{OEE} = 0.90625 \times 0.1054 \times 0.9864$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.0942 = 9.42\%$$

**Step 7: Comment**
- **OEE = 9.42%** is far below the world-class benchmark of **$\geq 85\%$**.
- The plant is in the **"Crisis" zone** ($< 40\%$).
- **Major Loss:** **Performance** is catastrophically low (10.54%) — the actual output is only ~10% of the theoretical capacity.
- **Recommendation:** Investigate chronic speed losses (small stoppages, reduced speed operation), then deploy SMED and autonomous maintenance.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between Lean Manufacturing and World Class Manufacturing. Why is WCM considered a "strategic umbrella" over Lean?**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Lean Manufacturing | World Class Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Tactical — focuses on shop floor | Strategic — covers entire enterprise |
| **Primary Goal** | Eliminate *Muda* (waste) | Holistic excellence (quality, cost, delivery, safety, morale) |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System (1950s) | Schonberger, Hayes, Wheelwright (1980s–2000s) |
| **Key Tools** | 5S, Kanban, VSM, JIT | Six Sigma, TPM, TQM, Lean, Industry 4.0 |
| **Quality Focus** | Built-in quality (Poka-Yoke) | Statistical quality (Six Sigma) + built-in |
| **Metrics** | Lead Time, Takt Time | OEE, OTD, DPMO, LTIR |
| **Technology** | Manual / low-tech | Digital — IoT, AI, Big Data |
| **Sustainability** | Not a primary focus | Embedded in WCM (Green Manufacturing) |

**Why WCM is a "Strategic Umbrella":**

1. **Integration**: WCM does not replace Lean — it **absorbs** Lean as one of its pillars. Lean handles waste; WCM adds variation control (Six Sigma) and equipment reliability (TPM).
2. **Holistic Coverage**: Lean alone cannot address equipment breakdowns or statistical quality problems. WCM does.
3. **Digital Extension**: WCM naturally extends to Industry 4.0 (smart factories), whereas Lean remains largely manual.
4. **Customer-Centricity**: WCM places the Voice of Customer at the center; Lean is internally focused on waste.

> **Conclusion:** Lean is a **tactical weapon**; WCM is the **strategic doctrine** that decides when and how to deploy Lean, Six Sigma, and TPM together.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Lean with WCM** — stating that WCM *replaces* Lean. (WCM *includes* Lean; it is not a replacement.) → **−2 marks**
> 2. **OEE Numerical Errors** — confusing *Operating Time* with *Load Time*, or forgetting to subtract rejected parts from total output. → **−1 to −2 marks**
> 3. **Listing without explaining** — in 7-mark questions, merely listing tools (5S, Kaizen) without linking them to the WCM pillar scores poorly. Always **state which pillar** the tool supports. → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Ignoring Morale/Safety** — many students discuss only Quality and Cost, forgetting the third pillar (Safety & Morale). → **−2 marks**
> 5. **No real-world examples** — for a 7-marker, at least **one industrial example** (Toyota, Maruti, Bosch) elevates the answer to a full-mark band.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: World Class Manufacturing**

- **Definition**: WCM = Integrated, results-oriented pursuit of excellence across **Quality, Cost, Delivery, Safety, Morale**.
- **Three Pillars**: *(1) Cost & Quality*, *(2) Delivery & Speed*, *(3) Safety & Morale*.
- **Origin**: Schonberger (1980s), evolved from Mass → Lean → Six Sigma → WCM.
- **OEE Formula**: $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ → **World Class $\geq 85\%$**.
- **Six Sigma**: $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (**DPMO**).
- **Takt Time**: $T_t = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ — sets the heartbeat of production.
- **Lean ≠ WCM**: Lean is a tool; WCM is the strategy that deploys it.
- **Key Tools Map**: Pillar 1 → Six Sigma, TQM, Kaizen | Pillar 2 → JIT, Kanban, VSM | Pillar 3 → TPM, 5S, Autonomation.
- **Continuous Improvement**: WCM has **no end-state** — it is a perpetual PDCA cycle.
- **Industry 4.0 Synergy**: Modern WCM integrates IoT, AI-based predictive maintenance, and digital twins.
- **India Context**: Maruti Suzuki's WCM journey (2012 onwards) reduced rejection rates from **5% to <0.5%** in 5 years — a textbook case study worth citing.

> **Final Tip:** Always answer WCM questions using the **Pillar Framework** — it is the examiner's mental checklist. If your answer touches all three pillars with at least one tool each, you are guaranteed a 7/7 on the conceptual sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Manufacturing is the integrated set of operations, processes, and systems that transform raw materials, components, or information into finished products with **specified quality, cost, and delivery** characteristics, while adding **value** to satisfy customer requirements.

In the broadest sense, manufacturing encompasses every activity from product design, process planning, production, quality assurance, and logistics, to after-sales service. It is the backbone of any industrial economy and constitutes the **second** stage of the complete value chain: *procurement → manufacturing → distribution*.

## 1.2 Definition of World Class Manufacturing (WCM)

> [!NOTE]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM):** A **strategic management philosophy** and an integrated set of **principles, practices, and tools** that enables an organization to produce goods and services of the **highest quality, at the lowest cost, with the shortest lead time**, while continuously improving to achieve excellence on a global benchmark scale.

The term was popularized in the 1980s when Japanese manufacturers began dominating global markets with products that were simultaneously cheaper, better, and faster. Researchers like **Richard Schonberger** and **Robert Hall** documented these practices, and the phrase became the gold standard for operational excellence.

## 1.3 Definitions by Eminent Scholars (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Author / Source | Definition of WCM |
|---|---|
| **Richard J. Schonberger** | Producing world-class products of the highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time through continuous improvement of people, processes, and technology. |
| **Robert W. Hall** | A company-wide commitment to pursue **perfection** by eliminating all forms of waste (muda), inconsistency (mura), and overburden (muri). |
| **Colin Gunn** | A firm that competes on the basis of **quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and service** at globally benchmarked levels. |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Manufacturing at world-class levels means driving every process toward the **ideal state**—zero defects, zero waste, zero delays. |
| **M. J. Stahl** | A total management philosophy that integrates **Just-In-Time (JIT), TQM, TPM, Kaizen, and employee involvement** into a single coherent competitive system. |
| **KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement** | A holistic system that achieves operational excellence by aligning people, processes, and technology with customer value. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Examiners often award 2–3 marks simply for quoting **two different expert definitions** correctly. Always pair the Schonberger definition with one other scholar.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Olympic Athlete of Industry

Imagine two runners training for the **Olympics**. An ordinary factory is like a casual jogger who runs whenever the weather permits. A **World Class Manufacturer** is the Olympic athlete:
- **Trains every day** (continuous improvement — Kaizen).
- **Measures every heartbeat and step** (KPIs, Six Sigma metrics).
- **Has a coach, physiologist, and nutritionist** working together (integrated systems — JIT + TQM + TPM).
- **Aims for a personal best, then breaks it** (benchmarking + breakthrough improvement).

The athlete is not "born" world class. World class is a **journey**, not a destination.

## 1.5 The "World Class" Benchmark

What does "world class" actually mean numerically? It means operating at the **top decile (10 %)** of global performance for your industry in:
- Quality (defect rate < 3.4 DPMO — Six Sigma level)
- Cost (lowest quartile unit cost)
- Delivery (OTD > 98 %)
- Lead time (cycle time reduced by 90 % from baseline)
- Flexibility (mix-models without penalty)
- Safety (zero accidents)

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** "World class" is **relative** to the global industry benchmark, not an absolute number. A small bakery can be world class in its segment, just as Toyota is in automotive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of the WCM Definition

When the KTU syllabus says "Definition of WCM", it expects you to articulate **what makes a manufacturer world class**. The accepted answer always contains the following **five competitive priorities**:

1. **Quality (Q)** — Zero-defect philosophy, Six Sigma levels, customer-perceived excellence.
2. **Cost (C)** — Lowest total cost of ownership through waste elimination.
3. **Delivery (D)** — On-time delivery > 98 %, shortest possible lead time.
4. **Flexibility (F)** — Volume flexibility, mix flexibility, design flexibility.
5. **Service (S)** — Lifetime support, after-sales responsiveness.

These are universally abbreviated as **Q-CDS-FS** or **Q-C-D-F-S**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these five?** They were empirically derived by **Hill (1985)** and refined by **Gunn (1987)** as the dimensions on which global customers evaluate manufacturers. KTU examiners treat this list as a **must-memorize** structure.

## 2.2 The Three Sub-Systems Inside WCM

WCM is **not a single technique**—it is a federation of proven methodologies:

| Sub-System | Full Form | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **JIT** | Just-In-Time | Produce only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed. |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management | Embed quality in every employee, every process, every step. |
| **TPM** | Total Productive Maintenance | Achieve zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents through operator-driven maintenance. |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous Improvement | Small, incremental, daily improvements by every employee. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A 14-mark Part-B question on "Definition of WCM" almost always includes the sub-question: *"Explain the major elements/pillars of WCM."* Memorize the **JIT + TQM + TPM + Kaizen** quartet.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although WCM is conceptual, KTU numerical problems in Module 1 use the following foundational metrics. **Master these formulas—they appear in every KTU OEMET835 question paper.**

| Metric | Formula | Standard Unit | World-Class Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Productivity | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | units / hour | Top 10 % of industry |
| OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $\text{OEE} = A \times P_e \times Q$ | percentage | $\geq 85\,\%$ |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units at first attempt}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | percentage | $\geq 99\,\%$ |
| Cycle Time (CT) | $\text{CT} = \text{Process end time} - \text{Process start time}$ | seconds | Minimized |
| Takt Time (TT) | $\text{TT} = \dfrac{\text{Available production time}}{\text{Customer demand}}$ | seconds / unit | Equals demand rhythm |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | defects / million | $\leq 3.4$ (Six Sigma) |
| Value-Added Ratio (VAR) | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{\text{Value-added time}}{\text{Total lead time}}$ | ratio (0–1) | $\rightarrow 1$ |
| On-Time Delivery (OTD) | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders delivered on time}}{\text{Total orders}} \times 100\,\%$ | percentage | $\geq 98\,\%$ |
| Inventory Turns (IT) | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | turns / year | $\geq 12$ |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal failure} + \text{External failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | ₹ / unit | $\rightarrow 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, every vertical bar that *would* normally denote absolute value or "such that" has been written as `\vert` or `\mid` in actual LaTeX to keep the markdown table intact. The rendered formulas above are clean and safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The WCM definition is not an academic curiosity. It is the **operating charter** for:

- **Automotive OEM plants** (Toyota, Tata Motors) targeting OEE $\geq 85\,\%$.
- **Aerospace component shops** targeting FPY $\geq 99.99\,\%$ for flight-critical parts.
- **Electronics contract manufacturers** (Foxconn, Wistron) where takt time can be as low as **15 seconds per smartphone module**.
- **Indian MSMEs** participating in the **ZED (Zero Defect Zero Effect)** certification scheme of the Government of India, which is the Indian adaptation of WCM.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked *"Why is WCM relevant to India?"*, mention the **ZED Certification Scheme** (Make-in-India initiative) and the **Toyota Production System** as global role models.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — Accessible Logic

- **Why Quality?** Because defects are pure waste—the customer does not pay for a defective part.
- **Why Cost?** Because a manufacturer selling at 10 % above market cost will lose price-sensitive global customers.
- **Why Delivery?** Because late delivery invalidates the prior two—Q and C are useless if the part arrives in March for a January requirement.
- **Why Flexibility?** Because today's batch-of-1000 is tomorrow's batch-of-1 (mass customization).
- **Why Service?** Because the sale is not the end; the **lifetime value** of the customer is the real revenue.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Evolution of Manufacturing — A Step-by-Step Walk-Through

The very word "world class" only makes sense when you know **where manufacturing has come from**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to narrate the **evolutionary journey** in any definition question. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1 — Craft Production (Pre-1760)

A single skilled artisan produced the entire product, one piece at a time.

- **Output:** 1 unit / day
- **Quality:** Variable, depending on the artisan's mood
- **Cost:** Extremely high
- **Example:** A 17th-century sword-smith

### Step 2 — Mass Production (1760–1913, Eli Whitney & Henry Ford)

Interchangeable parts and the moving assembly line (Ford Highland Park, 1913) standardized output.

- **Output:** 1 Model-T every 93 minutes (down from 12 hours)
- **Quality:** Uniform, but inspection-based
- **Cost:** Reduced by **5×** over craft methods
- **Example:** Ford Model-T production line

### Step 3 — Lean Production / Toyota Production System (1950–1980)

Taiichi Ohno introduced **JIT** and **Jidoka (autonomation)** at Toyota. This was the **first system** to simultaneously pursue quality, cost, and delivery.

- **Output:** 1 unit every 15 seconds at a Toyota line
- **Quality:** Built-in (defect stops the line — *Andon cord*)
- **Waste eliminated:** 7 wastes (*muda*), 1 waste of overburden (*muri*), 1 waste of unevenness (*mura*)

### Step 4 — World Class Manufacturing (1980–Present)

Researchers (Schonberger, Hall, Womack & Jones) observed that Toyota's edge came not from a single trick but from an **integrated management philosophy**. They coined the term "World Class Manufacturing" to describe the **ideal end-state** every factory should chase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logical Chain to Remember:** *Craft → Mass → Lean → World Class*. This four-step ladder is the most-cited evolution answer in KTU OEMET835 exam papers.

### Step 3.2 — Mathematical Derivation: OEE from First Principles

OEE is the most-tested numerical formula in WCM. The KTU board often asks students to **derive OEE from its three sub-components**. Here is the exhaustive derivation.

**Given:** A CNC machine operates for one 8-hour shift.

$$
\text{Planned Production Time} = 8 \text{ hours} = 480 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Availability ($A$)**

Availability accounts for **downtime losses** (breakdowns, changeovers, setup).

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{\text{Planned Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

Suppose downtime = 48 minutes.

$$
A = \dfrac{480 - 48}{480} = \dfrac{432}{480} = 0.90 = 90\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance ($P_e$)**

Performance accounts for **speed losses** (running slower than ideal cycle time).

$$
P_e = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

Suppose ideal cycle time = 1 minute / piece, and total pieces produced = 380.

$$
P_e = \dfrac{1 \times 380}{432} = \dfrac{380}{432} = 0.8796 = 87.96\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality ($Q$)**

Quality accounts for **defect losses** (rejects and rework).

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
$$

Suppose 360 pieces are good out of 380.

$$
Q = \dfrac{360}{380} = 0.9474 = 94.74\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P_e \times Q
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.90 \times 0.8796 \times 0.9474
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7500 = 75.00\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** World-class OEE benchmark is **85 %**. The machine above, at 75 %, has a 10 % improvement opportunity — a typical opening line for any KTU improvement-based question.

### Step 3.3 — Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major Manufacturing Paradigms

The KTU 2024 syllabus often asks a **comparison table** of manufacturing systems. Below is the exhaustive analytical matrix you should reproduce in your answer script.

| Dimension | Craft Production | Mass Production | Lean Production | World Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | Pre-1760 | 1760–1950 | 1950–1980 | 1980–present |
| **Output Volume** | Low (1–10) | High (1000s) | High + variety | High + variety + customization |
| **Variety** | High | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Quality Approach** | Inspection of finished | Statistical QC (Shewhart) | Built-in (Jidoka) | Zero-defect (Six Sigma) |
| **Cost Focus** | Material cost | Labor cost | Inventory cost | Total cost of ownership |
| **Lead Time** | Days–weeks | Days | Hours | Hours–minutes |
| **Wastes Addressed** | None | Defects | 7 muda + muri + mura | All wastes, all processes |
| **Workforce Skill** | Master craftsman | Semi-skilled | Multi-skilled | Multi-skilled + empowered |
| **Supplier Relation** | Local | Adversarial | Partnership | Strategic alliance |
| **Key Proponent** | Medieval guilds | Henry Ford | Taiichi Ohno | Schonberger / Hall |
| **Example Company** | Local blacksmith | Ford Model-T plant | Toyota (1980s) | Toyota, Tesla, Boeing |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question titled *"Distinguish between Mass Production and World Class Manufacturing"* requires a **comparison table** (above) and **three to four explanatory sentences** for each row. Always lead with the **year of origin** and end with a **contemporary example**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the "World Class Threshold" — A Logical Proof

The KTU definition is silent on **what number constitutes "world class"**. The board, however, expects students to justify the **85 % OEE benchmark**. Here is the logical derivation:

**Step 1 — World-class availability** = 90 % (≤ 10 % planned + unplanned downtime, achieved by TPM).

**Step 2 — World-class performance** = 95 % (running at ideal cycle time 95 % of the time, achieved by SMED and line balancing).

**Step 3 — World-class quality** = 99 % (≤ 1 % defectives, achieved by Poka-Yoke and Six Sigma).

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
\text{OEE}_{\text{World Class}} = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.99
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_{\text{World Class}} = 0.84645 \approx 85\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is the single most-valued 4-line proof in Module 1 of OEMET835.** Practice writing it from memory. Examiners award full marks only when **all three thresholds are stated AND multiplied** in the correct sequence.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Evolutionary Ladder from Craft to World Class

The following Mermaid block renders the **four-stage manufacturing evolution** described in Section 3.1. It is the diagram you should draw on a KTU answer sheet when the question is *"Explain the evolution of manufacturing systems."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB["STAGE 2: MASS PRODUCTION"]:::mass
    nodeC["STAGE 3: LEAN PRODUCTION"]:::lean
    nodeD["STAGE 4: WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING"]:::wcm

    nodeA -->|Industrial Revolution 1760| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Toyoda Looms 1926  Ohno 1950| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Schonberger 1986  Hall 1987| nodeD

    subgraph PILLARS["WCM INTEGRATED PILLARS"]
        pillar1["JIT Just In Time"]
        pillar2["TQM Total Quality Management"]
        pillar3["TPM Total Productive Maintenance"]
        pillar4["KAIZEN Continuous Improvement"]
        pillar1 --- pillar2
        pillar2 --- pillar3
        pillar3 --- pillar4
    end

    nodeD -. integrates .-> pillar1
    nodeD -. integrates .-> pillar2
    nodeD -. integrates .-> pillar3
    nodeD -. integrates .-> pillar4

    classDef craft fill:#E8DCC4,stroke:#8B6F47,color:#000000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:** Trace the **arrows** top-to-bottom for chronological evolution. The bottom subgraph shows the **four integrated pillars** that the WCM definition unifies. The dashed lines from `nodeD` to each pillar convey that WCM **integrates** (not replaces) the earlier systems.

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a WCM plant. It explains *how* the abstract definition translates into a real factory floor.

The five colored tool-modules (JIT, TQM, TPM, Kaizen, Six Sigma) are shown as **dashed support lines**, which examiners interpret as *"these are embedded in every stage"*.

The KTU definition always cites the **five competitive priorities**. The wheel diagram below visually anchors them around the central goal of **Customer Satisfaction**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q1.** Define World Class Manufacturing. Mention **any two** characteristics of a WCM firm.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** World Class Manufacturing is a strategic management philosophy that integrates Just-In-Time, Total Quality Management, Total Productive Maintenance, and Kaizen to produce goods of the **highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time**, thereby achieving global benchmark standards.
>
> **Characteristic 1 (1 mark):** Continuous improvement in every process, every day, by every employee (the Kaizen principle).
>
> **Characteristic 2 (1 mark):** Customer-driven quality — quality is defined by the customer, not the factory, and the firm targets zero defects.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** List the **five competitive priorities** on which a world class manufacturer competes and state the world-class target for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Priority | World-Class Target |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality | $\leq 3.4$ defects per million opportunities (Six Sigma) |
| 2 | Cost | Lowest quartile of global industry cost curve |
| 3 | Delivery | On-time delivery $\geq 98\,\%$ |
| 4 | Flexibility | Volume and mix changeover $\leq 10$ minutes (SMED) |
| 5 | Service | 24×7 lifetime customer support, $\geq 95\,\%$ first-call resolution |

*(1 mark for the list, 1 mark for the targets, 1 mark for the specific numerical values.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Part-B "Definition of WCM" questions, students commonly lose marks in the following three ways:
> 1. **Defining WCM without quoting an expert** (lose 2 marks — always attribute the definition to Schonberger / Hall / Gunn).
> 2. **Listing the four pillars but not explaining the integration logic** (lose 3 marks — examiners want to see *why* JIT + TQM + TPM work together, not just a bullet list).
> 3. **Forgetting to derive the 85 % OEE benchmark** (lose 2 marks — a numerical anchor converts a vague answer into a rigorous one).

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q3 (a).** Define World Class Manufacturing in detail. Quote the definitions of **at least two** experts and explain the **major elements** that constitute a WCM system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b).** With the aid of a suitable diagram, describe the **evolution of manufacturing systems** from craft production to world class manufacturing, highlighting the key contributions of **Henry Ford** and **Taiichi Ohno**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3 (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Formal Definition (2 marks)**

World Class Manufacturing (WCM) is a **holistic management philosophy** that integrates the principles of Just-In-Time (JIT), Total Quality Management (TQM), Total Productive Maintenance (TPM), and Kaizen to produce goods and services of the **highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time**, achieving global benchmark performance on every competitive priority.

**Step 2 — Expert Quotations (2 marks, 1 each)**

- **Richard J. Schonberger (1986):** WCM produces the highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead-time products through continuous improvement of **people, processes, and technology**.
- **Robert W. Hall (1987):** WCM is a company-wide commitment to pursue **perfection** by systematically eliminating **muda** (waste), **mura** (inconsistency), and **muri** (overburden) in every process.

**Step 3 — Major Elements of WCM (3 marks)**

1. **JIT (Just-In-Time):** Produces only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed. Reduces inventory, lead time, and storage cost.
2. **TQM (Total Quality Management):** Embeds quality into every employee, every process, every step. Targets the *ideal state* of zero defects.
3. **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance):** Achieves zero breakdowns, zero accidents, and zero defects through operator-driven, preventive maintenance.
4. **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement):** Empowers every worker—from the shop-floor to the CEO—to make small, daily, incremental improvements.
5. **Integration Logic:** These four elements are not independent; they form a **federated system** in which JIT pulls demand, TQM ensures quality, TPM guarantees machine availability, and Kaizen drives the system to evolve continuously.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3 (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Diagram (2 marks)**

Redraw the **Evolutionary Ladder** from Section 4.1 of these notes: a four-stage flowchart (Craft → Mass → Lean → WCM), each stage labelled with its era and an example.

**Step 2 — Craft Production (1 mark)**

Pre-1760. Single skilled artisans made complete products by hand. Low output, variable quality, high cost. Example: medieval blacksmith.

**Step 3 — Mass Production & Henry Ford (2 marks)**

Eli Whitney introduced **interchangeable parts** in 1799. **Henry Ford** combined this with the **moving assembly line** in 1913 at Highland Park. The Ford Model-T production time dropped from 12 hours to 93 minutes. Quality became uniform but relied on end-of-line inspection. The focus was *efficiency of the line*, not the worker.

**Step 4 — Lean Production & Taiichi Ohno (2 marks)**

**Taiichi Ohno**, Toyota engineer, developed the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** between 1950 and 1980. He invented two pillars: **JIT** (pull-based production) and **Jidoka** (autonomation — the line stops the moment a defect is detected, the *Andon cord*). TPS introduced the **7 wastes (muda)** and laid the foundation for WCM. The 1980s oil crisis rewarded Toyota's lean approach, and Western researchers (Womack & Jones, 1990, *The Machine That Changed the World*) coined "Lean Production" to describe it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Sub-Point:** Always end with the statement *"Schonberger (1986) and Hall (1987) generalized the Toyota system into the WCM framework, applicable to any industry"*. This line alone is worth 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **Q4 (a).** Define Manufacturing and World Class Manufacturing. Compare and contrast **Mass Production** with **World Class Manufacturing** along any **five dimensions**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b).** A CNC machine operates for one 8-hour shift. Planned production time is 480 minutes. The actual downtime is 60 minutes. Total pieces produced during the operating time are 360. The ideal cycle time is 1.0 minute per piece, and the number of good pieces is 330. Calculate the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** and comment on whether the machine is **world class**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4 (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Manufacturing (1 mark)**

Manufacturing is the integrated set of operations and processes that transform raw materials, components, or information into finished products with specified quality, cost, and delivery characteristics, while adding value for the customer.

**Step 2 — Definition of WCM (2 marks)**

World Class Manufacturing is a strategic management philosophy that produces the highest quality goods at the lowest cost and the shortest lead time, integrating JIT, TQM, TPM, and Kaizen to achieve global benchmark performance. (Quoting Schonberger earns an extra 0.5 mark.)

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (4 marks, 0.8 per row)**

| Dimension | Mass Production | World Class Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality approach** | End-of-line inspection, Statistical QC | Built-in quality, Poka-Yoke, Six Sigma |
| **Inventory** | Large buffer stocks to absorb variability | Minimal, pull-based (JIT) |
| **Lead time** | Days to weeks | Hours to minutes |
| **Workforce** | Semi-skilled, narrow tasks | Multi-skilled, empowered, problem-solving |
| **Supplier relationship** | Adversarial, multiple suppliers | Strategic partnership, few suppliers |
| **Variety** | Low (one product, few variants) | High (mass customization) |
| **Customer focus** | Internal, production-driven | External, customer-driven |

---

#### Model Solution for Q4 (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Planned production time = 480 min
- Downtime = 60 min
- Total pieces produced = 360
- Ideal cycle time = 1.0 min / piece
- Good pieces = 330

**Step 1 — Compute Availability (1 mark)**

$$
A = \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance (1.5 marks)**

$$
P_e = \dfrac{1.0 \times 360}{420} = \dfrac{360}{420} = 0.8571 = 85.71\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{330}{360} = 0.9167 = 91.67\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P_e \times Q
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.8571 \times 0.9167
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.6875 = 68.75\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (0.5 mark)**

The OEE is **68.75 %**, which is **far below the world-class benchmark of 85 %**. The machine is **not world class**; it requires improvement in **availability** (reduce downtime via TPM), **performance** (eliminate speed losses via SMED), and **quality** (implement Poka-Yoke and Six Sigma).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students forget to compute **Quality** first and use *Total Pieces Produced* instead of *Good Pieces* in the formula. Always remember: $Q$ uses **good pieces / total pieces**, not just total pieces.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the "Definition" Topic of OEMET835 Module 1**

- **WCM** is a **management philosophy**, not a single tool. Memorize this distinction — it is the most-asked definition question.
- **Five competitive priorities** of WCM: **Quality, Cost, Delivery, Flexibility, Service** (Q-C-D-F-S). Always list **all five** in the same order.
- **Four integrated pillars** of WCM: **JIT, TQM, TPM, Kaizen**. Add **Six Sigma** in modern industry contexts for full marks.
- **Three scholar definitions** to memorize: **Schonberger (1986)**, **Hall (1987)**, **Gunn (1987)**. Quote at least two in any 7-mark answer.
- **Evolutionary ladder:** *Craft → Mass → Lean → World Class*. The 1986/1987 cut-off for WCM is a frequently asked date.
- **World-class OEE benchmark = 85 %**, derived as $0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.99$. Practice this derivation.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY) target $\geq 99\,\%$; DPMO target $\leq 3.4$ (Six Sigma).**
- **The "world class" qualifier** is *relative to industry benchmark*, not absolute.
- **OEE formula** = Availability × Performance × Quality. Units are dimensionless ratios that, when multiplied, yield a percentage.
- **Indian context:** Mention **ZED Certification** (Make-in-India) and **Toyota Production System** as practical anchors.
- **Avoid** the common error of writing "WCM = Lean + Six Sigma + TQM". WCM is a *broader umbrella* that *contains* these systems, not a sum of them.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** A definition answer that quotes **Schonberger**, lists **five priorities**, names **four pillars**, draws the **evolution diagram**, and ends with the **85 % OEE proof** will reliably score **full marks** in any KTU 2024 paper on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historic Journey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Historic Journey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is an integrated, holistic, and multi-faceted manufacturing management philosophy that synthesizes the best practices derived from the global evolution of production systems. It represents the **culmination of a centuries-long historic journey** of human manufacturing advancement — transitioning from artisan craftsmanship through industrial mechanization, mass production, lean systems, and finally into digital, cyber-physical production ecosystems.

The **"Historic Journey"** refers to the chronological evolution of manufacturing paradigms from the pre-industrial era (Craft Production) to the modern era (Industry 4.0 / Industry 5.0), encompassing the philosophical, technological, and managerial transformations that have shaped contemporary production systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The historic journey establishes the *philosophical lineage* of WCM. Students must recognize that WCM is NOT an invention but an **amalgamation** of lessons learned from every previous manufacturing era.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of manufacturing history as the evolution of a **kitchen recipe** passed down through generations:

- **Grandmother's Era (Craft Production):** Every dish cooked by hand, customized to the eater's mood — slow, expensive, beautiful.
- **Industrial Era (Mass Production):** A factory kitchen producing 10,000 identical biryanis a day — fast, cheap, but no customization.
- **Japanese Innovation (Lean/TPS):** A master chef who cooks only when the customer is hungry (Just-in-Time), wastes no spice, and improves every dish after each customer feedback (Kaizen).
- **Modern Era (WCM & Industry 4.0):** A smart kitchen where sensors detect when ingredients run low, robots assist the chef, and every dish's quality is digitally tracked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** WCM is the "**chef's ultimate cookbook**" — it borrows the *soul* of craftsmanship, the *scale* of mass production, the *wisdom* of lean, and the *intelligence* of digital systems.

---

## 1.3 Foundational Constants, Metrics & Standards

| Term | Definition / Significance |
|---|---|
| **Takt Time** | The rate at which a finished product must be produced to meet customer demand |
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | World-class benchmark: **≥ 85%** (Availability × Performance × Quality) |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | World-class benchmark: **≥ 99%** |
| **Inventory Turns** | World-class benchmark: **≥ 24 turns/year** |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | World-class benchmark: **≥ 99%** |
| **Lead Time** | World-class benchmark: days, not weeks |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | Six Sigma benchmark: **≤ 3.4 DPMO** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are the **destination markers** of the historic journey. Each era contributed one or more of these metrics as its legacy.

---

## 1.4 The Six Eras of Manufacturing Evolution (Overview Map)

The historic journey can be broadly divided into **six interconnected eras**:

1. **Era 1 — Craft Production (Pre-1776)**
2. **Era 2 — Industrial Revolution 1.0 (1776 – 1870)** — Mechanization
3. **Era 3 — Industrial Revolution 2.0 (1870 – 1969)** — Mass Production & Electrification
4. **Era 4 — Industrial Revolution 3.0 (1969 – 2011)** — Computer & Automation
5. **Era 5 — Industrial Revolution 4.0 (2011 – Present)** — Cyber-Physical Systems
6. **Era 6 — Industrial Revolution 5.0 / WCM (Emerging)** — Human-Machine Collaboration
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Era-by-Era Deep Dive

### **Era 1 — Craft Production (Pre-1776)**

- **Core Idea:** Every product was hand-made by a skilled artisan.
- **Characteristics:**
  - High customization, low volume
  - Skill resided in the worker, not the process
  - Tools were simple (hammer, chisel, loom)
  - Quality depended on the individual craftsman
- **Limitation:** Not scalable; quality inconsistent across artisans.
- **Legacy:** Reminded modern manufacturers that **skill, pride, and craftsmanship** still matter (basis of WCM's human-centric pillars).

### **Era 2 — Industrial Revolution 1.0 (1776 – 1870)**

- **Trigger Events:**
  - James Watt's **steam engine** (1769, improved 1776)
  - **Eli Whitney's interchangeable parts** (1799, muskets for the U.S. Army)
  - **Eli Whitney's cotton gin** (1793)
- **Characteristics:**
  - Mechanization powered by **water and steam**
  - Transition from cottage industry to **factories**
  - Standardized, replaceable parts → enabled later assembly lines
- **Key Insight:** Standardization of parts = foundation of modern mass production.

### **Era 3 — Industrial Revolution 2.0 (1870 – 1969)**

- **Trigger Events:**
  - **Henry Ford's moving assembly line** (December 1, 1913, Highland Park Plant)
  - **Ford Model T** (1908–1927) — 15 million units sold
  - **Taylor's Scientific Management** (1911) — time-and-motion studies
  - Introduction of **electrical energy**
- **Characteristics:**
  - Division of labor → specialized workstations
  - Production time for Model T dropped from **12.5 hours to 93 minutes**
  - Mass production of standardized goods
  - **Fordism**: Low cost via high volume
- **Limitation:** Inflexible to customer variety; massive inventory buffers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ford's Famous Quote:** *"Any customer can have a car painted any color that he wants, so long as it is black."* — illustrating the trade-off between volume and variety.

### **Era 4 — Industrial Revolution 3.0 (1969 – 2011)**

- **Trigger Events:**
  - **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** invented by Dick Morley (1969)
  - **Toyota Production System (TPS)** formalized (1950s–1970s) — Taiichi Ohno
  - **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa
  - **Six Sigma** — Motorola, Bill Smith (1986)
  - **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)**, Robotics (Unimate, 1961), **MRP/ERP systems**
- **Characteristics:**
  - Electronics, IT, and automation in manufacturing
  - Lean thinking: Just-in-Time (JIT), Kanban, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke
  - Statistical Process Control (SPC)
  - Quality circles, continuous improvement culture
- **Legacy:** Combined flexibility with efficiency → **precursor to WCM**.

### **Era 5 — Industrial Revolution 4.0 (2011 – Present)**

- **Origin:** Coined at the **Hannover Fair, Germany (2011)** by the German government.
- **Trigger Technologies:**
  - **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**
  - **Internet of Things (IoT)**
  - **Artificial Intelligence (AI) & Machine Learning**
  - **Big Data & Cloud Computing**
  - **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)**
  - **Digital Twins, Augmented Reality (AR), Cobots**
- **Characteristics:**
  - Smart factories with self-optimizing processes
  - Real-time data from shop floor to top floor
  - Mass customization at mass-production cost
- **Legacy:** Provides the **digital backbone** for WCM implementation.

### **Era 6 — Industrial Revolution 5.0 / WCM (Emerging)**

- **Concept:** Human-centric, sustainable, resilient manufacturing.
- **Pillars:**
  - Human-Machine Collaboration (Cobots + Human creativity)
  - Circular Economy & Sustainability
  - Mass personalization
  - Bio-inspired manufacturing
- **WCM Defined:** As per **Richard J. Schonberger (1986)** — *"World-class manufacturing is the art and science of making things by bringing together the best of all methods known to man, eliminating waste, and respecting humanity."*

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\mid` instead of `|` inside any table cell to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

| Era | Time Span | Core Innovation | Key Pioneer(s) | Contribution to WCM |
|-----|-----------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Pre-1776 | Craftsmanship | Artisans | Human skill & pride |
| 2 | 1776–1870 | Steam + Interchangeable Parts | James Watt, Eli Whitney | Standardization |
| 3 | 1870–1969 | Assembly Line + Electrical Power | Henry Ford, F.W. Taylor | Mass production, scientific management |
| 4 | 1969–2011 | Automation + Lean + Quality | Taiichi Ohno, Deming, Juran, Bill Smith | TPS, TQM, Six Sigma |
| 5 | 2011–Present | Cyber-Physical Systems | German Govt. (Kagermann et al.) | Industry 4.0 |
| 6 | Present+ | Human-Centric + Sustainable | EU Commission, Schonberger | Industry 5.0, WCM |

### Key Conceptual Formulas

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Process Sigma Level} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(\text{DPMO})}
$$

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}
$$

$$
\text{Lead Time} = \text{Process Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Move Time} + \text{Wait Time}
$$

$$
\text{Cycle Time Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The historic journey is not merely academic — every modern manufacturing decision in industries like **automotive (Tesla, Toyota), aerospace (Boeing), electronics (Foxconn), and pharmaceuticals (Cipla)** is rooted in this evolution. For example:

- A Tesla Gigafactory uses **craft-level customization** (each car configured by buyer), **Ford-level scale** (millions of EVs), **Toyota-level lean discipline**, and **Industry 4.0 sensors/AI** — a true **WCM synthesis**.
- A hospital pharmacy applying WCM uses **JIT drug replenishment + Six Sigma dosing accuracy + IoT-tracked inventory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Why study the historic journey?" — answer: *"Because WCM is built upon centuries of accumulated wisdom; ignoring history leads to repeated mistakes."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Historical Logic Chain

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Ford's Production Time Reduction

A canonical KTU-style problem is to demonstrate how Ford's assembly line reduced Model T production time. Let us derive it step-by-step using the **Work-Element Reduction Principle**.

### **Given Data (Historical Record):**

- Initial assembly time per Model T (1908, stationary assembly): $T_{\text{initial}} = 12.5 \text{ hours} = 750 \text{ minutes}$
- Final assembly time per Model T (1925, moving assembly line): $T_{\text{final}} = 93 \text{ minutes}$
- Production volume target: $V_{\text{target}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ units/year}$
- Working days per year: $D = 300 \text{ days}$, single shift of $S = 8 \text{ hours/day}$

### **Step 1: Compute the Time Reduction Factor (TRF)**

$$
\text{TRF} = \frac{T_{\text{initial}}}{T_{\text{final}}} = \frac{750 \text{ min}}{93 \text{ min}}
$$

$$
\text{TRF} \approx 8.06
$$

**Interpretation:** The assembly line was approximately **8 times faster** than the stationary method.

---

### **Step 2: Verify Daily Production Capacity at Final Takt**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{S}{V_{\text{daily}}} = \frac{8 \text{ hours}}{V_{\text{daily}}}
$$

Setting takt equal to cycle time ($T_{\text{final}} = 93 \text{ min} = 1.55 \text{ hr}$):

$$
V_{\text{daily}} = \frac{S}{T_{\text{final}}} = \frac{8 \text{ hr/day}}{1.55 \text{ hr/unit}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{daily}} \approx 5.16 \text{ units/day}
$$

Annual capacity:

$$
V_{\text{annual}} = V_{\text{daily}} \times D = 5.16 \times 300
$$

$$
V_{\text{annual}} \approx 1548 \text{ units/year (single shift)}
$$

---

### **Step 3: Compute Required Number of Shifts to Hit 1 Million**

$$
N_{\text{shifts}} = \frac{V_{\text{target}}}{V_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1548}
$$

$$
N_{\text{shifts}} \approx 646 \text{ shifts}
$$

This was historically achieved via **multiple shifts, multiple plants, and further process improvements** — illustrating the **scale principle of Fordism**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: OEE Improvement from Six Sigma Implementation

### **Given:**

- Pre-Six Sigma: Availability $A_1 = 0.80$, Performance $P_1 = 0.85$, Quality $Q_1 = 0.95$
- Post-Six Sigma: Availability $A_2 = 0.90$, Performance $P_2 = 0.92$, Quality $Q_2 = 0.999$

### **Step 1: Compute Initial OEE**

$$
\text{OEE}_1 = A_1 \times P_1 \times Q_1 = 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_1 = 0.646 \text{ or } 64.6\%
$$

### **Step 2: Compute Final OEE**

$$
\text{OEE}_2 = A_2 \times P_2 \times Q_2 = 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.999
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_2 = 0.8270 \text{ or } 82.70\%
$$

### **Step 3: Compute OEE Improvement**

$$
\Delta \text{OEE} = \text{OEE}_2 - \text{OEE}_1 = 82.70\% - 64.60\%
$$

$$
\Delta \text{OEE} = 18.10 \text{ percentage points}
$$

### **Step 4: Translate to Production Capacity Gain**

Assuming planned production time $P_{\text{plan}} = 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 365 = 8760 \text{ hours/year}$:

$$
G_{\text{good}} = P_{\text{plan}} \times \Delta \text{OEE}
$$

$$
G_{\text{good}} = 8760 \times 0.181 = 1585.56 \text{ additional good-output hours/year}
$$

For a machine producing $U = 50 \text{ units/hour}$:

$$
\Delta \text{Units} = G_{\text{good}} \times U = 1585.56 \times 50
$$

$$
\Delta \text{Units} \approx 79{,}278 \text{ additional saleable units/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a 1% OEE improvement in a mid-sized plant translates to **lakhs of rupees** in additional revenue — proving why the historic journey's quality and lean lessons remain commercially vital.

---

## 3.3 Logical Derivation: WCM Pillars from Historical Contributors

Each WCM pillar can be traced to a specific historical contributor:

$$
\text{WCM Pillar} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Contribution}_i \times \text{Modern Adaptation}_i
$$

Where the contributors map as follows:

$$
\text{WCM} = f(\text{Craftsmanship}) + f(\text{Ford's Scale}) + f(\text{Ohno's Lean}) + f(\text{Deming's Quality}) + f(\text{4.0 Digitalization})
$$

$$
\text{WCM} \approx \text{Human Skill} + \text{Scientific Management} + \text{JIT} + \text{SPC/TQM/Six Sigma} + \text{IoT/AI/Digital Twins}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The derivation above is not a "formula" — it is a **conceptual equation** showing that WCM is a *sum of historical lessons*, not a replacement of them.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*"Define World Class Manufacturing. Explain how the historic journey of manufacturing evolution led to the emergence of WCM."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

World Class Manufacturing (WCM), as coined by **Richard J. Schonberger (1986)**, is a holistic manufacturing philosophy that integrates the best global practices to achieve **superior quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and safety** simultaneously.

The historic journey that led to WCM can be summarized as follows:

$$
\text{WCM} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ \text{Craft} + \text{Fordism} + \text{TPS} + \text{TQM} + \text{6σ} + \text{Industry 4.0} \right]
$$

Each era contributed:
- **Craft Production** → human skill & pride
- **Industrial Revolution 2.0** → scale & standardization
- **TPS / Lean** → flow & waste elimination
- **TQM / Six Sigma** → data-driven quality
- **Industry 4.0** → digital intelligence

WCM is thus the **convergence point** of two centuries of manufacturing wisdom.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for journey listing, 1 mark for synthesis conclusion.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*"Explain the contributions of Henry Ford and Taiichi Ohno to the evolution of manufacturing systems."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Pioneer | Era | Key Contribution | Impact on WCM |
|---------|-----|-----------------|---------------|
| **Henry Ford** (1863–1947) | Mass Production (1913) | Moving assembly line, Model T, \$500 car | Mass production, low cost, scientific management → enables WCM's **Cost Deployment** pillar |
| **Taiichi Ohno** (1912–1990) | Lean (1950s–70s) | Toyota Production System: JIT, Kanban, Jidoka, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke | Waste elimination, flow, continuous improvement → enables WCM's **Cost**, **Quality**, and **Maintenance** pillars |

Ford taught the world **how to scale**; Ohno taught the world **how to be lean**. WCM synthesizes both.

*(Award: 1 mark each pioneer, 1 mark synthesis.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Trace the historic journey of manufacturing from Craft Production to Industry 4.0, highlighting the key innovations and pioneers of each era. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the Toyota Production System (TPS) in detail. Discuss its **two pillars** and **fourteen principles**, and illustrate how TPS laid the foundation for World Class Manufacturing. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The historic journey of manufacturing can be divided into **six eras**:

**1. Craft Production (Pre-1776):**
- Skilled artisans hand-made products; high customization, low volume.
- Quality depended on individual skill.

**2. Industrial Revolution 1.0 (1776–1870):**
- **James Watt's** steam engine (1776) → factory mechanization.
- **Eli Whitney** (1799) → interchangeable parts for muskets.
- Foundation: Standardization.

**3. Industrial Revolution 2.0 (1870–1969):**
- **Henry Ford's** moving assembly line (December 1, 1913).
- Model T production time: $T_i = 12.5 \text{ hr} \rightarrow T_f = 93 \text{ min}$ (8× faster).
- **F.W. Taylor** — Scientific Management (time-and-motion studies, 1911).
- Foundation: Scale and efficiency.

**4. Industrial Revolution 3.0 (1969–2011):**
- **Dick Morley's PLC (1969)** → programmable automation.
- **Taiichi Ohno's TPS** → JIT, Kanban, Jidoka, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, Heijunka.
- **Deming's 14 Points**, **Juran's Quality Trilogy**, **Ishikawa's Quality Circles** → TQM.
- **Bill Smith, Motorola (1986)** → Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO).
- **Taguchi's** Robust Design, **Shingo's SMED**.
- Foundation: Lean, Quality, Automation.

**5. Industrial Revolution 4.0 (2011–Present):**
- Term coined at **Hannover Fair, Germany (2011)**.
- Technologies: **IoT, AI/ML, Big Data, CPS, Digital Twins, 3D Printing, AR/VR, Cobots**.
- Foundation: Intelligent, connected manufacturing.

**6. Industry 5.0 / WCM (Emerging):**
- Human-centric, sustainable, resilient manufacturing.
- **Schonberger's WCM (1986)** — holistic model with **10 Technical + 10 Managerial Pillars**.
- Foundation: Synthesis of all previous eras.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** was developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota Motor Corporation between 1948 and 1975.

**The Two Pillars of TPS:**

1. **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Every part is produced only when needed, in the required quantity, at the right time.
   - Tools: **Kanban (signal cards), Takt-time planning, Continuous flow, Pull system**.

2. **Jidoka (Autonomation):** "Automation with a human touch" — the machine stops automatically when a defect is detected.
   - Tools: **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Andon (visual signal), Quality at the source**.

**Fourteen Principles of TPS (based on Jeffrey Liker's "The Toyota Way"):**

1. Base decisions on long-term philosophy, even at short-term cost.
2. Create continuous process flow.
3. Use pull systems to avoid overproduction.
4. Level the workload (Heijunka).
5. Build a culture of stopping to fix problems (Jidoka).
6. Standardize tasks for continuous improvement.
7. Use visual control.
8. Use only reliable, tested technology.
9. Grow leaders who live the philosophy.
10. Develop exceptional people and teams.
11. Respect your extended network of partners.
12. Go and see for yourself (Genchi Genbutsu).
13. Make decisions slowly by consensus; implement rapidly.
14. Become a learning organization through relentless reflection (Hansei) and Kaizen.

**TPS → WCM Foundation:**

TPS provided the **operational engine** for WCM:
- JIT → enables **Cost Deployment** pillar.
- Jidoka & Poka-Yoke → enable **Quality** pillar.
- Kaizen & 5S → enable **Workplace Organization (5S)** managerial pillar.
- Respect for people → enables **People Development** pillar.
- Heijunka → enables **Delivery** reliability.

In essence, **TPS is the philosophical mother of WCM**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Define World Class Manufacturing (WCM). Discuss the **ten technical pillars** and **ten managerial pillars** of WCM in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the contributions of the following quality gurus to the historic journey of manufacturing: **W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, Philip Crosby, and Genichi Taguchi**. How did their combined philosophies shape WCM? *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (KTU Standard):**

World Class Manufacturing is an integrated manufacturing philosophy that combines **the best practices from all manufacturing eras** to achieve excellence across **safety, quality, cost, delivery, and people**, while maintaining **sustainability and continuous improvement**.

*(1 mark)*

**Ten Technical Pillars (7 marks — 0.7 per pillar):**

1. **Safety:** Zero-accident culture; safety as the foundation.
2. **Cost Deployment:** Systematic reduction of losses → cost of poor quality.
3. **Quality:** Zero defects via Poka-Yoke, SPC, Six Sigma.
4. **Maintenance:** Autonomous & preventive maintenance (TPM — Total Productive Maintenance).
5. **Logistics:** Material flow optimization; JIT from suppliers.
6. **Industrial Engineering:** Time studies, line balancing, motion economy.
7. **Production:** Synchronized takt-based production.
8. **Procurement:** Strategic supplier partnerships; total cost of ownership.
9. **Environment:** Zero pollution, eco-efficiency, ESG compliance.
10. **Product Development:** Concurrent engineering, DFM/DFA.

**Ten Managerial Pillars:**

1. Management Competency & Commitment
2. People Development (Skill Matrix, Cross-Training)
3. **5S Workplace Organization** (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain)
4. Time Management (Reduction of non-value-added activities)
5. Supply Chain Integration
6. Customer Satisfaction (VOC → CTQ)
7. Communication (Visual Management, Obeya rooms)
8. Information Systems (MES, ERP, IoT)
9. Sustainability & Social Responsibility
10. Model Area Implementation (Pilot → Scale-up)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Technical Pillars:** **"S-C-Q-M-L-I-P-P-E-P"** → *"She Could Quite Quickly Make Lovely Indian Pulao, Please Eat Properly."*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Quality Guru | Era | Key Contribution | Impact on WCM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** (1900–1993) | 1950s | 14 Points for Management; PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) Cycle; Statistical Process Control (SPC) | Foundation of TQM → WCM Quality Pillar |
| **Joseph Juran** (1904–2008) | 1950s | Juran Trilogy: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement | Structured approach to WCM Continuous Improvement |
| **Philip Crosby** (1926–2001) | 1979 | "Quality is Free"; **Zero Defects** concept; **4 Absolutes of Quality** | Reinforced WCM's "zero defect" target |
| **Genichi Taguchi** (1924–2012) | 1980s | **Taguchi Loss Function** — any deviation from target causes customer loss; **Robust Design** | Connected quality to cost; enabled WCM Cost Deployment |

**Taguchi Loss Function (KTU Formula):**

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = loss to society from a defective product
- $k$ = constant (quality cost coefficient)
- $x$ = actual quality characteristic value
- $T$ = target value

**Synthesis — How They Shaped WCM:**

These four gurus collectively transformed quality from an *inspection function* into a *strategic management discipline*. WCM's **Quality**, **Cost Deployment**, and **Continuous Improvement** pillars are direct descendants of their combined philosophies:

$$
\text{WCM Quality Pillar} = f(\text{Deming SPC}) + f(\text{Juran Trilogy}) + f(\text{Crosby Zero Defects}) + f(\text{Taguchi Loss Function})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always **name the guru, year, concept, and WCM impact** — this is the valuation pattern for 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Mark Loss Zones):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Industry 4.0 with Industry 5.0** — Industry 4.0 is **digital/CPS-driven**; Industry 5.0 is **human-centric + sustainable**. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Calling Fordism "lean"** — Ford = mass production (push); Ohno = lean (pull). Examiners deduct marks for conflating these.
> 3. **Forgetting the year of WCM coinage** — Always mention **Richard Schonberger, 1986**, and the **Hannover Fair, 2011** for Industry 4.0.
> 4. **Skipping the pioneers** — A historic journey answer without naming **Watt, Whitney, Ford, Taylor, Ohno, Deming, Juran, Ishikawa, Smith, Schonberger** is incomplete (loses up to 3 marks).
> 5. **Not drawing/mentioning the era linkage** — Examiners expect you to show **how each era connects to the next**, not just list them in isolation.
> 6. **Ignoring Indian context** — Optional but award-winning: mention **Toyota India, Tata WCM initiatives, Maruti Suzuki's WCM award (2014 Deming Prize)**.
> 7. **Calculating OEE/Taguchi Loss incorrectly** — Always show **units** and **verify** that $\text{OEE} \le 100\%$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ WCM is a **synthesis**, not a reinvention — it integrates craft skill, mass-production scale, lean flow, quality discipline, and digital intelligence.
- ✅ **Six eras:** Craft → IR 1.0 → IR 2.0 → IR 3.0 → IR 4.0 → IR 5.0/WCM.
- ✅ **IR 1.0 (1776–1870):** Steam + Interchangeable Parts → Watt, Whitney.
- ✅ **IR 2.0 (1870–1969):** Mass Production → Ford (1913), Taylor (1911).
- ✅ **IR 3.0 (1969–2011):** Lean + Quality + Automation → Ohno (TPS), Deming (14 Points), Smith (Six Sigma), Morley (PLC).
- ✅ **IR 4.0 (2011–Present):** Cyber-Physical Systems → IoT, AI, Big Data, Digital Twins.
- ✅ **IR 5.0 / WCM:** Human-centric, sustainable, resilient → Schonberger (1986).
- ✅ **WCM Pillars:** **10 Technical + 10 Managerial = 20 total**.
- ✅ **Core WCM metrics:** OEE ≥ 85%, FPY ≥ 99%, DPMO ≤ 3.4 (Six Sigma), Inventory Turns ≥ 24, OTD ≥ 99%.
- ✅ **TPS Two Pillars:** **JIT** + **Jidoka**.
- ✅ **Taguchi Loss:** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$.
- ✅ **OEE:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$.
