| **SCM** | Safe supplier audits | Vendor maintenance | Vendor Kanban | Incoming QA | Supplier kaizen | — | Lower PO cost |
| **Cost** | ROI on safety | ROI on maintenance | ROI on inventory | ROI on prevention | ROI on engagement | ROI on integration | — |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Tip:** If asked "How do the pillars interact?", always explain that **SHE is the foundation**, **TPM/JIT/TQM are the operational core**, and **Kaizen is the engine** that drives all other pillars forward.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define World Class Manufacturing (WCM). List any SIX of its pillars.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** WCM is a strategic management philosophy that integrates all organizational processes to achieve **world-class standards** in quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and safety — meeting or exceeding global best practices in every operational dimension.

**Six Pillars (any six accepted):**
1. Safety, Health & Environment (SHE)
2. Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
3. Just-in-Time (JIT) / Lean Production
4. Total Quality Management (TQM)
5. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
6. Customer Focus
7. Supply Chain Management (SCM)
8. Workforce Management
9. Cost Reduction
10. Lead Time Reduction

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any 6 pillars.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **10 pillars of World Class Manufacturing** with their objectives and key tools. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC turning center has the following operating data for one shift:
- Total shift time: **480 minutes**
- Planned breaks: **40 minutes**
- Planned maintenance: **30 minutes**
- Unplanned downtime (breakdowns): **35 minutes**
- Ideal cycle time: **2.0 min/piece**
- Total pieces produced: **180 pieces**
- Defective pieces: **12 pieces**

**Calculate:**
(i) Availability, Performance, and Quality rates.
(ii) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).
(iii) Comment on the result against the world-class benchmark and suggest **two corrective actions**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a)**

The 10 pillars of WCM (also see Section 2.1 of these notes for full details) are summarized below in tabular form for quick recall:

| # | Pillar | Primary Objective | Key Tool(s) |
|---|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | SHE | Zero accidents, zero pollution | HIRA, PPE, ISO 14001 |
| 2 | TPM | Maximize OEE | OEE tracking, Autonomous Maintenance |
| 3 | JIT / Lean | Eliminate waste, pull flow | Kanban, 7 Wastes, Takt Time |
| 4 | TQM | Zero defects, customer satisfaction | 7 QC tools, DMAIC, Six Sigma |
| 5 | Kaizen | Continuous incremental improvement | PDCA, 5S, Suggestion schemes |
| 6 | Customer Focus | VOC to VOP | QFD, CTQ tree, NPS |
| 7 | SCM | End-to-end integration | SCOR, VMI, CPFR |
| 8 | Workforce | Multi-skilled, engaged employees | Skill matrix, Quality circles |
| 9 | Cost Reduction | Eliminate non-value-added cost | ABC, Target costing, COPQ |
| 10 | Lead Time Reduction | Compress OTD | VSM, SMED, Cellular layout |

The pillars are **interdependent**: SHE is the moral foundation, TPM/JIT/TQM are operational, and Kaizen is the engine that improves all pillars continuously. A truly world-class manufacturer is one that has achieved maturity in all 10 pillars simultaneously — partial adoption (e.g., JIT without TPM) leads to unstable flow. *(7 marks: 1 mark for systematic listing + 1 mark for objective + 1 mark for key tool per pillar set; bonus for the interdependence discussion.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b)**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{shift} = 480$ min
- $T_{breaks} = 40$ min
- $T_{planned\_maint} = 30$ min
- $T_{downtime} = 35$ min
- $T_{cycle\_ideal} = 2.0$ min/piece
- $N_{total} = 180$ pieces
- $N_{defective} = 12$ pieces

**Step (i)(1): Compute Load Time.**

$$
T_{load} = T_{shift} - T_{breaks} - T_{planned\_maint}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{load} = 480 - 40 - 30 = 410 \text{ minutes}
$$

*Logic:* The CNC machine was *expected* to run for 410 minutes, excluding breaks and scheduled maintenance.

**Step (i)(2): Compute Operating Time.**

$$
T_{operating} = T_{load} - T_{downtime} = 410 - 35 = 375 \text{ minutes}
$$

*Logic:* The machine actually ran (did useful work) for 375 minutes.

**Step (i)(3): Compute Availability (A).**

$$
A = \frac{T_{load} - T_{downtime}}{T_{load}} = \frac{410 - 35}{410} = \frac{375}{410} = 0.9146
$$

Converting:

$$
A \approx 91.46\%
$$

**Step (i)(4): Compute Performance (P).**

$$
P = \frac{T_{cycle\_ideal} \times N_{total}}{T_{operating}} = \frac{2.0 \times 180}{375} = \frac{360}{375} = 0.9600
$$

Converting:

$$
P = 96.00\%
$$

**Step (i)(5): Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
N_{good} = N_{total} - N_{defective} = 180 - 12 = 168 \text{ pieces}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \frac{168}{180} = 0.9333 = 93.33\%
$$

**Step (ii): Compute OEE.**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9146 \times 0.9600 \times 0.9333
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.8196 \approx 81.96\%
$$

**Step (iii): Comment and Recommend.**

| Metric | Computed | World-Class | Status |
|--------|----------|-------------|--------|
| A | 91.46% | $\geq 90\%$ | OK |
| P | 96.00% | $\geq 95\%$ | OK |
| Q | 93.33% | $\geq 99\%$ | **Below benchmark** |
| **OEE** | **81.96%** | $\geq 85\%$ | **Below benchmark** |

**Conclusion:** The OEE of 81.96% is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%**, primarily due to the **Quality factor (93.33%)** being the weakest. The biggest improvement opportunity lies in reducing defects.

**Two Corrective Actions:**
1. **Quality Maintenance (within TPM)** — Apply Poka-Yoke (error-proofing) on the loading fixture to eliminate the defect root cause. Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts to detect drift in tool wear and trigger dressing before defects occur.
2. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** — Launch a 5-day Kaizen event to study the 12 defects (Pareto analysis) and implement Why-Why analysis on the top defect mode. Train the operator in autonomous quality inspection. *(7 marks: 1 mark each for steps i.1 to i.3, 1 mark for OEE, 1 mark for comparison table, 2 marks for diagnosis + actions.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **TPM pillar of WCM** in detail. Define OEE and describe its **six big losses**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company operates a single-shift assembly line:
- Shift time: **8 hours (480 min)**
- Breaks: **45 min**
- Customer demand: **400 units per shift**
- Currently, the line produces **450 units in a shift**
- Defect rate: **4%**
- The line is **balanced** (cycle time uniform across all stations)

**Calculate:**
(i) Takt time
(ii) Current cycle time
(iii) Whether the current output meets demand
(iv) Current first-pass yield (FPY)
(v) If the company wants to reduce setup time by **60%** using SMED, what is the new effective cycle time and does it improve on-time delivery? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a)**

**Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a pillar of WCM aimed at achieving **zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents** through proactive and preventive maintenance involving **all employees** — not just the maintenance department.

**Origin:** Formalized by Seiichi Nakajima at Nippondenso (Toyota) in 1971.

**Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

**The Six Big Losses** (grouped under the three OEE factors):

| Factor | Loss # | Loss Name | Description | Example |
|--------|--------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Availability** | 1 | Equipment Failure | Breakdown losses | Motor burnout |
| | 2 | Setup & Adjustment | Changeover losses | Die change time |
| **Performance** | 3 | Minor Stops | Idling, sensor trips | Jam at feeder |
| | 4 | Reduced Speed | Running below design speed | Operator throttling |
| **Quality** | 5 | Defects | Scrap and rework | Out-of-spec part |
| | 6 | Startup/Yield Losses | Rejects during warm-up | First 10 parts scrapped |

**TPM has 8 sub-pillars** (Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Focused Improvement, Early Equipment Management, Training, SHE, Office TPM).

**World-class OEE benchmark:** $\geq 85\%$ (i.e., A $\geq 90\%$, P $\geq 95\%$, Q $\geq 99\%$). *(7 marks: 2 marks for TPM definition + objective, 1 mark for OEE formula, 3 marks for six big losses table, 1 mark for benchmark.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b)**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{shift} = 480$ min
- $T_{breaks} = 45$ min
- $D = 400$ units/shift
- $N_{produced} = 450$ units/shift
- Defect rate: $4\%$

**Step (i): Compute Takt Time.**

$$
T_{avail} = T_{shift} - T_{breaks} = 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{avail}}{D} = \frac{435}{400} = 1.0875 \text{ min/unit}
$$

$$
T_{takt} \approx 1.09 \text{ min/unit}
$$

*Logic:* To meet demand of 400 units in 435 min, one unit must roll off the line every 1.09 min.

**Step (ii): Compute Current Cycle Time.**

Since the line produced 450 units in 435 available minutes:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{T_{avail}}{N_{produced}} = \frac{435}{450} = 0.9667 \text{ min/unit}
$$

$$
T_{cycle} \approx 0.97 \text{ min/unit}
$$

*Logic:* On average, the line produces one unit every 0.97 min — faster than the takt time, but this includes defective units.

**Step (iii): Does Output Meet Demand?**

**Good units produced** = $450 \times (1 - 0.04) = 450 \times 0.96 = 432$ units.

Since $432 > 400$, the **good output meets demand**, with a buffer of 32 units. ✅

**However**, the line is **over-producing** (450 > 400), violating the JIT principle of "produce only what is needed." The excess 18 good units (432 - 400 = 32) and all 18 defective units (4% of 450) represent **overproduction waste** in JIT terms.

**Step (iv): Compute First-Pass Yield (FPY).**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Good units first time}}{\text{Total units started}} = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = 96.00\%
$$

*Logic:* 96% of units pass the first inspection; the remaining 4% require rework or are scrapped — a clear Kaizen target.

**Step (v): SMED Impact.**

Assume the original setup time was $T_{setup,old}$. After SMED, it is reduced by 60%:

$$
T_{setup,new} = 0.40 \times T_{setup,old}
$$

If we assume a typical $T_{setup,old} = 30$ min (one changeover per shift), then:

$$
T_{setup,new} = 0.40 \times 30 = 12 \text{ min}
$$

**New effective cycle time** (using the recovered time for production):

$$
T_{cycle,new} = \frac{T_{avail} + (T_{setup,old} - T_{setup,new})}{N_{produced}} = \frac{435 + 18}{450} = \frac{453}{450} = 1.0067 \text{ min/unit}
$$

$$
T_{cycle,new} \approx 1.007 \text{ min/unit}
$$

*Comparison:* $T_{cycle,new} = 1.007$ min, which is **less than takt time of 1.0875 min**. The line is now **better synchronized with customer demand** — JIT principle satisfied.

**On-time delivery improvement:** Yes, the line can now produce $435 / 1.007 \approx 432$ units in the same available time, with FPY at 96% giving $432 \times 0.96 = 414.7$ good units — even more buffer for OTD. *(7 marks: 1 mark each for (i), (ii), (iii), (iv); 3 marks for SMED analysis in (v).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **OEE Component Confusion:** Many students confuse "Performance" with "Production Rate." Performance is **not** how much you produced — it is the ratio of **ideal production to actual production** in the **available operating time** (excluding downtime). Use $P = (T_{cycle,ideal} \times N) / T_{operating}$, **not** $P = N / D$.
>
> 2. **Takt Time vs. Cycle Time:** Takt is a **demand-derived target**; cycle time is the **process capability**. The line is **balanced** when $T_{cycle} \leq T_{takt}$, not the reverse.
>
> 3. **Pillar Count Drift:** Some textbooks cite 5, 7, 9, 10, or even 15 pillars. In KTU 2024, the **10-pillar model** is the safe default — but always state your source assumption.
>
> 4. **Confusing TPM with TQM:** TPM is **equipment-focused**; TQM is **product/process/customer-focused**. TQM does not cover OEE; TPM does.
>
> 5. **Kaizen vs. Kaikaku:** Kaizen = small, continuous, low-cost, employee-driven. Kaikaku = radical, technology-driven, management-driven. Mixing them up costs marks.
>
> 6. **Shewhart vs. Deming PDCA:** The PDCA cycle is also called the **Deming Wheel** (or Shewhart Cycle). Always use the term "Deming Cycle" in KTU answers for full credit.
>
> 7. **OEE Units:** OEE is always a **percentage** (or decimal). Never report OEE in absolute units like "pieces per shift."
>
> 8. **Setup-Time Reduction Calculation:** When asked to "find the new cycle time after SMED," the recovered time should be **added to the productive time available**, not multiplied with the existing cycle time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-Exam Quick Recall — Keep This List Visible**

- **WCM** = world-class standards in Quality + Cost + Delivery + Flexibility + Safety.
- **10 Pillars** = SHE, TPM, JIT/Lean, TQM, Kaizen, Customer Focus, SCM, Workforce, Cost Reduction, Lead Time Reduction.
- **SHE is Pillar #1** — moral/legal foundation; **Kaizen is the engine** that improves all others.
- **OEE = A × P × Q** — each component should be $\geq 90\%$ for world-class; OEE target $\geq 85\%$.
- **Six Big Losses** of OEE: Equipment Failure, Setup/Adjustment, Minor Stops, Reduced Speed, Defects, Startup/Yield.
- **5S = Seiri + Seiton + Seiso + Seiketsu + Shitsuke** — Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **7 Wastes (Muda):** Overproduction, Waiting, Transport, Over-processing, Inventory, Motion, Defects (+ 8th: Unused Talent).
- **Takt Time** = Available Time / Customer Demand — sets the line's heartbeat.
- **JIT = Pull system** (Kanban); **Push system** is the **traditional MRP** approach (JIT's opposite).
- **TQM Tools:** 7 QC tools + DMAIC + 5 Why + Fishbone (Ishikawa).
- **PDCA** = Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Wheel) — the universal improvement cycle.
- **Six Sigma Target:** $\leq 3.4$ DPMO = $6\sigma$ quality level.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** = capable process; $\geq 1.67$ = highly capable.
- **Little's Law:** WIP = Throughput Time × Throughput Rate.
- **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- **Target Cost** = Market Price − Required Profit Margin.
- **SMED** = Single Minute Exchange of Die — set-up $\leq 10$ min target.
- **VSM** = Value Stream Mapping (current vs. future state).
- **TPM = 8 sub-pillars** — remember Autonomous Maintenance is the foundation of TPM.
- **Pillars are interdependent** — never treat them as silos; always show cause-and-effect.
- **WCM House analogy:** Foundation (SHE + Workforce) + Pillars (10 disciplines) + Beams (PDCA, 5S, Training) + Roof (Customer Satisfaction).
- **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** = product of individual step yields; FPY = first-pass yield at one step.
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle** = DIO + DSO − DPO — measure of SCM efficiency.
- **POI (Perfect Order Index)** = product of on-time, in-full, damage-free, accurate-doc — single SCM KPI.
- **PDCA vs. DMAIC** — PDCA is the *continuous daily* cycle; DMAIC is the *project-based* Six Sigma cycle. Both co-exist in WCM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Word Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> *"WCM is built on ten pillars, rests on SHE, runs on Kaizen, and roofs customer satisfaction. Master the OEE formula, the 5S list, the 7 Wastes, and the PDCA cycle — and you master Module 1."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Techniques of World Class Manufactures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Control Techniques of World Class Manufactures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Control Techniques in World Class Manufacturing (WCM) **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is an integrated, holistic, and continuously evolving manufacturing management philosophy that aims to achieve the highest levels of manufacturing excellence by eliminating all forms of waste, defects, and inefficiencies across the entire value chain. Control techniques in WCM refer to the systematic, statistically-validated, and behaviourally-driven methodologies used to **monitor, regulate, stabilize, and continuously improve** manufacturing processes so that output consistently meets or exceeds customer expectations at minimum cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** Control techniques are the operational pillars of WCM that ensure *process stability*, *capability*, and *continuous improvement*. They bridge the gap between strategic intent (Lean, Six Sigma, TQM) and shop-floor execution. In formal KTU terminology, the control techniques of WCM include: 1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and Control Charts. 2. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** tools. 3. **Six Sigma** methodology (DMAIC). 4. **Kaizen** and continuous improvement practices. 5. **5S** workplace organization. 6. **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing). 7. **Jidoka / Autonomation**. 8. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and Kanban. 9. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**. 10. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** and the PDCA cycle. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Ship Autopilot:** Imagine you are sailing a cruise ship from Cochin to the Maldives. The autopilot (control technique) continuously checks the ship's heading against the planned route. The moment the ship drifts (a defect), the autopilot corrects it (corrective action) using small, constant nudges — not by turning the ship 180° but by micro-adjustments. Similarly, **WCM control techniques are the autopilot of a factory** — they detect micro-deviations in processes and correct them *before* the customer ever notices. Another powerful analogy is the **human body's homeostatic system**. Your body maintains a constant temperature of **98.6°F (37°C)**. When temperature rises, you sweat (corrective action). When it falls, you shiver (another corrective action). Likewise, control charts maintain processes within **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — the "physiological" range of a healthy process. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standardized metrics are central to evaluating WCM control techniques: - **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)**: A dimensionless number measuring how well a process fits within specification limits. Target value: **≥ 1.33** (industry benchmark), **≥ 1.67** for safety-critical parts. - **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)**: Accounts for centering. Target value: **≥ 1.33**, ideally **≥ 1.50**. - **Six Sigma Level: 3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities). **σ = 6σ**. - **Control Chart Constants (A₂, D₃, D₄, d₂)**: Standardized constants derived from the **standard normal distribution** for sample sizes $n = 2$ to $n = 25$. - **Takt Time**: Customer demand rate, calculated as $\text{Available Production Time} \div \text{Customer Demand}$. --- ## 1.4 Hierarchy of WCM Control Techniques ``` WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING (WCM) │ ├── STRATEGIC CONTROLS (Top Floor) │ ├── TQM (Total Quality Management) │ ├── Six Sigma (DMAIC) │ └── Benchmarking │ ├── TACTICAL CONTROLS (Middle Floor) │ ├── SPC & Control Charts │ ├── FMEA │ ├── TPM │ └── PDCA Cycle │ └── OPERATIONAL CONTROLS (Shop Floor) ├── 5S, Kaizen ├── Poka-Yoke ├── Jidoka / Autonomation └── Kanban / JIT ``` > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to Remember the Techniques — "SPC-TQF-JKPA":** > **S**PC, **P**oka-Yoke, **C**ontrol Charts → **T**QM, **Q**uality Circles → **F**MEA, **J**idoka, **J**IT, **K**anban → **P**DCA, **A**utonomation, **5S** & **K**aizen. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Statistical Process Control (SPC) — The Foundation of WCM **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is a data-driven methodology that uses statistical tools (primarily control charts) to monitor and control a process. It was pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** at Bell Laboratories and later popularized by **W. Edwards Deming** in post-war Japan. ### Operational Logic of SPC: 1. **Measure** the process output at regular intervals using rational subgroups. 2. **Plot** the data points on a time-ordered control chart with a **Center Line (CL = process mean)**, **Upper Control Limit (UCL)**, and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**. 3. **Analyze** patterns using Western Electric Rules to detect *special cause variation* vs. *common cause variation*. 4. **Intervene** only when special causes are detected (according to Deming's philosophy). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deming's Insight (Key KTU High-Yield Concept):** *Common cause variation* (~86% of process variability) is inherent to the system and requires management action to fix the *system*. *Special cause variation* (~14%) is assignable and requires shop-floor intervention. ### 2.1.1 The Two Fundamental Distributions: - **Within-subgroup variation ($s$ or $\bar{R}$):** Short-term, immediate capability. - **Between-subgroup variation ($\bar{X}$):** Long-term, drift over time. ## 2.2 Control Charts — The Heartbeat of SPC Control charts distinguish between two types of variation using the **3-Sigma Rule** based on the standard normal distribution: $$\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$ $$\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$ $$\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$ Where the constants $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are tabulated for each subgroup size $n$. ### Types of Control Charts (KTU High-Yield): | Variable Type | Chart Name | What it Plots | Best For | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Continuous (variable) | $\bar{X}$-chart | Subgroup means | Process centering | | Continuous (variable) | $R$-chart | Subgroup ranges | Process spread | | Continuous (variable) | $I$-chart (Individuals) | Individual readings | Batch / slow processes | | Continuous (variable) | $MR$-chart | Moving range | Detecting drift | | Discrete (attribute) | $p$-chart | Fraction defective | Variable sample size | | Discrete (attribute) | $c$-chart | Defects per unit | Constant inspection area | | Discrete (attribute) | $u$-chart | Defects per unit (variable) | Variable inspection area | > [!NOTE] > **Why "3 Sigma"?** Chebyshev's Inequality guarantees that for *any* distribution, at least 89% of data lies within $\pm 3\sigma$. For a normal distribution, **99.73%** lies within $\pm 3\sigma$, leaving only **0.27%** in the tails. This gives a false-alarm probability of just **2.7 parts per thousand**, making it optimal for practical control. ## 2.3 Process Capability Indices — Quantifying "Control" ### 2.3.1 Process Capability Index ($C_p$): $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$ Where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. $C_p$ measures **spread only**, ignoring centering. ### 2.3.2 Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$): $$C_{pk} = \min\left( C_{pu}, C_{pl} \right) = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$ $C_{pk}$ accounts for **both spread AND centering**. It is the **true** metric used in WCM audits. ### 2.3.3 Sigma Level ($Z$): $$Z = 3 \cdot C_{pk}$$ For a Six Sigma process, $C_{pk} = 2.0$ (because $1.5\sigma$ shift allowance gives effective $\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = \sigma_{\text{short-term}} + 1.5\sigma$). ## 2.4 The DMAIC Methodology (Six Sigma) **DMAIC** is the structured problem-solving framework for Six Sigma projects: | Phase | Full Name | KTU High-Yield Tools | Output | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **D** | Define | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ | Problem Statement | | **M** | Measure | Control Charts, MSA, $C_{pk}$, DPMO | Baseline $\sigma$ | | **A** | Analyze | Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Whys, Hypothesis Testing | Root Cause | | **I** | Improve | DOE, Pilot Runs, Poka-Yoke | Solution Validated | | **C** | Control | SPC, Control Plans, Standardization | Sustained Improvement | ## 2.5 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Cycle / Shewhart Cycle) A 4-stage continuous improvement loop: 1. **Plan:** Identify the problem and plan a change. 2. **Do:** Implement the change on a small scale. 3. **Check:** Analyze the results (data-driven). 4. **Act:** If successful, standardize; if not, revise and loop back. > [!TIP] > **PDCA vs. DMAIC:** PDCA is a *philosophical loop* (used daily at Toyota, e.g.). DMAIC is a *project-based statistical roadmap* with stronger statistical tooling. ## 2.6 Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing) Coined by **Shigeo Shingo** (Toyota). Poka-Yoke devices prevent errors from becoming defects. Three categories: - **Contact method:** Sensor detects defect physically (e.g., misaligned part). - **Fixed-value method:** Only a predetermined number of actions allowed (e.g., 4 bolts → machine stops after 4 tightenings). - **Sequence method:** Steps must occur in order (e.g., gear-shift interlocks in cars). ## 2.7 Just-In-Time (JIT) and Kanban - **JIT:** A pull-based production system where each operation produces only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed. - **Kanban:** The visual signaling system (Japanese for "card" or "signboard") that triggers production. - **Takt Time:** $\tau = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{demand}}}$ where $T_{\text{available}}$ is daily available time and $D_{\text{demand}}$ is daily customer demand. ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is your high-yield reference for the KTU exam. Memorize it.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Marks Weight | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{X}$-chart upper limit | 2 | | 2 | $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{X}$-chart lower limit | 2 | | 3 | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | $R$-chart upper limit | 2 | | 4 | $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | $R$-chart lower limit | 2 | | 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability (spread) | 3 | | 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\left[ \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right]$ | Process capability (centered) | 4 | | 7 | $Z = 3 C_{pk}$ | Sigma level | 1 | | 8 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$ | Defects per million opportunities | 2 | | 9 | $\tau = T_{\text{available}} / D_{\text{demand}}$ | Takt time | 1 | | 10 | $\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $p$-chart upper limit | 3 | | 11 | $\text{UCL}_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $c$-chart upper limit | 3 | | 12 | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Standard error of the mean | 2 | ### Standard Control Chart Constants (Extract for $n = 2$ to $7$): | $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | | 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | | 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | | 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | | 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | | 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | ## 2.9 Real-World Utility of These Techniques - **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors):** JIT + Kanban reduce inventory carrying costs by 20-40%. - **Electronics (Foxconn):** Poka-Yoke eliminates 90% of human assembly errors. - **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** FMEA + Six Sigma achieves safety-critical $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$. - **Pharmaceutical (Cipla, Sun Pharma):** SPC ensures zero-defect drug batches, complying with **FDA 21 CFR Part 820**. - **Software (Microsoft, Google):** Adapted DMAIC for bug reduction (Google's "Six Sigma SRE" team). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Control Chart Limits — From Shewhart's Principle ### 3.1.1 Starting Premise: If a process is *in statistical control*, its quality characteristic $X$ follows a **Normal Distribution** $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$: $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[ -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right]$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of $\bar{X}$-chart UCL: **Step 1:** Consider a subgroup of size $n$ drawn from a stable process. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is itself a random variable. **Step 2:** By the **Central Limit Theorem**, the distribution of $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal with: - Mean: $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$ - Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ **Step 3:** Apply the **3-Sigma Shewhart Control Principle** (which states that any data point lying more than $3\sigma$ from the mean is evidence of an out-of-control condition): $$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the expressions from Step 2: $$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ **Step 5:** In practice, $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown. We estimate them using $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand mean) and $\dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ (where $d_2$ is the unbiased estimator relating $\bar{R}$ to $\sigma$): $$\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute these estimators back into Step 4: $$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \left(\frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}\right) \bar{R}$$ **Step 7:** Define the **control chart constant** $A_2 \equiv \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, which gives the final form: $$\boxed{\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}}$$ By symmetry around $\bar{\bar{X}}$: $$\boxed{\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style > [!NOTE] > **Worked Problem:** A piston ring diameter has USL = 74.040 mm and LSL = 73.960 mm. Five subgroups of size $n = 5$ are measured, giving $\bar{R} = 0.020$ mm and $\bar{\bar{X}} = 74.000$ mm. Estimate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the control limits. ### Step 1: Estimate Process Standard Deviation For $n = 5$, from the constant table, $d_2 = 2.326$. $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.020}{2.326} = 0.008598 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Process Capability Index $C_p$ $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.040 - 73.960}{6 \times 0.008598}$$ $$C_p = \frac{0.080}{0.051588} = 1.551$$ ### Step 3: Calculate $C_{pk}$ First compute $C_{pu}$ (upper capability): $$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.040 - 74.000}{3 \times 0.008598} = \frac{0.040}{0.025794} = 1.551$$ Then compute $C_{pl}$ (lower capability): $$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.000 - 73.960}{3 \times 0.008598} = \frac{0.040}{0.025794} = 1.551$$ Since both are equal, the process is perfectly centered: $$C_{pk} = \min(1.551, 1.551) = 1.551$$ ### Step 4: Sigma Level $$Z = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.551 = 4.65\sigma$$ This is between 4σ and 5σ — a strong process but not yet Six Sigma. ### Step 5: Control Limits for $\bar{X}$-chart For $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$: $$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 74.000 + (0.577)(0.020) = 74.0115 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 74.000 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 74.000 - (0.577)(0.020) = 73.9885 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 6: Control Limits for $R$-chart For $n = 5$, $D_3 = 0$ and $D_4 = 2.114$: $$\text{UCL}_R = (2.114)(0.020) = 0.0423 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{CL}_R = 0.020 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{LCL}_R = (0)(0.020) = 0 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 7: Interpretation - Process capability $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.551 > 1.33$ → **Process is CAPABLE**. - All data lies within control limits → **Process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL**. - Sigma level $4.65\sigma$ → **Excellent for general manufacturing**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of $p$-chart Limits The $p$-chart monitors the *fraction defective* in variable-size samples. The fraction defective $\hat{p} = \dfrac{\text{Number of defectives}}{\text{Sample size } n}$. **Step 1:** The number of defectives $D$ follows a **Binomial Distribution** $B(n, p)$. **Step 2:** The sample proportion $\hat{p}$ has: - Mean: $\mu_{\hat{p}} = p$ - Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ **Step 3:** Apply the 3-sigma rule: $$\text{UCL}_p = p + 3\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$ **Step 4:** Estimate $p$ using $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$: $$\boxed{\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Control Technique | Problem Type | Best WCM Technique | Justification | KTU High-Yield | |:---|:---|:---|:---:| | Monitoring average of a continuous variable | $\bar{X}$-chart | CLT applies, mean is most informative | ★★★★★ | | Monitoring variability / spread | $R$-chart | Complements $\bar{X}$, detects changes in $\sigma$ | ★★★★★ | | Variable batch size, attribute data | $p$-chart | Binomial distribution fits | ★★★★ | | Constant inspection area, defect counts | $c$-chart | Poisson distribution fits | ★★★ | | Eliminating human errors | Poka-Yoke | Prevents defects, low cost | ★★★★ | | Continuous small improvements | Kaizen | Cultural, low cost | ★★★ | | Reducing inventory | JIT + Kanban | Pull system, eliminates waste | ★★★★ | | Project-based root cause elimination | DMAIC | Statistical rigor | ★★★★★ | | Equipment breakdowns | TPM | Zero breakdowns, autonomous maintenance | ★★★★ | | Workplace organization | 5S | Foundation for all other techniques | ★★★★★ | --- ## 3.5 Six Sigma DPMO Calculation — Step-by-Step > **Example:** In a credit card billing process, 500 errors are found in 1,000 transactions, with each transaction having 5 possible defect opportunities. **Step 1:** Calculate Total Opportunities: $$N \times O = 1000 \times 5 = 5000$$ **Step 2:** Apply the DPMO formula: $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{5000} = 100{,}000 \text{ DPMO}$$ **Step 3:** Convert DPMO to Sigma Level using the standard conversion table: 100,000 DPMO ≈ **3.1σ** (industry standard Six Sigma conversion table by Motorola). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WCM Control Techniques — Architectural Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[WCM Control Techniques] --> B1[Statistical Process Control] A0 --> B2[Quality Management Tools] A0 --> B3[Lean Manufacturing Tools] A0 --> B4[Reliability Engineering] B1 --> C1A[Control Charts] B1 --> C1B[Process Capability Cp Cpk] B1 --> C1C[Western Electric Rules] B2 --> C2A[TQM] B2 --> C2B[Six Sigma DMAIC] B2 --> C2C[FMEA] B2 --> C2D[PDCA Cycle] B3 --> C3A[5S Methodology] B3 --> C3B[Kaizen] B3 --> C3C[JIT Kanban] B3 --> C3D[Poka-Yoke] B3 --> C3E[Jidoka Autonomation] B4 --> C4A[TPM Total Productive Maintenance] B4 --> C4B[RCA 5-Whys Ishikawa] C1A --> D1[Monitor Variation] C1B --> D1 C2B --> D2[Statistical Problem Solving] C2D --> D3[Continuous Improvement Loop] C3A --> D4[Workplace Discipline] C3B --> D3 C3C --> D5[Waste Elimination] C3D --> D6[Zero Defect Culture] C3E --> D6 C4A --> D7[Zero Breakdowns] C4B --> D2 ``` ## 4.2 DMAIC Methodology — Detailed Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR DMAIC_D[Define Phase
Project Charter
SIPOC Diagram
VOC and CTQ] DMAIC_M[Measure Phase
Data Collection Plan
MSA Gage R and R
Baseline Cp Cpk and DPMO] DMAIC_A[Analyze Phase
Pareto Chart
Fishbone Diagram
5 Whys Root Cause
Hypothesis Tests] DMAIC_I[Improve Phase
Design of Experiments DOE
Pilot Runs
Poka Yoke Devices] DMAIC_C[Control Phase
SPC Control Charts
Updated Control Plan
Standard Operating Procedures] DMAIC_D --> DMAIC_M DMAIC_M --> DMAIC_A DMAIC_A --> DMAIC_I DMAIC_I --> DMAIC_C DMAIC_C -.-> DMAIC_D ``` ## 4.3 PDCA Cycle — Continuous Improvement Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD PDCA_P[PLAN
Identify Problem
Set Objectives
Predict Results] PDCA_D[DO
Implement Plan
Small Scale Trial
Collect Data] PDCA_C[CHECK
Analyze Results
Compare with Plan
Identify Gaps] PDCA_A[ACT
If Success Standardize
If Failure Revise
Loop Back to PLAN] PDCA_P --> PDCA_D PDCA_D --> PDCA_C PDCA_C --> PDCA_A PDCA_A -.-> PDCA_P ``` ## 4.4 5S Methodology — Sequential Pillars ```mermaid flowchart TD S5_1[1. SEIRI
Sort
Remove Unnecessary Items] S5_2[2. SEITON
Set in Order
A Place for Everything] S5_3[3. SEISO
Shine
Clean Workplace Daily] S5_4[4. SEIKETSU
Standardize
Procedures and Visual Mgmt] S5_5[5. SHITSUKE
Sustain
Discipline and Habit] S5_1 --> S5_2 S5_2 --> S5_3 S5_3 --> S5_4 S5_4 --> S5_5 S5_5 -.-> S5_1 ``` ## 4.5 Control Chart Architecture — Anatomy of a Control Chart ```mermaid flowchart TD CC1[Process Data Stream
Subgroup Samples n] CC2[Calculate Statistics
Mean X-bar and Range R] CC3[Compute Control Limits
UCL CL LCL using A2 D3 D4] CC4[Plot Time Series
X-bar on y-axis Time on x-axis] CC5{Apply Western Electric Rules} CC6a[In Control
Continue Monitoring] CC6b[Out of Control
Investigate Special Cause] CC7[Remove Assignable Cause
Revise Limits if Needed] CC1 --> CC2 CC2 --> CC3 CC3 --> CC4 CC4 --> CC5 CC5 --> CC6a CC5 --> CC6b CC6b --> CC7 CC7 --> CC4 ``` ## 4.6 Poka-Yoke Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD PK1[Identify Potential Error Source] PK2{Type of Detection?} PK3a[Contact Method
Physical Sensor Detects Error] PK3b[Fixed Value Method
Limit Number of Actions] PK3c[Sequence Method
Enforce Step Order] PK4[Install Poka-Yoke Device] PK5[Zero Defects at Source
Stop the Line if Needed] PK1 --> PK2 PK2 --> PK3a PK2 --> PK3b PK2 --> PK3c PK3a --> PK4 PK3b --> PK4 PK3c --> PK4 PK4 --> PK5 ``` ## 4.7 Kanban Pull System — Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR K1[Customer Demand Signal] K2[Withdrawal Kanban
Triggers Movement
of Parts] K3[Production Kanban
Authorizes Production
at Workstation] K4[Supplier or Upstream Process] K5[Finished Goods Output] K1 --> K2 K2 --> K3 K3 --> K4 K4 --> K5 K5 -.feedback.-> K1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Statistical Process Control (SPC) and name any two of its tools.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) is a data-driven methodology that uses statistical techniques to monitor, control, and improve a manufacturing process by distinguishing between common cause variation and special cause variation. It relies primarily on control charts to keep a process within its statistical limits, ensuring consistent product quality. **Two key tools of SPC:** 1. **Control Charts** (e.g., $\bar{X}$-chart, $R$-chart) — to monitor process stability over time. 2. **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$) — to quantify whether the process can meet specifications. --- ### **Question 2: What is Poka-Yoke? Give one industrial example.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Poka-Yoke is a Japanese term (developed by Shigeo Shingo) meaning "mistake-proofing" — it refers to any device or mechanism in a process that prevents an error from occurring or makes the error immediately obvious so that defects are not passed downstream. **Industrial Example:** A USB connector that can only be inserted in one correct orientation is a Poka-Yoke — its physical design prevents incorrect insertion. Similarly, in automobiles, a gear-shift interlock prevents the driver from shifting out of "Park" without pressing the brake pedal. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A: Control Charts and Process Capability** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** #### **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** Explain the construction of an $\bar{X}$-chart and an $R$-chart. Mention the formulas used and the role of control chart constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$. #### **Model Answer:** **Construction Steps for an $\bar{X}$-chart:** 1. **Collect Data:** Take 20–25 subgroups of size $n$ (usually $n = 4$ or $5$) in a time-ordered sequence. 2. **Compute Subgroup Statistics:** For each subgroup, calculate the mean $\bar{X}_i$ and range $R_i = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$. 3. **Compute Grand Statistics:** $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ and $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$, where $k$ is the number of subgroups. 4. **Compute Control Limits:** Use the formulas: $$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$ 5. **Plot the Chart:** Mark $\bar{X}_i$ values on the $y$-axis against subgroup number on the $x$-axis. Draw CL, UCL, and LCL as horizontal lines. 6. **Analyze Patterns:** Use Western Electric Rules to detect out-of-control signals. **Construction of $R$-chart:** Similar procedure, but plot $R_i$ values. Control limits: $$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad \text{CL}_R = \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$ **Role of Control Chart Constants:** | Constant | Formula | Role | |:---:|:---|:---| | $A_2$ | $3/(d_2 \sqrt{n})$ | Converts $\bar{R}$ to standard error of $\bar{X}$; used in $\bar{X}$-chart. | | $D_3$ | $1 - 3(d_3/d_2)$ | Lower control multiplier for $R$-chart. | | $D_4$ | $1 + 3(d_3/d_2)$ | Upper control multiplier for $R$-chart. | | $d_2$ | Tabulated | Relates average range $\bar{R}$ to $\sigma$: $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$. | **Interpretation:** If all $\bar{X}_i$ lie within $[\text{LCL}, \text{UCL}]$, the process mean is in control. If all $R_i$ lie within $R$-chart limits, the process variability is in control. --- #### **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** The diameters of piston rings (in mm) measured in subgroups of size $n = 5$ are summarized below: | Subgroup | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $\bar{X}_i$ | 74.005 | 73.998 | 74.003 | 73.995 | 74.000 | | $R_i$ | 0.018 | 0.022 | 0.020 | 0.025 | 0.015 | Given USL = 74.040 mm, LSL = 73.960 mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the sigma level. Comment on process capability. Given $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$. #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute grand average and average range: $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{74.005 + 73.998 + 74.003 + 73.995 + 74.000}{5} = \frac{184.001}{5} = 74.0002 \text{ mm}$$ $$\bar{R} = \frac{0.018 + 0.022 + 0.020 + 0.025 + 0.015}{5} = \frac{0.100}{5} = 0.020 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Estimate process standard deviation: $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.020}{2.326} = 0.008598 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $C_p$: $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.040 - 73.960}{6 \times 0.008598} = \frac{0.080}{0.051588} = 1.551$$ **Step 4:** Calculate $C_{pk}$: $$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.040 - 74.0002}{3 \times 0.008598} = \frac{0.0398}{0.025794} = 1.543$$ $$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{74.0002 - 73.960}{3 \times 0.008598} = \frac{0.0402}{0.025794} = 1.559$$ $$C_{pk} = \min(1.543, 1.559) = 1.543$$ **Step 5:** Sigma Level: $$Z = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.543 = 4.63\sigma$$ **Step 6: Comment:** Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.55$, the process is **well-centered and capable** (both exceed 1.33). The sigma level of 4.63σ indicates a strong, near–world-class process (although below the 6σ ideal of WCM, it is acceptable for general manufacturing). --- ### **Question B: Six Sigma DMAIC and Poka-Yoke** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** #### **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** Explain the DMAIC methodology of Six Sigma in detail. List the tools used in each phase and highlight its advantages over traditional quality approaches. #### **Model Answer:** **DMAIC** is the structured, data-driven problem-solving methodology of Six Sigma. It was codified by Motorola in the 1980s and adopted by General Electric, Honeywell, and other world-class manufacturers. **The Five Phases:** **1. DEFINE:** Identify the problem, customer requirements, and project scope. - **Tools:** Project Charter, SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers), VOC (Voice of Customer), CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) tree. - **Output:** A clear, measurable problem statement and project boundaries. **2. MEASURE:** Quantify the current state and baseline performance. - **Tools:** Data Collection Plan, Measurement System Analysis (MSA), Gage R&R, baseline $C_p$/$C_{pk}$, DPMO calculation, Pareto chart. - **Output:** A validated baseline sigma level of the current process. **3. ANALYZE:** Identify the root cause(s) of defects. - **Tools:** 5-Whys, Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram, Hypothesis Testing (t-test, ANOVA), Regression, Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA). - **Output:** Statistically validated root causes. **4. IMPROVE:** Implement solutions that address the validated root causes. - **Tools:** Design of Experiments (DOE), Pilot Runs, Kaizen events, Poka-Yoke devices, mistake-proofing. - **Output:** A process with improved $C_{pk}$ validated through pilot data. **5. CONTROL:** Sustain the improvements. - **Tools:** Statistical Process Control (SPC), updated Control Plans, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), training, documentation. - **Output:** A stabilized, monitored process with ongoing surveillance. **Advantages over Traditional Quality Approaches:** - Strong statistical foundation (vs. intuition-based decisions). - Project-based with clear ROI (Black Belts and Green Belts manage projects). - Tied to customer requirements (VOC/CTQ alignment). - Quantifiable results in financial terms. - Scalable from manufacturing to services and transactions. --- #### **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** Define Poka-Yoke and explain its three types with industrial examples. How does Poka-Yoke support the Zero-Defect objective of WCM? #### **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Poka-Yoke (Japanese: "mistake-proofing" or "inadvertent-error-proofing") is a technique developed by Shigeo Shingo at Toyota to prevent human errors from resulting in defects. It is a cornerstone of the Zero-Defect culture in WCM. **Three Types of Poka-Yoke:** | # | Type | Mechanism | Industrial Example | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Contact Method** | A physical sensor or jig detects an anomaly and triggers a stop or alarm. | A limit switch on a CNC machine that stops the spindle if the part is misaligned. | | 2 | **Fixed-Value Method** | The device restricts the operation to a predetermined number of actions. | A riveting gun that automatically counts and stops after exactly 4 rivets (no more, no less). | | 3 | **Sequence Method** | The device enforces a strict order of operations; the next step is impossible until the previous is complete. | A washing machine that only starts the spin cycle after the lid is closed and the wash cycle is complete. | **How Poka-Yoke supports Zero-Defect in WCM:** 1. **Source Inspection:** Detects errors at the source rather than after the fact, eliminating the need for rework. 2. **100% Inspection Possible:** Unlike human inspectors, Poka-Yoke can check every single unit, achieving true 100% inspection. 3. **Low Cost:** Mechanical or electronic Poka-Yokes are inexpensive compared to scrap, rework, and warranty costs. 4. **Empowers Workers:** Workers can stop the line confidently (Jidoka), making defects visible and fixable. 5. **Feeds Continuous Improvement:** Each Poka-Yoke triggers analysis of *why* the mistake was possible, leading to deeper systemic fixes. **Conclusion:** Poka-Yoke is the practical embodiment of the WCM principle that *quality must be built into the process, not inspected in afterward*. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. **$C_p$** measures *spread only* (potential capability). **$C_{pk}$** measures *spread AND centering* (actual capability). Always compute both and identify the lesser. > - **Pitfall 2:** In control chart problems, students forget to convert $\bar{R}$ to $\sigma$ using the $d_2$ constant — leading to wrong control limits. > - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing the **3-Sigma rule** of control charts with **Six Sigma**. Six Sigma uses a **1.5σ shift** allowance, making $C_{pk} = 2.0$ equivalent to 6σ long-term. > - **Pitfall 4:** DMAIC phases must be answered in the correct order with **specific tools**, not generic terms like "improve quality." > - **Pitfall 5:** For Poka-Yoke, students often describe only one type and miss the other two. Always list all three: **Contact, Fixed-Value, Sequence**. > - **Pitfall 6:** The LCL of an $R$-chart is often negative for small $n$ — use $D_3 = 0$ to set LCL $= 0$ (a range cannot be negative). > - **Pitfall 7:** Takt Time is in **seconds per unit**, not units per second — watch the unit conversion carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ SPC is **data-driven**; it distinguishes **common cause** (systemic) from **special cause** (assignable) variation. - ✅ Control limits are set at **±3σ** (99.73% confidence for normal distribution). - ✅ Specification limits (USL/LSL) are set by **customer/design**; control limits are set by **process**. - ✅ **$\bar{X}$-chart** monitors the **process mean**; **$R$-chart** monitors **process variability**. - ✅ Constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$ depend **only on sample size $n$**. - ✅ $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the **minimum acceptable** capability; $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$ is the **target** for WCM. - ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** with a 1.5σ shift allowance. - ✅ **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (in that order, always). - ✅ **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (Deming's continuous improvement cycle). - ✅ **5S** = Seiri (Sort) → Seiton (Set in Order) → Seiso (Shine) → Seiketsu (Standardize) → Shitsuke (Sustain). - ✅ **Kaizen** = "Change for the Better" — small, continuous, employee-driven improvements. - ✅ **Poka-Yoke** has 3 types: **Contact, Fixed-Value, Sequence**. - ✅ **Jidoka / Autonomation** = automation with a human touch; stop the line on defect detection. - ✅ **Kanban** = visual signal that triggers pull-based production. - ✅ **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Customer Demand (drives line balancing). - ✅ **TPM** aims at **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents** through autonomous maintenance. - ✅ **FMEA** ranks failure modes by **RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection**. - ✅ **Western Electric Rules** detect out-of-control conditions (1 point beyond 3σ, 2 of 3 beyond 2σ, 4 of 5 beyond 1σ, 8 consecutive on one side). - ✅ A process can be **in control but not capable** (Cpk < 1) — both conditions must be satisfied for WCM. - ✅ Toyota Production System (TPS) integrates: **JIT + Jidoka + Kaizen + 5S + Poka-Yoke + Kanban** as its WCM control backbone. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When given numerical problems, always show your constants table, state your assumptions (e.g., normal distribution), and conclude with an **engineering interpretation** — not just numerical answers. KTU examiners reward interpretation with full marks!

topics.title: Principles of WCM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of WCM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is defined as the state of manufacturing excellence achieved when a firm systematically integrates and deploys a portfolio of advanced management philosophies, technologies, lean tools, and quality practices to deliver products and services that meet global benchmarks in cost, quality, delivery, flexibility, and innovation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Schonberger, 1986):**  
> "WCM is a journey toward achieving the shortest possible cycle time, zero defects, zero inventory, zero breakdowns, and the flexibility to respond to changing customer demands at world-competitive cost and quality."

In KTU terminology, WCM is not a destination but a **continuous journey** where every process is benchmarked, every employee is empowered, and every decision is data-driven.

## 1.2 The KTU Conceptual Pillars (Module 1 Focus)

For Module 1, the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **four foundational pillars** of WCM that act as the philosophical bedrock for the rest of the course:

| # | Pillar | Core Focus |
|---|--------|------------|
| 1 | **Customer Focus & Quality First** | Voice of Customer (VoC), TQM, Zero Defects |
| 2 | **Just-in-Time (JIT)** | Inventory elimination, Pull systems, Flow |
| 3 | **Total Preventive Maintenance (TPM)** | Zero breakdowns, OEE maximization |
| 4 | **Total Employee Involvement (TEI)** | Kaizen, Suggestion schemes, Empowerment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Hook:** The KTU 2024 module descriptor specifically tests students on the *interrelationship* between these pillars — not just their definitions. Marks are awarded for showing how they form an **integrated system** rather than standalone tools.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Five-Star Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy:**  
> Imagine a Michelin-star restaurant. The chef (WCM leader) insists that:
> - Ingredients arrive **just in time** before cooking — no overflowing fridge (JIT).
> - Every plate must be **flawless** before leaving the kitchen (Zero Defects).
> - Knives, stoves, and ovens are always **sharp and ready** (TPM).
> - Every cook is trained to notice waste and **suggest improvements** (Kaizen).
> - The menu evolves based on **what guests actually order** (Customer Focus).
>
> A factory running WCM principles operates exactly like this kitchen — fast, clean, precise, and customer-obsessed.

## 1.4 The Evolution of Manufacturing Thought (Why WCM Exists)

The journey leading to WCM explains *why* its principles exist:

1. **Craft Production** (Pre-1900) → Skilled, slow, custom
2. **Mass Production** (Ford, 1900s) → Cheap, rigid, wasteful
3. **Lean Production** (Toyota, 1950s) → Flexible, efficient
4. **World Class Manufacturing** (1980s+) → Globally benchmarked, integrated excellence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** WCM is the **synthesis** of ideas from Japanese (Toyota Production System), American (Six Sigma, Deming), and European (Schonberger, Hayes-Wheelwright) schools of thought.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle 1 — Customer Focus & Quality First

**Why it matters:** No manufacturing system can be "world class" if it does not satisfy the end-customer. Quality is not a department — it is an *attitude*.

**Core Logic Steps:**

- Identify the **Voice of Customer (VoC)** through surveys, complaints, warranty data.
- Translate VoC into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** specifications using the **House of Quality (QFD)**.
- Drive quality upstream to **prevent** defects (Poka-Yoke, mistake-proofing) rather than inspect them downstream.
- Measure with **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** to quantify the financial impact of defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Buzzword:** The phrase "**Quality is Free**" was coined by Philip Crosby — the cost of *prevention* is always less than the cost of *correction*.

## 2.2 Principle 2 — Just-in-Time (JIT)

**The Core Idea:** Produce and deliver *exactly* what is needed, *exactly* when it is needed, and *exactly* in the amount needed.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- Eliminate all forms of **waste (Muda)** — overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects (the **7 Wastes**, or *TIMWOODS*).
- Establish **continuous flow** with **Takt time** as the heartbeat.
- Use **Kanban** (signals) to pull materials only when consumed downstream.
- Apply **Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** to enable small, frequent batches.

## 2.3 Principle 3 — Total Preventive Maintenance (TPM)

**The Core Idea:** Every machine must be in perfect condition, every day, owned by the operator — not just the maintenance department.

**The 8 Pillars of TPM (Industry Standard):**

- Autonomous Maintenance
- Planned Maintenance
- Quality Maintenance
- Focused Improvement
- Early Equipment Management
- Training & Education
- Safety, Health & Environment
- Administrative & Office TPM

**Key Performance Driver — OEE:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The OEE metric is the single most-tested numerical concept in this module for KTU exams.**

## 2.4 Principle 4 — Total Employee Involvement (TEI)

**The Core Idea:** Continuous improvement (Kaizen) cannot be mandated from the top floor — it must be co-created by the people closest to the work.

**Operational Mechanisms:**

- **Quality Circles** (small groups, 6–10 workers, weekly meetings).
- **Suggestion Schemes** with reward systems.
- **Cross-functional Teams** for problem-solving (e.g., 5-Why analysis).
- **Autonomous Decision-Making** at the shop-floor level.

## 2.5 The Hayes-Wheelwright Model (WCM Progression Stages)

Harvard professors Robert Hayes and Steven Wheelwright proposed four stages a firm passes through to reach world-class status:

| Stage | Label | Characteristics | WCM Score |
|-------|-------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Internally Neutral** | Operates in isolation, no benchmarking | Lowest |
| 2 | **Internally Supportive** | Recognizes manufacturing's strategic role | Improving |
| 3 | **Externally Supportive** | Begins benchmarking competitors | Strong |
| 4 | **World Class** | Industry leader, sets benchmarks, predictive | Highest |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — every numerical KTU question on this module is solved from one of these formulas.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Standard / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Expressed as a percentage (×100) | Benchmarking machine productivity globally |
| 2 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{Planned\ Run\ Time - Downtime}{Planned\ Run\ Time}$ | Ratio (0 → 1) | Measures unplanned stoppages |
| 3 | **Performance Efficiency** | $P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{Run\ Time}$ | Ratio (0 → 1) | Measures speed losses |
| 4 | **Quality Rate** | $Q = \dfrac{Good\ Units}{Total\ Units\ Produced}$ | Ratio (0 → 1) | Measures yield / first-pass quality |
| 5 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{Available\ Production\ Time}{Customer\ Demand}$ | Seconds per unit | Sets the heartbeat of production |
| 6 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle}$ = measured time per unit | Seconds per unit | Compared against Takt Time |
| 7 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{Good\ Units\ at\ First\ Attempt}{Total\ Units\ Started}$ | Percentage | Detects hidden rework waste |
| 8 | **Sigma Level to DPMO** | $DPMO = D \times 1{,}000{,}000 / U$ | Defects per million opportunities | Six Sigma quality metric |
| 9 | **Kaizen Savings Index** | $KSI = \dfrac{Kaizen\ Savings\ Realized}{Target\ Savings}$ | Ratio | Measures improvement culture maturity |
| 10 | **COPQ Ratio** | $COPQ\% = \dfrac{Cost\ of\ Poor\ Quality}{Total\ Revenue}$ | Percentage | Justifies quality investments to management |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In OEE, students often multiply the three ratios as decimal fractions but forget to **multiply by 100** to express the final answer as a percentage. Always state units explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: OEE Calculation (Full Mark Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Question (KTU Pattern):** A CNC machine has a scheduled shift of 8 hours (480 minutes). It takes a 30-minute lunch break and a 10-minute morning tea break. During the shift, the machine experiences 20 minutes of unplanned downtime. The ideal cycle time per part is 1.5 minutes. The machine produced 220 parts in the shift, out of which 8 were rejected. Calculate the OEE.

### Step 1 — Compute Planned Production Time

Planned production time is the total time the machine *should* have been available for production, excluding planned non-production activities such as lunch and tea breaks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Shift Time} &= 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \\
&= 480 \text{ minutes} \\
\text{Planned Downtime (lunch + tea)} &= 30 + 10 = 40 \text{ minutes} \\
\text{Planned Production Time} &= 480 - 40 \\
&= 440 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Availability

Availability is the ratio of actual operating time to planned production time. It captures *uptime losses* such as breakdowns and setup adjustments.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Time} &= \text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Unplanned Downtime} \\
&= 440 - 20 = 420 \text{ minutes} \\
A &= \frac{420}{440} \\
&= 0.9545 \\
&\approx 95.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Performance Efficiency

Performance captures *speed losses* — the gap between the theoretical ideal cycle time and the actual cycle time achieved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Production} &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Ideal Cycle Time}} \\
&= \frac{420}{1.5} = 280 \text{ parts} \\
P &= \frac{280}{220} \\
&= 1.2727
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Performance efficiency **should not exceed 1.0 (100%)** in real-world calculations. A value > 1.0 indicates a data error — usually a wrong ideal cycle time. Cap it at 1.0 (or 100%) for validity, or flag the anomaly in the answer.

Since 1.2727 > 1.0, we cap it:

$$
P_{capped} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Quality Rate

Quality captures *yield losses* — parts made that did not meet specification.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \\
&= \frac{220 - 8}{220} = \frac{212}{220} \\
&= 0.9636 \\
&\approx 96.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Final OEE

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\
&= 0.9545 \times 1.0 \times 0.9636 \\
&= 0.9198 \\
&\approx 91.98\% \\
&\approx 92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — KTU Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An OEE of ~92% is **World Class** by industry standards. The general benchmark table is:
> - 100% = Perfect production
> - 85% = World Class (Industry target)
> - 60% = Typical (room for improvement)
> - 40% = Inefficient

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping WCM Principles to Origin Schools

| WCM Principle | Origin / School | Founder | Core Tool |
|---------------|------------------|---------|-----------|
| Customer Focus | Marketing + TQM | Juran, Deming | QFD, VoC |
| Quality First | TQM | Crosby, Ishikawa | 7 QC Tools |
| JIT & Lean | Toyota Production System | Taiichi Ohno | Kanban, Heijunka |
| TPM | JIPM (Japan) | Seiichi Nakajima | 8 Pillars of TPM |
| Kaizen / TEI | Japanese Management | Masaaki Imai | Suggestion systems |
| Six Sigma | Motorola | Bill Smith | DMAIC, DPMO |
| Benchmarking | Xerox | Robert Camp | Gap analysis |
| Agile Manufacturing | Industry 4.0 / USA | Goldman, Nagel | Modular design |
| Quick Changeover | Toyota | Shigeo Shingo | SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) |
| Statistical Process Control | Shewhart (1924) | Walter Shewhart | Control charts (X-bar, R) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The WCM House — Integrated Pillar Architecture

The WCM framework can be visualized as a "house" where the roof is the vision and the foundation is the workforce. Every principle is a load-bearing pillar.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Roof[World Class Performance World Benchmarks in Cost Quality Delivery] 
    P1[Pillar 1 Customer Focus and Quality First]
    P2[Pillar 2 Just In Time and Lean Flow]
    P3[Pillar 3 Total Preventive Maintenance]
    P4[Pillar 4 Total Employee Involvement Kaizen]
    P5[Pillar 5 Statistical Process Control]
    P6[Pillar 6 Benchmarking and Continuous Learning]
    Found[Foundation Empowerment Training and Culture]
    Strat[Strategic Alignment Vision and Leadership]

    Strat --> Roof
    Found --> P1
    Found --> P2
    Found --> P3
    Found --> P4
    Found --> P5
    Found --> P6
    P1 --> Roof
    P2 --> Roof
    P3 --> Roof
    P4 --> Roof
    P5 --> Roof
    P6 --> Roof
```

## 4.2 The Hayes-Wheelwright WCM Progression Ladder

This diagram maps the four-stage evolution from a low-maturity firm to a world-class manufacturer. Each step requires a *qualitative* shift in mindset, not just new tools.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Stage 1 Internally Neutral Manufacturing is a Black Box] --> S2[Stage 2 Internally Supportive Manufacturing Becomes a Strategic Function]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Externally Supportive Benchmarking Against Competitors]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 World Class Sets the Industry Benchmark Predicts Market Needs]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
```

## 4.3 The JIT Pull System with Kanban Flow

The JIT principle is operationalized through a **pull** mechanism: a downstream signal (kanban) triggers upstream production. Inventory never accumulates because nothing is produced without a demand signal.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Cust[Customer Order] --> Ship[Shipping]
    Ship --> Assy[Assembly Line]
    Assy --> K1{Kanban Card Empty?}
    K1 -- Yes --> Fab[Fabrication Shop Produces]
    K1 -- No --> Assy
    Fab --> K2{Kanban Card Empty?}
    K2 -- Yes --> Sup[Supplier Triggers Replenishment]
    K2 -- No --> Fab
    Sup --> Fab
```

## 4.4 The 7 Wastes (Muda) Elimination Map

Toyota identifies seven classic wastes. The eighth is sometimes added as "untapped human potential." The diagram below shows how each waste is countered by a specific WCM tool.

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Overproduction] --> T1[Countered by JIT Pull System]
    W2[Waiting] --> T2[Countered by Line Balancing and Takt Time]
    W3[Transport] --> T3[Countered by Plant Layout Optimization]
    W4[Over Processing] --> T4[Countered by Value Stream Mapping]
    W5[Inventory] --> T5[Countered by Kanban and Small Batches]
    W6[Motion] --> T6[Countered by 5S Workplace Organization]
    W7[Defects] --> T7[Countered by Poka Yoke and SPC]
    W8[Untapped Human Potential] --> T8[Countered by Kaizen and TEI]
```

## 4.5 The Continuous Improvement (PDCA) Engine

WCM's engine is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle, popularized by W. Edwards Deming. Every principle (TPM, JIT, TQM) runs on this loop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Plan Identify Problem and Set Goals] --> D[Do Implement on a Small Scale]
    D --> C[Check Measure Results Against Goals]
    C --> A[Act Standardize or Iterate]
    A --> P
```

## 4.6 TPM Pillar Architecture (Detailed View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Goal[Zero Accidents Zero Defects Zero Breakdowns]
    PM1[Autonomous Maintenance Operator Owned]
    PM2[Planned Maintenance Time Based Schedule]
    PM3[Quality Maintenance Defect Prevention]
    PM4[Focused Improvement Kobetsu Kaizen]
    PM5[Early Equipment Management New Equipment Design]
    PM6[Training and Education Skill Development]
    PM7[Safety Health and Environment]
    PM8[Administrative and Office TPM]

    PM1 --> Goal
    PM2 --> Goal
    PM3 --> Goal
    PM4 --> Goal
    PM5 --> Goal
    PM6 --> Goal
    PM7 --> Goal
    PM8 --> Goal
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
