<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between Order Qualifiers and Order Winners with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Order Qualifier | Order Winner |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A minimum threshold characteristic that a firm MUST possess to be considered as a supplier | A distinguishing characteristic that wins the customer's order over competing suppliers |
| **If missing** | Firm is rejected outright | Firm is considered but not preferred |
| **Example (Automobile)** | Airbags and ABS — every car must have them | Best fuel efficiency in segment (e.g., Maruti's mileage claim) |
| **Example (IT Services)** | ISO 27001 certification — must-have for security-conscious clients | Fastest go-live for a complex SAP implementation |
| **Nature** | Necessary condition (qualification) | Sufficient condition (winning) |
| **Time-dependence** | Relatively stable | Changes with market dynamics |

*Marking Note (1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example pair).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the six competitive priorities in manufacturing and state ONE metric for any two of them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six competitive priorities are:
1. Cost
2. Quality
3. Delivery
4. Flexibility
5. Service
6. Innovation

**Two metrics with units:**
- **Cost** $\rightarrow$ Unit manufacturing cost (₹ / unit) or Cost-Competitiveness Ratio $CCR = C_{Firm} / C_{Industry}$
- **Quality** $\rightarrow$ First-Pass Yield (FPY) in **%** OR Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)
- **Delivery** $\rightarrow$ On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) in **%**
- **Flexibility** $\rightarrow$ Setup time reduction in **minutes** (SMED metric)
- **Service** $\rightarrow$ Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) in **hours**
- **Innovation** $\rightarrow$ Time-to-Market (TTM) in **months**

*Marking Note: 1 mark for listing all six correctly, 2 marks for metrics with units.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Hayes-Wheelwright four-stage model** of manufacturing competitiveness. Discuss how a typical Indian manufacturing firm would progress through these stages, citing at least two real industrial examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the aid of a Production Possibility Frontier (PPF) diagram, explain the **concept of trade-offs** among competitive priorities. Discuss how modern WCM tools (Lean, Six-Sigma, Industry 4.0) attempt to manage this trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Hayes-Wheelwright Model (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Hayes-Wheelwright model (1984) is the canonical framework describing the evolution of a manufacturing function's strategic role. It proposes four progressive stages, each representing a higher level of strategic contribution to the firm.

**2. Stage-by-Stage Description (4 marks):**

- **Stage 1 — Internally Neutral:** Manufacturing is *reactive* and acts as a buffer between internal demands (e.g., sales, engineering) and shop-floor realities. It offers no strategic differentiation. *Example:* Many pre-1990s Indian PSU manufacturing units (e.g., early-cycle BHEL) — production was schedule-driven, with no input into product design or market positioning.

- **Stage 2 — Externally Neutral:** Manufacturing begins *benchmarking* its performance against industry peers, achieving parity. It still does not *shape* strategy. *Example:* Hero Cycles in the 1990s — world-class cost levels matched Chinese imports, but no innovation lead.

- **Stage 3 — Internally Supportive:** Manufacturing proactively *supports* the chosen business strategy by developing tailored capabilities (cost leadership, customization, etc.). *Example:* Maruti Suzuki in the 2000s — manufacturing excellence (Suzuki Production System adapted as Maruti Production System) supported the cost-leadership business strategy.

- **Stage 4 — Externally Supportive:** Manufacturing becomes a *strategic weapon* that influences the entire industry. *Example:* Tata Nano project — manufacturing innovation (radical low-cost platform) created an entirely new market segment.

**3. Critical Analysis (2 marks):**
The progression is non-linear and requires conscious leadership commitment. Firms can regress if strategy shifts without manufacturing alignment. The model is critiqued for being Western-centric; in Indian contexts, *Stage 3* is often the realistic ceiling for most firms in the medium term, given capital and skill constraints.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Trade-offs & PPF (7 marks)

**1. Define Trade-off (1 mark):**
A trade-off in WCM refers to the strategic impossibility of simultaneously maximizing all competitive priorities with a fixed resource envelope. Choosing *more* of one priority typically yields *less* of another.

**2. Mathematical Form (2 marks):**
The trade-off is captured by the constraint:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot P_i \leq C_{resource}$$

where $w_i$ is the strategic weight of priority $i$, $P_i$ is its achieved level, and $C_{resource}$ is the total resource budget. The PPF represents the maximum combinations of $P_A$ and $P_B$ that can be achieved simultaneously.

**3. Diagrammatic Explanation (2 marks):**
The PPF is a downward-sloping curve in the $(P_A, P_B)$ plane. Any point *inside* the frontier represents sub-optimal resource use. Any point *beyond* it is unachievable without increasing $C_{resource}$ or shifting the frontier outward.

**4. How WCM Tools Manage the Trade-off (2 marks):**
- **Lean Manufacturing** — reduces waste, effectively *expanding* the resource budget $C_{resource}$ and shifting the PPF outward.
- **Six-Sigma** — reduces variation, allowing the same quality level to be achieved with *less* over-engineering, freeing capacity.
- **Industry 4.0 (IoT, AI, Cyber-Physical Systems)** — enables mass customization, breaking the classical cost-vs-flexibility trade-off through real-time reconfigurability.
- **Agile Manufacturing** — modular, scalable systems absorb demand volatility, relaxing the volume-flexibility cost penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

> **(a)** Define the term **"Competitive Priorities"** in World Class Manufacturing. Discuss any **FOUR** of them in detail, with appropriate industrial examples and one quantitative metric per priority. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concepts of **"Order Qualifiers"** and **"Order Winners"** with a real-time case study of a manufacturing firm of your choice. Show with a decision tree how a customer moves from qualification to order placement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Competitive Priorities (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Competitive priorities are the strategic dimensions along which a manufacturing firm chooses to compete in its target market — the capabilities it deliberately cultivates to attract and retain customers, consistent with its overall business strategy.

**2. Discussion of Four Priorities (4 marks, 1 mark each):**

**(i) Cost (Price Leadership):**
The ability to produce at the lowest sustainable cost, enabling aggressive market pricing.
- *Industrial Example:* Foxconn in contract electronics manufacturing — leverages Chinese/Tamil Nadu scale economies.
- *Metric:* **Unit manufacturing cost** in **₹/unit** OR **Cost-Competitiveness Ratio** $CCR = C_{Firm} / C_{Industry}$ (target $< 1$).

**(ii) Quality:**
Dual construct: *Performance quality* (high specification) and *Conformance quality* (low defect rate).
- *Industrial Example:* Maruti Suzuki's conformance quality — world-class paint and assembly finish in the B-segment.
- *Metric:* **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** in **%** OR **DPMO** for six-sigma programs.

**(iii) Delivery (Speed + Reliability):**
The combination of *rapid order fulfillment* and *on-time consistency*.
- *Industrial Example:* Amazon's delivery network — same-day / next-day in metros.
- *Metric:* **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** in **%**, calculated as $OTIF = (N_{OTIF} / N_{Total}) \times 100$.

**(iv) Flexibility (Volume/Mix/Product):**
The capacity to adapt output volume, product mix, or design without excessive cost penalty.
- *Industrial Example:* Toyota's flexible production lines — able to switch between Corolla, Camry, and hybrid variants on the same line.
- *Metric:* **SMED setup time** in **minutes** (Single-Minute Exchange of Die).

**3. Concluding Remark (2 marks):**
Each priority requires dedicated infrastructure and culture. The strategic art is to choose the *right two or three* to excel in, given the firm's market and capabilities. Pursuing all six equally is a recipe for *strategic mediocrity*.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Order Qualifiers & Winners (7 marks)

**1. Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Order Qualifier:** A baseline manufacturing/market characteristic without which the customer will not even consider the firm. It is a *gating* criterion.
- **Order Winner:** A characteristic that *differentiates* the firm from competitors and tips the customer's choice in its favor. It is a *discriminating* criterion.

**2. Case Study — Hyundai Motor India (3 marks):**

Consider a Mumbai-based customer purchasing a mid-size SUV in 2024.

**Order Qualifiers (must-haves):**
- 5-star GNCAP/BNCAP crash safety rating
- Standard 6-airbag configuration
- BS-VI Phase-2 emission compliance
- Service network reach in customer's city

**Order Winners (differentiators):**
- Highest-in-segment warranty (e.g., 5-year unlimited-km warranty by Hyundai)
- ADAS Level-2 features at lower price point than Toyota/Suzuki
- Turbo-petrol engine refinement + best fuel economy in class

**Why it qualifies as a "real-time" example:** These are not textbook constructs; the 2024 Indian auto market visibly segments on these qualifiers and winners, as observed in monthly sales ranking shifts.

**3. Decision-Tree Logic (2 marks):**
The customer's decision process follows a sequential filter:

1. *Filter by Order Qualifiers:* Does the car pass minimum safety, emissions, and serviceability gates? **No** $\rightarrow$ rejected. **Yes** $\rightarrow$ enters consideration set.
2. *Compare on Order Winners:* Among the surviving candidates, which offers the best warranty, features-per-rupee, and ownership cost? Winner is selected.
3. *Strategic Implication:* A firm failing on a qualifier (e.g., missing ADAS in 2024) is *invisible* to the customer. A firm meeting qualifiers but losing on winners gets *considered but not selected* (the "showroom-floor reject" phenomenon).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Competitive Priority Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "competitive priority" with "manufacturing objective":** A priority is a *customer-facing capability*; an objective is an *internal measurable target*. Don't blur them.
> 2. **Listing priorities without examples:** For 7-mark questions, *every* priority cited MUST be backed by an industrial example and a quantitative metric. Bare lists earn at most 60% marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the trade-off:** When asked about WCM, examiners expect explicit discussion of *why* all six cannot be maximized simultaneously. Skipping this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing Hayes-Wheelwright and Hill frameworks incorrectly:** Hayes-Wheelwright is the 4-stage model; Hill's contribution is the order qualifier/winner distinction. Keep them separate.
> 5. **Forgetting units in formulas:** Any numeric calculation (OTIF, FPY, RTY) MUST have **%** or the relevant SI unit. Missing units = 0.5 mark loss per occurrence.
> 6. **Using only Indian examples is acceptable, but examiners reward examples from varied industries (auto + pharma + IT) to test depth.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Competitive Priorities** = *strategic* capabilities a firm chooses to compete on; they are *market-derived*, not internally invented.
- [ ] **Six priorities:** Cost, Quality, Delivery, Flexibility, Service, Innovation.
- [ ] **Order Qualifier** = necessary threshold; **Order Winner** = discriminating differentiator.
- [ ] A characteristic can shift between *Qualifier* and *Winner* over time (e.g., ABS was a *Winner* in 2000, is a *Qualifier* in 2024).
- [ ] **Hayes-Wheelwright Four Stages:** *Internally Neutral → Externally Neutral → Internally Supportive → Externally Supportive*.
- [ ] **Trade-off** is captured by the constraint $\sum w_i P_i \leq C_{resource}$; modern WCM tools shift the PPF outward, not eliminate it.
- [ ] **Quality has two faces:** Performance quality (design excellence) and Conformance quality (defect-freedom).
- [ ] **Delivery has two faces:** Speed (fast) and Reliability (on-time) — they are independent metrics.
- [ ] **Flexibility has four faces:** Volume, Mix, Product, Process flexibility.
- [ ] **OTIF Formula:** $OTIF = (N_{on-time\,in-full} / N_{total}) \times 100$; world-class target $\geq 95\%$.
- [ ] **FPY Formula:** $FPY = (N_{good @ first pass} / N_{input}) \times 100$.
- [ ] **RTY Formula:** $RTY = \prod Y_i$ — quality degrades *multiplicatively* across stages.
- [ ] **Sigma–DPMO relation:** $6\sigma \approx 3.4$ DPMO; $4\sigma \approx 6,210$ DPMO.
- [ ] **Six-Sigma + Lean = Lean Six-Sigma** is the WCM quality program combining both.
- [ ] **Industry 4.0 technologies** (IoT, AI, CPS, Digital Twins) relax but do NOT remove trade-offs.
- [ ] **Indian industrial exemplars to remember:** Maruti (cost-quality-delivery), Tata Nano (innovation), Infosys (service quality), L&T (service-engineering integration), Titan (product quality + brand).
- [ ] **Mass Customization** = strategic WCM aspiration to break the classical cost-vs-variety trade-off.
- [ ] In KTU exam answers, **always end with a concluding strategic recommendation**, not just a description.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Aggregate Planning # MODULE 2: AGGREGATE PLANNING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is the *medium-range* (3 to 18 months) operations management activity of matching the **supply** of output (production rate, workforce level, inventory on hand) with the **forecasted demand** for a family of similar products, while **minimizing total relevant costs** (hiring, firing, overtime, holding, backordering, subcontracting). It is called *aggregate* because it does not plan individual SKUs, but rather **product families** (e.g., "all sedans", not "Honda City white 1.5L petrol"). The plan generated is later *disaggregated* into a **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. The horizon sits strategically between: | Horizon | Time Frame | Plan Type | |---|---|---| | Long Range | > 1 year | Capacity, Facility, Technology | | **Aggregate Range** | **3 – 18 months** | **Workforce, Production Rate, Inventory** | | Short Range | < 3 months | Scheduling, Sequencing, Dispatching | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bucket-Well" Analogy) Imagine your company is a **farmer with a large water tank (inventory)**. Rainfall is **demand**, and a tap (workforce + production) fills the tank. The farmer cannot control the rain, but he can: - Adjust the **tap flow** (workforce / production rate), - Let the **tank level** rise and fall (inventory), - **Buy** extra water from neighbours (subcontracting / overtime), - **Dump** excess water (backorder / lost sales). Aggregate Planning is the farmer deciding *how hard to turn the tap each month* so that the tank never overflows (excess inventory cost) and never empties (stockout), while spending the *least money* on tap adjustments and water purchases. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Aggregate Planning is fundamentally a **cost-minimization matching problem** between a volatile demand curve and a flexible supply curve. ### 1.3 Objectives of Aggregate Planning (KTU Board Perspective) 1. **Minimize total cost** of production, inventory, and workforce adjustments. 2. **Maximize customer service** by avoiding stockouts and backorders. 3. **Smooth production** to avoid erratic workforce fluctuations. 4. **Establish a feasible production rate** within capacity limits. 5. Provide a **bridge** between strategic planning and the Master Production Schedule (MPS). 6. Maintain **stable workforce** wherever possible (socially and legally mandated in many contexts). ### 1.4 Inputs and Outputs of Aggregate Planning ``` INPUTS OUTPUTS ───────── ─────── • Demand Forecasts (D_t) • Production Quantities (P_t) • Current Inventory (I_0) • Workforce Levels (W_t) • Capacity Constraints • Inventory Levels (I_t) • Cost Parameters • Subcontracting Levels (S_t) • Company Policy (hiring caps) • Backorder Levels (B_t) • External Constraints (overtime limits) • Total Cost ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pure Aggregate Planning Strategies ### 2.1.1 Strategy 1: Chase Strategy (Demand Matching) - **Idea:** Production rate *exactly* matches demand each period. - **Workforce:** Fluctuates each period (hires in high demand, fires in low demand). - **Inventory:** Held at near-zero (no buffer stock). - **Pros:** Very low inventory carrying cost. - **Cons:** Very high hiring/firing cost; morale issues; skill erosion; legal constraints in India under the Industrial Disputes Act. ### 2.1.2 Strategy 2: Level Strategy (Constant Workforce) - **Idea:** Production rate and workforce are **held constant** across all periods. - **Inventory:** Accumulates in low-demand periods, depletes in high-demand periods. - **Pros:** Stable workforce, no hiring/firing cost. - **Cons:** High inventory holding cost; risk of stockouts; requires large warehouses. ### 2.1.3 Strategy 3: Mixed / Hybrid Strategy - **Idea:** Uses a combination — modest workforce changes + overtime/undertime + inventory + subcontracting. - **Reality check:** This is what *most real WCM companies* use because pure strategies are extreme. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always *compare* the total cost of at least 2 strategies numerically in 14-mark questions. Examiners reward a clear tabular comparison. ## 2.2 The Six "Levers" of Aggregate Planning The planner can manipulate exactly **six supply variables**: 1. **Production rate** (units/period) 2. **Workforce size** (number of workers) 3. **Overtime / Undertime** (hours beyond or below regular shift) 4. **Inventory levels** (stock carried) 5. **Backorders / Lost sales** (deferred demand) 6. **Subcontracting / Part-time labor** (external capacity) ## 2.3 Cost Components (The KTU Cost Universe) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the cost names and their typical units — they appear every year.** | Symbol | Cost | Formula | Typical Unit | |:---:|---|---|:---:| | $C_{RT}$ | Regular-time production cost | $C_{RT} \cdot P_{RT}$ | ₹ / unit | | $C_{OT}$ | Overtime production cost | $C_{OT} \cdot P_{OT}$ | ₹ / unit (usually $1.5 \times C_{RT}$) | | $C_H$ | Hiring / Layoff-off cost | $C_H \cdot \vert \Delta W \vert^+$ | ₹ / worker hired | | $C_F$ | Firing / Layoff cost | $C_F \cdot \vert \Delta W \vert^-$ | ₹ / worker fired | | $C_{Inv}$ | Inventory holding cost | $C_{Inv} \cdot I_t$ | ₹ / unit / period | | $C_{BO}$ | Backorder cost | $C_{BO} \cdot B_t$ | ₹ / unit / period | | $C_{Sub}$ | Subcontracting cost | $C_{Sub} \cdot S_t$ | ₹ / unit | | $C_{UT}$ | Undertime / Idle time cost | $C_{UT} \cdot U_t$ | ₹ / hour | ## 2.4 Decision Variables and Constraints For each period $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Production in period } t: \quad & P_t = P_{RT,t} + P_{OT,t} + S_t \\[4pt] \text{Workforce balance: } \quad & W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t \\[4pt] \text{Inventory balance: } \quad & I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t - B_{t-1} + B_t \\[4pt] \text{Ending inventory: } \quad & I_t \ge 0 \quad \text{(if no backorders allowed)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Decision Variables:** $W_t$, $H_t$, $F_t$, $P_{RT,t}$, $P_{OT,t}$, $S_t$, $I_t$, $B_t$ for every $t$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation | Application | |---|---|---| | Required Production | $P_t = D_t + (I_t - I_{t-1})$ | Material & capacity planning | | Workforce Required | $W_t = \dfrac{P_t}{\text{Output per worker per period}}$ | Headcount calculation | | Hiring Required | $H_t = \max(0,\ W_t - W_{t-1})$ | If demand rises | | Firing Required | $F_t = \max(0,\ W_{t-1} - W_t)$ | If demand falls | | Net Change in Workforce | $\Delta W_t = W_t - W_{t-1} = H_t - F_t$ | Decision variable | | Total Cost | $TC = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left[ C_{RT} P_{RT,t} + C_{OT} P_{OT,t} + C_{Sub} S_t + C_H H_t + C_F F_t + C_{Inv} I_t + C_{BO} B_t \right]$ | Minimize over all $t$ | | Inventory Carryover | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ | Basic (no backorders) | ## 2.6 Methods of Aggregate Planning (KTU Most-Tested Topic) The KTU syllabus categorizes the techniques as: ### A. Informal / Graphical Methods - **Cumulative Graphical Plot:** Plot cumulative demand vs. cumulative production. Each unit the production line is *above* the demand line is inventory; *below* is backorder. - **Trial-and-error charting:** Manager sketches multiple scenarios. ### B. Mathematical / Optimization Methods - **Linear Programming (LP) / Mixed Integer Programming (MIP):** Formulate as a cost-minimization LP; solved by simplex. - **Transportation Method of LP:** Most popular KTU exam problem. Treats each period's supply option (regular time, overtime, subcontract) as a *source* and each period's demand as a *destination*; inventory & backorder cells connect consecutive periods. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Transportation Method** is the single most-asked AP technique in KTU ESE papers. Master it. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility In **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** environments (e.g., Toyota, Tata Steel, Bosch India), aggregate planning: - Feeds directly into **Heijunka** (production leveling) — a Toyota Production System pillar. - Aligns with **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** contracts. - Drives **Takt-time** calculations on the shop floor. - Is foundational for implementing **Lean** and **Theory of Constraints (TOC)** in capacity decisions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning The transportation method is essentially an **LP allocation problem** with a special *staircase* structure. ### Step 1 — Identify Sources (Supply Options) For each period $t$, the available supply options are: - **Regular time** capacity (e.g., $R_t$ units) — cost $= C_{RT}$ - **Overtime** capacity (e.g., $O_t$ units, usually $\le 25\%$ of $R_t$) — cost $= C_{OT}$ - **Subcontract** capacity (e.g., $S_t$ units) — cost $= C_{Sub}$ Each of these is a separate **source row** in the transportation tableau. ### Step 2 — Identify Destinations (Demand Periods) Each period $t$ has a forecast demand $D_t$, treated as a **destination column**. ### Step 3 — Add Inventory and Backorder "Dummy" Capacity A common trick: Add one extra **inventory-carry column** before period 1 (this represents stock already on hand $I_0$) and one **backorder-accumulation column** at the end. Then: - Cost of shipping from period $t$ to period $t+1$ (inventory) $= C_{Inv}$ per unit per period. - Cost of shipping from period $t$ to period $t-1$ (backorder) $= C_{BO}$ per unit per period. ### Step 4 — Set Up the Tableau The general cost cell from **source** $i$ to **destination** $j$ is $C_{ij}$, where: - If $i$ and $j$ are the *same* period: $C_{ij} = C_{RT}$, $C_{OT}$, or $C_{Sub}$ depending on source type. - If $i < j$ (carry inventory forward): $C_{ij} = (j - i) \cdot C_{Inv}$. - If $i > j$ (back-order): $C_{ij} = (i - j) \cdot C_{BO}$. ### Step 3.1: Balance the Tableau Let: - Total Supply $\Sigma_i S_i = \sum_t (R_t + O_t + S_t^{cap}) + I_0$ - Total Demand $\Sigma_j D_j = \sum_t D_t$ **If Supply > Demand:** Add a *dummy demand* column with zero cost to absorb slack. **If Supply < Demand:** Add a *dummy supply* row with penalty cost (e.g., $C_{BO}$) for unmet demand. ### Step 5 — Solve Using Standard Transportation Algorithms Three classic algorithms (in increasing exam-friendliness): 1. **Northwest Corner Rule** (initial feasible solution). 2. **Least Cost Method** (better initial solution). 3. **Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** (best initial solution — most commonly used in KTU). 4. **MODI Method / Stepping Stone** (optimality test). ### Worked Analytical Derivation: Linear Programming Formulation Minimize total cost $Z$: $$ \begin{aligned} Z = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \Big[ & C_{RT} P_{RT,t} + C_{OT} P_{OT,t} + C_{Sub} S_t + C_H H_t + C_F F_t \\ & + C_{Inv} I_t + C_{BO} B_t \Big] \end{aligned} $$ Subject to the following constraints (for each period $t$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(1) Production: } & P_{RT,t} + P_{OT,t} + S_t + B_{t-1} - B_t + I_{t-1} - I_t = D_t \\[4pt] \text{(2) Workforce: } & W_t - W_{t-1} = H_t - F_t \\[4pt] \text{(3) Capacity: } & P_{RT,t} \le R_t^{\max} \quad ; \quad P_{OT,t} \le O_t^{\max} \\[4pt] \text{(4) Non-negativity: } & P_{RT,t},\, P_{OT,t},\, S_t,\, H_t,\, F_t,\, I_t,\, B_t,\, W_t \ge 0 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trick:** The LP formulation is theoretical, but the **Transportation Method is what KTU numerical questions test**. Always show your tableau clearly. ### 3.2 Aggregate Planning Numerical Worked Example (Level Strategy) **Given:** - Demand (units) across 4 periods: $D_1 = 200,\ D_2 = 300,\ D_3 = 400,\ D_4 = 300$. Total $= 1200$. - Initial Inventory $I_0 = 0$, Final Inventory $I_4 = 0$. - Regular production cost: $C_{RT} = \text{₹}\,20$/unit. - Inventory holding cost: $C_{Inv} = \text{₹}\,5$/unit/period. - Hiring cost: $C_H = \text{₹}\,100$/worker, Firing cost $C_F = \text{₹}\,200$/worker. - Each worker produces 10 units/period. Initial workforce $W_0 = 25$. **Level Strategy Solution:** Total demand $= 1200$ units, over 4 periods. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average production per period } \bar{P} &= \frac{1200}{4} = 300 \text{ units/period} \\[4pt] \text{Constant workforce } \bar{W} &= \frac{300}{10} = 30 \text{ workers} \end{aligned} $$ Hiring needed: $\Delta W = 30 - 25 = 5$ workers → Hiring cost $= 5 \times 100 = \text{₹}\,500$. Inventory by period (Production = 300 each period, Demand = $D_t$): $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= I_0 + P_1 - D_1 = 0 + 300 - 200 = 100 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_2 &= I_1 + P_2 - D_2 = 100 + 300 - 300 = 100 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_3 &= I_2 + P_3 - D_3 = 100 + 300 - 400 = 0 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_4 &= I_3 + P_4 - D_4 = 0 + 300 - 300 = 0 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ Average inventory over the horizon: $$ \bar{I} = \frac{100 + 100 + 0 + 0}{4} = 50 \text{ units} $$ Total cost (Level Strategy): $$ \begin{aligned} TC_{\text{Level}} &= (C_{RT} \cdot \sum P_t) + (C_H \cdot H) + (C_{Inv} \cdot \bar{I}) \\[4pt] &= (20 \times 1200) + (100 \times 5) + (5 \times 50) \\[4pt] &= 24{,}000 + 500 + 250 = \text{₹}\,24{,}750 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Comparison Matrix (Level vs. Chase — The Standard KTU Comparison) | Cost Component | Level Strategy | Chase Strategy | |---|---|---| | Production Cost $(C_{RT} \times \text{units})$ | ₹ 24,000 | ₹ 24,000 | | Hiring Cost | ₹ 500 (5 hires) | High (frequent hires/fires) | | Firing Cost | ₹ 0 | High (workers let go) | | Inventory Holding Cost | ₹ 250 (avg 50 units) | ₹ 0 | | Backorder Cost | ₹ 0 | ₹ 0 | | **Total Cost** | **₹ 24,750** | **Variable (often higher)** | > [!TIP] > **Rule of thumb:** If $C_{Inv}$ is **low** and $C_H, C_F$ are **high** → use **Level Strategy**. If $C_{Inv}$ is **high** and $C_H, C_F$ are **low** → use **Chase Strategy**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Planning in WCM ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Long Range Plan
