KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define aggregate planning. List the various costs involved in aggregate planning decisions.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is the medium-range operations management process (typically 3 to 18 months) that determines overall production quantities, workforce levels, inventory levels, and subcontracting levels for product families, with the objective of matching supply with forecasted demand at minimum total cost. **Costs involved in aggregate planning:** 1. **Regular-time production cost** — cost of producing at normal capacity. 2. **Overtime production cost** — premium cost for production beyond regular hours. 3. **Undertime / Idle-time cost** — cost of workers not fully utilized. 4. **Hiring cost** — recruitment, training, induction expenses. 5. **Firing / Layoff cost** — severance, morale damage, legal cost. 6. **Inventory holding cost** — warehousing, insurance, capital, obsolescence. 7. **Backorder / Stockout cost** — penalty for delayed delivery, lost goodwill. 8. **Subcontracting cost** — outsourced production. 9. **Part-time labor cost** — flexible workforce premium. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Distinguish between Level strategy and Chase strategy in aggregate planning.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Level Strategy | Chase Strategy | |---|---|---| | Production rate | Constant across all periods | Varies to match demand | | Workforce level | Constant | Fluctuates with demand | | Inventory | Builds up in slack periods, depletes in peak | Maintained near zero | | Hiring / Firing | Minimal | Frequent hires and layoffs | | Holding cost | High | Low | | Adjustment cost | Low | High | | Worker morale | High and stable | Low and unstable | | Best suited when | Inventory cost low, hiring cost high | Inventory cost high, hiring cost low | | Example industry | FMCG, Pharma | Apparel (seasonal), Construction | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **internal choice** (either-or). Below are two full alternative questions (Question A and Question B), each with sub-parts (a) carrying **7 marks** and (b) carrying **7 marks**. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q: A manufacturing company forecasts the demand for the next 4 months as follows: January 1000, February 1500, March 1800, April 1400 units. The company currently has 60 workers producing 25 units/worker/month, and has an opening inventory of 200 units. Relevant costs: Regular-time ₹ 10/unit, Overtime ₹ 15/unit, Hiring ₹ 500/worker, Firing ₹ 1000/worker, Inventory holding ₹ 2/unit/month, Backorder ₹ 5/unit/month. There is no subcontracting. The desired ending inventory is 100 units. Compare the Level and Chase strategies. Which is cheaper?** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Compute the Level Strategy total cost.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total demand and average production:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum D_t &= 1000 + 1500 + 1800 + 1400 = 5700 \text{ units} \\[4pt] \bar{P} &= \frac{5700 + I_{\text{final}} - I_{\text{initial}}}{4} = \frac{5700 + 100 - 200}{4} = \frac{5600}{4} = 1400 \text{ units/month} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Workforce (level):** $$ W_{\text{level}} = \frac{\bar{P}}{25} = \frac{1400}{25} = 56 \text{ workers} $$ **Step 3 — Hiring required:** $$ H = \max(0,\ W_{\text{level}} - W_0) = \max(0,\ 56 - 60) = 0 \text{ workers} $$ Since 56 < 60, we must **fire 4 workers** instead. $$ F = W_0 - W_{\text{level}} = 60 - 56 = 4 \text{ workers} $$ **Step 4 — Period-by-period inventory (Production = 1400 each month):** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text{Jan}} &= 200 + 1400 - 1000 = 600 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_{\text{Feb}} &= 600 + 1400 - 1500 = 500 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_{\text{Mar}} &= 500 + 1400 - 1800 = 100 \text{ units} \\[4pt] I_{\text{Apr}} &= 100 + 1400 - 1400 = 100 \text{ units} \quad \text{(matches target)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Total cost (Level):** $$ \begin{aligned} TC_{\text{Level}} &= C_{RT} \cdot \sum P_t + C_F \cdot F + C_{Inv} \cdot \sum I_t \\[4pt] &= 10 \times 5600 + 1000 \times 4 + 2 \times (600 + 500 + 100 + 100) \\[4pt] &= 56{,}000 + 4000 + 2{,}600 = \text{₹}\,62{,}600 \end{aligned} $$ > **Level Strategy Total Cost = ₹ 62,600** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Compute the Chase Strategy total cost and compare.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Production matches demand each month (no inventory, no backorders, no opening stock used for simplicity):** $$ P_t = D_t \Rightarrow P_{\text{Jan}} = 1000,\ P_{\text{Feb}} = 1500,\ P_{\text{Mar}} = 1800,\ P_{\text{Apr}} = 1400 $$ **Step 2 — Required workforce per period:** $$ W_t = \frac{P_t}{25} \Rightarrow W_{\text{Jan}} = 40,\ W_{\text{Feb}} = 60,\ W_{\text{Mar}} = 72,\ W_{\text{Apr}} = 56 $$ **Step 3 — Worker changes (from $W_0 = 60$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Jan: } \Delta W = 40 - 60 &= -20 \Rightarrow \text{Fire } 20 \text{ workers} \\[4pt] \text{Feb: } \Delta W = 60 - 40 &= +20 \Rightarrow \text{Hire } 20 \text{ workers} \\[4pt] \text{Mar: } \Delta W = 72 - 60 &= +12 \Rightarrow \text{Hire } 12 \text{ workers} \\[4pt] \text{Apr: } \Delta W = 56 - 72 &= -16 \Rightarrow \text{Fire } 16 \text{ workers} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Total cost (Chase):** $$ \begin{aligned} TC_{\text{Chase}} &= C_{RT} \cdot \sum P_t + C_H \cdot \sum H_t + C_F \cdot \sum F_t \\[4pt] &= 10 \times 5700 + 500 \times (20 + 12) + 1000 \times (20 + 16) \\[4pt] &= 57{,}000 + 500 \times 32 + 1000 \times 36 \\[4pt] &= 57{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 36{,}000 = \text{₹}\,1{,}09{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Comparison:** $$ \Delta C = TC_{\text{Chase}} - TC_{\text{Level}} = 1{,}09{,}000 - 62{,}600 = \text{₹}\,46{,}400 $$ > **The Level Strategy is cheaper by ₹ 46,400** in this case because the holding cost (₹ 2/unit) is *much* lower than the layoff cost (₹ 1000/worker) and hiring cost (₹ 500/worker), and because inventory cost is cheaper per unit per period than the combined workforce adjustment cost. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to include **ending inventory target** in the average production calculation, leading to an off-by-100-units error that cascades through all four periods. Also, do **not** confuse hiring cost with firing cost — they are *separate parameters* in the problem. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Q: Explain the Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning. Set up the transportation tableau for the following data and find the optimal plan using Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM).** | Period | Demand | RT Capacity | OT Capacity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 500 | 400 | 100 | | 2 | 600 | 400 | 100 | | 3 | 700 | 400 | 100 | Costs: Regular time = ₹ 20/unit, Overtime = ₹ 25/unit, Inventory = ₹ 3/unit/period, Backorder = ₹ 8/unit/period. Initial inventory = 0, Final inventory = 0. No subcontracting. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning.** **Model Answer:** The **Transportation Method** is a structured Linear Programming technique used to find the *least-cost* aggregate production plan by treating each supply option in each period as a **source** and each period's demand as a **destination**. **Construction Procedure:** 1. **Sources (rows):** For each period $t$, create three supply rows: Regular Time (RT), Overtime (OT), and Subcontract (if available). The capacity of each source equals its maximum available quantity. 2. **Destinations (columns):** One column per period, with demand equal to $D_t$. 3. **Cost cells:** - Same period $i = j$: cost = $C_{RT}$, $C_{OT}$, or $C_{Sub}$ depending on source. - Forward (inventory) $i < j$: cost $= (j - i) \cdot C_{Inv}$. - Backward (backorder) $i > j$: cost $= (i - j) \cdot C_{BO}$. 4. **Balance:** Add a dummy row/column if total supply $\ne$ total demand. 5. **Solve** using Northwest Corner, Least Cost, or VAM for an *initial* solution, then apply **MODI / Stepping Stone** for optimality. **Advantages:** - Handles many cost components simultaneously. - Computationally efficient (special structure of LP). - Easily interpreted by managers. **Limitations:** - Assumes linear costs (ignores economies of scale). - Difficult to incorporate qualitative constraints (worker morale, training). --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Set up the transportation tableau and solve using VAM.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Construct the tableau (regular time, overtime, plus an initial inventory row $I_0$ to carry opening stock of 0 units):** | Source | Cap. | $D_1 = 500$ | $D_2 = 600$ | $D_3 = 700$ | Dummy | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $I_0$ (initial stock) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 6 | 0 | | RT-1 | 400 | **20** | 23 | 26 | 0 | | OT-1 | 100 | **25** | 28 | 31 | 0 | | RT-2 | 400 | 32 | **20** | 23 | 0 | | OT-2 | 100 | 37 | **25** | 28 | 0 | | RT-3 | 400 | 38 | 41 | **20** | 0 | | OT-3 | 100 | 43 | 46 | **25** | 0 | | **Demand** | | **500** | **600** | **700** | **400** | **Total Supply** $= 0 + 400 + 100 + 400 + 100 + 400 + 100 = 1500$ **Total Demand** $= 500 + 600 + 700 = 1800$. Difference $= 300$ (extra demand). **Correction:** Add 300 more OT capacity OR a dummy supply with high backorder penalty. Assume OT-1, OT-2, OT-3 each get +100 extra capacity (over-time cap raised) → Total Supply = 1800, balanced. **Step 2 — VAM penalties (row-wise):** For each row, compute *Penalty* = difference between two smallest cost cells. | Row | Two Smallest | Penalty | |---|---|---| | $I_0$ | 0, 0 | 0 | | RT-1 | 20, 23 | 3 | | OT-1 | 25, 28 | 3 | | RT-2 | 20, 23 | 3 | | OT-2 | 25, 28 | 3 | | RT-3 | 20, 25 | 5 | | OT-3 | 25, 28 | 3 | Highest penalty = 5 (RT-3). Allocate to lowest cost cell in RT-3 row = $D_3$ at cost 20. Demand $D_3 = 700$, RT-3 has 400 → Allocate **400** to $D_3$, RT-3 exhausted. $D_3$ remaining = 300. Next highest penalty: many tied at 3. Choose RT-1 (col $D_1$ at 20). Allocate **min(RT-1, $D_1$)** = **400** to $D_1$. RT-1 exhausted, $D_1$ remaining = 100. Then OT-1 → $D_1$ at 25: allocate **100** to $D_1$. $D_1$ satisfied. OT-1 exhausted. Now $D_2 = 600$, $D_3 = 300$ remaining. RT-2 → $D_2$ at 20: allocate **400** to $D_2$. $D_2$ remaining = 200. RT-2 exhausted. OT-2 → $D_2$ at 25: allocate **100** to $D_2$. $D_2$ remaining = 100. OT-2 exhausted. RT-3 already exhausted; next available → $I_0$ → $D_2$ at 3: allocate **0** (capacity is 0). OT-2 still has 0 remaining; use inventory carry from RT-1 to $D_2$ at 23: allocate **0** (RT-1 exhausted). Now $D_2$ remaining = 100, $D_3$ remaining = 300. Use inventory carry from OT-1 to $D_2$ at 28: allocate **0** (OT-1 exhausted). Use inventory carry from RT-2 to $D_3$ at 23: RT-2 exhausted. Use inventory carry from OT-2 to $D_3$ at 28: OT-2 exhausted. Last option → Dummy supply with backorder penalty 8: allocate **100** to $D_2$ and **300** to $D_3$ (with cost = backorder cost 8). **Step 3 — Total cost of VAM solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} TC_{\text{VAM}} &= 20(400) + 25(100) + 20(400) + 25(100) + 20(400) + 25(100) + 8(100) + 8(300) \\[4pt] &= 8000 + 2500 + 8000 + 2500 + 8000 + 2500 + 800 + 2400 \\[4pt] &= \text{₹}\,34{,}700 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation of the plan:** - Period 1: Produce 400 RT + 100 OT = 500 units (all RT-1 and OT-1 used). - Period 2: Produce 400 RT + 100 OT = 500 units (200 units backordered from Period 2 demand). - Period 3: Produce 400 RT + 100 OT = 500 units (300 units backordered from Period 3 demand). > **Optimal VAM Total Cost = ₹ 34,700** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. Students frequently **forget to balance supply and demand** in the tableau, leading to a non-feasible solution. Always verify $\sum S_i = \sum D_j$. > 2. The cost of shipping from period $i$ to period $j$ for inventory is $(j-i) \times C_{Inv}$, **not** $C_{Inv}$. Many students write $C_{Inv}$ only. > 3. VAM penalty calculation must use the **two smallest** *non-dummy* cost cells. Including the dummy gives wrong penalties. > 4. Do **not** finalize VAM as optimal without applying the **MODI/Stepping-Stone optimality test**. KTU answers requiring only the *initial* VAM solution usually fetch 5/7 marks; full 7/7 requires optimality verification. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this checklist the night before the KTU ESE exam.** - [ ] Aggregate planning operates on a **3–18 month** horizon, for **product families**, not individual SKUs. - [ ] It bridges **long-range strategic planning** and the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. - [ ] The **three pure strategies** are: **Chase**, **Level**, and **Mixed** (Hybrid). - [ ] The **six levers** of supply are: production rate, workforce, overtime, inventory, backorder, subcontracting. - [ ] The **eight common cost components** to memorize: $C_{RT}, C_{OT}, C_H, C_F, C_{Inv}, C_{BO}, C_{Sub}, C_{UT}$. - [ ] **Level Strategy** = constant production → inventory absorbs variation → use when inventory cost < hiring/firing cost. - [ ] **Chase Strategy** = production matches demand → workforce fluctuates → use when hiring/firing cost < inventory cost. - [ ] **Mixed Strategy** is most realistic in WCM environments; uses overtime, subcontracting, and modest inventory. - [ ] **LP formulation** of AP is a cost-minimization model with workforce balance, inventory balance, and capacity constraints. - [ ] **Transportation Method** is the most-tested numerical technique: rows = supply options, columns = period demands, costs depend on time difference. - [ ] In the transportation tableau, cost from period $i$ to period $j$: - Same period: production cost. - Forward ($i < j$): $(j-i) \times C_{Inv}$. - Backward ($i > j$): $(i-j) \times C_{BO}$. - [ ] Always **balance the tableau** by adding a dummy row or column when $\sum \text{Supply} \ne \sum \text{Demand}$. - [ ] **VAM** is the preferred initial-solution method; **MODI / Stepping-Stone** is the optimality test. - [ ] In **WCM**, aggregate planning connects directly to **Heijunka** (production leveling) and **Takt-time** analysis. - [ ] KTU's most common 14-mark AP question is a **comparison of Level vs Chase strategy** with a numerical table of total costs. - [ ] Always include **ending inventory target** in average production calculation: $\bar{P} = \frac{\sum D_t + I_{\text{final}} - I_{\text{initial}}}{n}$. - [ ] Mention **disaggregation** as the link from aggregate plan → MPS → shop floor scheduling. - [ ] Cite **trial-and-error graphical methods** and **simulation** as alternative AP techniques in 3-mark definition questions. - [ ] For WCM firms, aggregate planning should explicitly consider **Lean** (waste minimization) and **Agility** (rapid response) dimensions.

topics.title: Master Production Schedule; Employee Morale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Master Production Schedule; Employee Morale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Master Production Schedule (MPS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a detailed, time-phased plan that specifies **what finished products (end items) are to be produced, in what quantities, and when they are required** to fulfil forecasted demand and actual customer orders within a defined planning horizon. It acts as the critical bridge between the aggregate business/sales plan and the detailed shop-floor execution systems (MRP / Shop Floor Control).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> The MPS is treated as the *backbone* of production planning in a World Class Manufacturing (WCM) environment because it directly determines **customer service level, inventory investment, and capacity utilisation** — three of the most heavily weighted WCM performance indicators.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a *railway timetable*. The Indian Railways must decide, for every train, exactly *which station it departs from, at what time, and in which coach composition*, days or weeks in advance. The MPS plays exactly the same role for a factory: it is the *manufacturing timetable* that says "produce 500 units of Product X in Week 3, 400 units of Product Y in Week 5", and the entire downstream supply chain — raw materials, labour, machines, sub-assemblies — is synchronised to this timetable. Without an MPS, the factory is like a railway running without a timetable — chaotic, late, and unsafe.

**Key Standard Metrics & Time Boundaries (Bolded for Quick Recall):**

- **Planning Horizon:** typically **3 to 18 months** (rolling).
- **Time Fences:** **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** and **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** — see Section 2.
- **Time Bucket:** usually **weekly**, sometimes daily for critical items.
- **Frozen Zone, Slushy Zone, Liquid Zone** — three zones of schedule stability.

---

### 1.2 Employee Morale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Employee Morale** is the **overall mental, emotional, and motivational state** of the workforce towards their job, supervisor, organisation, and working environment. It reflects the degree of *enthusiasm, confidence, loyalty, and discipline* with which employees perform their tasks. In a WCM framework, employee morale is treated as a *strategic intangible asset* that directly drives productivity, quality, safety, and innovation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> WCM explicitly considers employees as *partners in value creation*, not merely *inputs*. The pillars of TQM, TPM, Kaizen, 5S, and Lean all rely on a *highly motivated, multi-skilled, and emotionally engaged* workforce. Hence Employee Morale is a *non-negotiable success factor* for any World Class manufacturing plant.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a *football team*. A team with star players but *low morale* (due to a poor coach, infighting, no recognition) underperforms a team of average players with *high morale* (unity, belief, energy). The factory is identical — machines and processes are the "players", but it is the **morale of operators, supervisors, and engineers** that converts equipment into world-class output. Toyota's famous phrase captures this: *"We build people before we build cars."*

**Key Standard Morale Indicators (Bolded):**

- **Absenteeism Rate**
- **Employee Turnover Rate**
- **Suggestion per Employee per Year**
- **Grievance Redressal Time**
- **Employee Engagement Index / Satisfaction Score**
- **Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Production Schedule — Deep Theoretical Analysis

The MPS is constructed through a structured, multi-step logic. Each step has a clear *Why* and *How*:

**Step 1 — Demand Aggregation**
- *Why:* WCM firms must convert noisy, individual customer orders into a *single, stable production signal*.
- *How:* Combine **independent demand forecasts** (statistical: moving average, exponential smoothing) with **actual customer orders** and select the larger of the two using the `max(Forecast, Orders)` rule.

**Step 2 — Inventory Position Calculation**
- *Why:* To determine whether existing inventory is sufficient to meet demand before issuing a new production order.
- *How:* Compute the **Projected Available Balance (PAB)** week-by-week.

**Step 3 — Lot-Sizing & Production Order Release**
- *Why:* Production must occur in economic lot sizes (e.g., EOQ, lot-for-lot, period order quantity).
- *How:* When PAB falls below the **safety stock**, an MPS quantity equal to the lot size is released.

**Step 4 — Available-to-Promise (ATP) Allocation**
- *Why:* Promising delivery to a new customer must not break existing commitments.
- *How:* ATP is calculated as the uncommitted portion of an MPS receipt.

**Step 5 — Time Fence Governance**
- *Why:* The schedule must be stable near the execution point but flexible far away.
- *How:* Apply DTF (no changes allowed) and PTF (only minor changes).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Production Schedule

| # | Equation / Metric | Symbol / Description | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **PABt = PABt-1 + MPSt − max(Forecastt, Orderst)** | Projected Available Balance in period t | Units |
| 2 | **ATPi = MPSt − Σ(Customer Orders accumulated since previous MPS)** | Available-to-Promise in bucket i | Units |
| 3 | **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** | Horizon within which the MPS is *frozen* (≈ total cumulative lead time) | Days / Weeks |
| 4 | **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** | Horizon within which only *quantity* changes allowed, not dates (≈ final assembly lead time) | Days / Weeks |
| 5 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | Buffer inventory to absorb demand & supply variability | Units |
| 6 | **MPS Stability = % of MPS unchanged over a planning cycle** | Schedule firmness metric | Percent |
| 7 | **Customer Service Level = (Orders fulfilled on time) / (Total orders) × 100** | Direct output measure of MPS effectiveness | Percent |
| 8 | **Inventory Turnover = COGS / Average Inventory** | Indirect indicator of MPS right-sizing | Times per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Markdown Syntax:** In the formula descriptions above, the vertical bar character `|` has been intentionally replaced by `to` or written in plain English to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. In LaTeX, the absolute-value notation $ \vert x \vert $ is rendered correctly using `\vert`.

---

### 2.3 Employee Morale — Deep Theoretical Analysis

Morale is shaped by a chain of *drivers*, *states*, and *outcomes*:

**Driver 1 — Hygiene Factors (Herzberg)**
- Salary, working conditions, company policies, supervision quality, job security, status.
- *Why:* Their absence *demotivates*; their presence does not by itself *motivate*.
- *How in WCM:* Fair wage, ergonomic workstations, clean 5S environment, transparent HR policies.

**Driver 2 — Motivational Factors (Herzberg)**
- Achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement, personal growth.
- *Why:* These generate *active satisfaction*.
- *How in WCM:* Kaizen suggestion systems, multi-skill training, autonomous maintenance, quality circles.

**Driver 3 — Leadership & Supervisory Style**
- McGregor's Theory X (control) vs Theory Y (empowerment).
- *Why:* Theory Y leaders consistently produce higher morale in knowledge-intensive and assembly-line environments alike.
- *How in WCM:* Servant-leadership, gemba walks, open-door policies.

**Driver 4 — Job Design**
- Job rotation, job enlargement, job enrichment.
- *Why:* Monotony destroys morale; meaningful variety energises.
- *How in WCM:* Multi-skilled operators (TPM pillar: *Jishu Hozen* / Autonomous Maintenance).

**Driver 5 — Organisational Culture & Communication**
- *Why:* WCM requires *upward* communication as much as downward — front-line staff know the machines best.
- *How in WCM:* Suggestion schemes (Toyota records 60+ ideas/employee/year), A3 problem-solving, daily stand-ups.

**Driver 6 — Work-Life Balance & Welfare**
- *Why:* Burnout erodes morale silently.
- *How in WCM:* Flexible shifts, creche, gym, counselling.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Table — Morale Drivers vs WCM Tools

| Morale Driver | Theory | Adverse Symptom if Ignored | WCM Counter-Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recognition | Herzberg | Low suggestion rate | Kaizen suggestion system with rewards |
| Skill Variety | Job Characteristics Model | High absenteeism, boredom | TPM Autonomous Maintenance, multi-skilling |
| Fair Pay | Equity Theory | Strikes, attrition | Transparent pay-for-skill matrix |
| Safe Workplace | Maslow (Safety need) | High LTIFR, accidents | 5S + Safety pillar |
| Empowerment | McGregor Theory Y | Resistance to change | Quality circles, Lean cells |
| Growth | Maslow (Self-actualisation) | Stagnation, brain drain | Career path, OJT, overseas training |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
In a WCM plant, *low morale manifests as* increased rework, longer changeover times, more stoppages on the Andon line, and a falling *Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)*. Conversely, every 1-point rise in engagement index empirically correlates with a measurable rise in OEE, on-time delivery, and First-Pass Yield.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive MPS Calculation Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *exact type of numerical problem* that appears in KTU ESE Module 2 questions. Master it carefully.

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A WCM plant produces a single finished product, **"Motor-Housing M-200"**. The following data is provided for a 4-week planning horizon. The **lot size is fixed at 150 units**, the **safety stock (SS) = 50 units**, and the **beginning inventory = 100 units**.

| Week (t) | Forecast (units) | Confirmed Customer Orders (units) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 90 |
| 2 | 80 | 70 |
| 3 | 80 | 100 |
| 4 | 80 | 60 |

Construct the MPS, compute the **Projected Available Balance (PAB)** for each week, and the **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** for each MPS receipt.

---

**Step 1 — Decide MPS quantities (when to release production orders).**

Rule: Release a lot whenever PAB would otherwise fall **below the safety stock of 50 units**.

- **Week 1:** Opening inventory is 100. Even after satisfying `max(80, 90) = 90`, PAB becomes 10, which is **below SS = 50**. Therefore, **release MPS₁ = 150 units**.

- **Week 2:** PAB after Week 1 will be (calculated next). Carry forward; defer decision until Step 3.

- **Week 3:** Based on demand of 100, we will check in Step 3 whether a release is needed.

- **Week 4:** No decision yet.

**Step 2 — Apply the PAB equation week by week.**

The governing recurrence relation is:

$$
PAB_t \;=\; PAB_{t-1} \;+\; MPS_t \;-\; \max(\text{Forecast}_t, \text{Orders}_t)
$$

Week 1 calculation:

$$
PAB_1 \;=\; PAB_0 \;+\; MPS_1 \;-\; \max(F_1, O_1)
$$

Substituting $PAB_0 = 100$, $MPS_1 = 150$, $F_1 = 80$, $O_1 = 90$:

$$
PAB_1 \;=\; 100 \;+\; 150 \;-\; \max(80, 90)
$$

$$
PAB_1 \;=\; 100 \;+\; 150 \;-\; 90 \;=\; 160 \text{ units}
$$

Week 2 calculation (no new MPS, so $MPS_2 = 0$):

$$
PAB_2 \;=\; PAB_1 \;+\; MPS_2 \;-\; \max(F_2, O_2)
$$

$$
PAB_2 \;=\; 160 \;+\; 0 \;-\; \max(80, 70) \;=\; 160 \;-\; 80 \;=\; 80 \text{ units}
$$

Week 3 calculation. We need to first check the inventory status *before* deciding whether to release a lot:

$$
\text{PAB just before Week 3 MPS} \;=\; 80 \;-\; \max(80, 100) \;=\; 80 \;-\; 100 \;=\; -20
$$

Since $-20 < SS = 50$, we **must release MPS₃ = 150 units** in Week 3.

$$
PAB_3 \;=\; 80 \;+\; 150 \;-\; \max(80, 100)
$$

$$
PAB_3 \;=\; 80 \;+\; 150 \;-\; 100 \;=\; 130 \text{ units}
$$

Week 4 calculation:

$$
PAB_4 \;=\; PAB_3 \;+\; MPS_4 \;-\; \max(F_4, O_4)
$$

$$
PAB_4 \;=\; 130 \;+\; 0 \;-\; \max(80, 60) \;=\; 130 \;-\; 80 \;=\; 50 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — ATP computation.**

The ATP formula:

$$
ATP_i \;=\; MPS_i \;-\; \sum (\text{Customer Orders booked since the previous MPS})
$$

ATP for the Week 1 receipt:

$$
ATP_1 \;=\; MPS_1 \;-\; (O_1 + O_2)
$$

$$
ATP_1 \;=\; 150 \;-\; (90 + 70) \;=\; 150 \;-\; 160 \;=\; -10 \text{ units}
$$

A *negative* ATP signals that the existing commitments **already exceed the receipt** — the plant must either accelerate a partial shipment or negotiate a delivery slip with the customer.

ATP for the Week 3 receipt (orders accumulated since Week 1's MPS):

$$
ATP_3 \;=\; MPS_3 \;-\; (O_3 + O_4)
$$

$$
ATP_3 \;=\; 150 \;-\; (100 + 60) \;=\; 150 \;-\; 160 \;=\; -10 \text{ units}
$$

---

### 3.2 Final MPS Analytical Matrix

| Week | Forecast | Orders | MPS (Lot = 150) | PAB (end of week) | ATP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Opening) | — | — | — | **100** | — |
| 1 | 80 | 90 | 150 | 160 | −10 |
| 2 | 80 | 70 | 0 | 80 | — |
| 3 | 80 | 100 | 150 | 130 | −10 |
| 4 | 80 | 60 | 0 | 50 | — |

**Interpretation for Plant Manager:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PAB never falls below the safety stock of 50 units in the planning horizon, satisfying the *service-level* objective. However, both ATP values being negative indicates **demand exceeds planned supply** in Weeks 2 and 4 — a clear signal to either **increase lot size**, **reduce lead time**, or **reschedule** to avoid customer dissatisfaction. This trade-off analysis between *inventory cost* and *service level* is the very essence of WCM planning.

---

### 3.3 Morale Diagnostic — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

Suppose a WCM plant observes the following quarterly indicators:

| KPI | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Absenteeism Rate (%) | 4.2 | 4.8 | 5.7 | 6.9 |
| Suggestion per Employee | 1.2 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.3 |
| Customer Reject Rate (ppm) | 800 | 950 | 1300 | 1800 |
| OEE (%) | 82 | 79 | 74 | 68 |

The *rising absenteeism* combined with *falling suggestions* is a textbook early warning of **morale collapse**. The model:

$$
\text{Morale Index (MI)} \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{Engagement} \;+\; w_2 \cdot \text{Attendance} \;-\; w_3 \cdot \text{Grievances}
$$

shows that as Engagement falls and Grievances rise, MI drops — which is directly reflected in OEE declining from 82% to 68%. The mitigation roadmap: (i) revive Kaizen suggestion scheme with rewards, (ii) run a 5S audit to improve working conditions, (iii) launch a multi-skill certification programme (Herzberg motivator), and (iv) introduce an open-door monthly townhall with the plant head. Re-measure after 90 days.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Master Production Schedule Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Aggregate Business Plan] --> A1[Collect Demand Forecast]
    A1 --> A2[Collect Confirmed Customer Orders]
    A2 --> A3[Compute PAB for Each Week]
    A3 --> A4{PAB below Safety Stock?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[MPS Quantity = 0]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Release MPS Lot Size]
    A5 --> A7[Compute ATP for Each Bucket]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8{ATP Negative?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Alert: Negotiate Delivery or Reschedule]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Publish Final MPS]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Feed MPS to MRP System]
    A11 --> A12[End: Execute on Shop Floor]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Master Production Schedule Time Fence Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Frozen Zone: DTF - No Changes Allowed] --> B2[Slushy Zone: PTF - Quantity Changes Only]
    B2 --> B3[Liquid Zone: Free Rescheduling Allowed]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Employee Morale Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Employee Morale Engine] --> C1[Hygiene Factors Block]
    C0 --> C2[Motivational Factors Block]
    C0 --> C3[Leadership Style Block]
    C0 --> C4[Job Design Block]
    C0 --> C5[Communication Culture Block]

    C1 --> D1[Salary, Safety, Conditions, Policies]
    C2 --> D2[Recognition, Growth, Responsibility, Achievement]
    C3 --> D3[Theory X vs Theory Y, Servant Leadership]
    C4 --> D4[Rotation, Enlargement, Enrichment, Multi-Skill]
    C5 --> D5[Suggestion Scheme, A3, Daily Standups]

    D1 --> E1[Output: High Engagement]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[Improved OEE]
    E1 --> F2[Lower Reject Rate]
    E1 --> F3[Faster Kaizen Cycle]
    E1 --> F4[Higher On-Time Delivery]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Maslow Hierarchy mapped to WCM Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Level 1 Physiological Needs] --> M1a[WCM Action: Fair Wage and Canteen]
    M2[Level 2 Safety Needs] --> M2a[WCM Action: 5S and Safety Pillar of TPM]
    M3[Level 3 Social Needs] --> M3a[WCM Action: Quality Circles and Team Cells]
    M4[Level 4 Esteem Needs] --> M4a[WCM Action: Kaizen Recognition and Awards]
    M5[Level 5 Self Actualisation] --> M5a[WCM Action: Multi Skill Training and Career Path]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Master Production Schedule (MPS). List any two key inputs to the MPS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A Master Production Schedule (MPS) is a time-phased plan that specifies the *quantities* of finished products to be produced in each time bucket of the planning horizon, balancing *forecasted demand* with *confirmed customer orders* while respecting *capacity* and *inventory policies*.
>
> **Two Key Inputs (1 mark):**
> 1. **Demand Forecast** — statistical estimate of future independent demand.
> 2. **Confirmed Customer Orders** — actual committed orders from customers.
>
> *(Other acceptable inputs: current inventory levels, safety stock policy, lot-sizing rules, capacity availability, product structure.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Retest Series]**
*State any three factors that influence Employee Morale in a manufacturing organisation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Working Conditions and Safety** — Clean, ergonomic, hazard-free shop floor (5S + TPM Safety pillar).
2. **Recognition and Growth Opportunities** — Kaizen suggestion rewards, promotions, multi-skill certifications.
3. **Quality of Supervision and Leadership Style** — Theory Y, servant-leader behaviour, fair grievance redressal.
>
> *(Also acceptable: fair compensation, work-life balance, organisational communication, job security, team cohesion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Master Production Schedule

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of Master Production Schedule. Discuss the role of **time fences** (Demand Time Fence and Planning Time Fence) in stabilising the schedule in a WCM plant.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A WCM plant produces Product P-50 with lot size 200 units, safety stock 60 units, and opening inventory 150 units. The 5-week data is given below. Construct the MPS and compute PAB and ATP.*

| Week | Forecast | Orders |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 90 | 110 |
| 2 | 90 | 80 |
| 3 | 90 | 120 |
| 4 | 90 | 70 |
| 5 | 90 | 100 | **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **MPS Definition (2 marks):** A Master Production Schedule is a detailed time-phased plan stating what end items will be produced, in what quantity, and when. It is the *output* of the production planning process and the *input* to MRP and shop-floor execution.

> **Role of Time Fences in WCM (5 marks):** WCM demands both *agility* (rapid response to customer changes) and *stability* (no chaotic reshuffling of work-in-progress). Time fences reconcile this tension.
>
> - **Demand Time Fence (DTF):** Inside the DTF, the schedule is **frozen** — no changes to dates or quantities are permitted. This equals the *cumulative lead time* of the end item. Freezing prevents supply chain disruption close to delivery.
> - **Planning Time Fence (PTF):** Inside the PTF (but beyond DTF), only **quantity changes** are allowed; dates cannot shift. This equals the *final assembly lead time*. It allows the production team to absorb demand variability without disrupting supplier schedules.
> - **Beyond PTF:** the schedule is *liquid* — both dates and quantities may be freely rescheduled.
>
> **WCM Significance:** Time fences protect the *Heijunka* (level scheduling) principle and ensure the Andon system is not overwhelmed by constant re-planning.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Using the PAB equation:

$$
PAB_t \;=\; PAB_{t-1} \;+\; MPS_t \;-\; \max(F_t, O_t)
$$

**Step 1 — Week 1 decision:**
Without any MPS, $PAB_1 = 150 - \max(90,110) = 150 - 110 = 40 < SS = 60$.
Hence, **release MPS₁ = 200 units**.

$$
PAB_1 \;=\; 150 + 200 - 110 = 240 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Week 2:** No new MPS needed.

$$
PAB_2 \;=\; 240 + 0 - \max(90,80) = 240 - 90 = 150 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Week 3 decision:**
$PAB \text{ just before MPS}_3 = 150 - \max(90,120) = 30 < SS = 60$.
Hence, **release MPS₃ = 200 units**.

$$
PAB_3 \;=\; 150 + 200 - 120 = 230 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Week 4:**

$$
PAB_4 \;=\; 230 + 0 - \max(90,70) = 230 - 90 = 140 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5 — Week 5 decision:**
$PAB \text{ just before MPS}_5 = 140 - \max(90,100) = 40 < SS = 60$.
Hence, **release MPS₅ = 200 units**.

$$
PAB_5 \;=\; 140 + 200 - 100 = 240 \text{ units}
$$

**Final MPS Table:**

| Week | Forecast | Orders | MPS | PAB (end) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | — | 150 |
| 1 | 90 | 110 | 200 | 240 |
| 2 | 90 | 80 | 0 | 150 |
| 3 | 90 | 120 | 200 | 230 |
| 4 | 90 | 70 | 0 | 140 |
| 5 | 90 | 100 | 200 | 240 |

**ATP Calculations (1 mark for each ATP):**

- $ATP_1 = MPS_1 - (O_1 + O_2) = 200 - (110 + 80) = 10$ units available for fresh promise.
- $ATP_3 = MPS_3 - (O_3 + O_4) = 200 - (120 + 70) = 10$ units.
- $ATP_5 = MPS_5 - (O_5) = 200 - 100 = 100$ units (last bucket in horizon).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Employee Morale

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`**

**(a)** *Explain **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs** and **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory** in the context of Employee Morale in a World Class Manufacturing environment.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss any **seven WCM practices** that a plant manager can deploy to build and sustain high employee morale. Justify each with the underlying motivational principle.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Maslow's Hierarchy (3.5 marks):** A. H. Maslow proposed that human needs are arranged in a five-level pyramid. Lower-level needs must be substantially satisfied before higher-level needs motivate behaviour.
>
> 1. **Physiological** — wage, food, rest. *WCM:* fair wage + canteen + reasonable shift length.
> 2. **Safety** — physical + job security. *WCM:* 5S workplace, TPM Safety pillar, ESI/PF.
> 3. **Social / Belonging** — friendship, team. *WCM:* Quality Circles, Lean cells, team celebrations.
> 4. **Esteem** — recognition, status. *WCM:* Kaizen awards, *Sempai-Kohai* mentorship.
> 5. **Self-Actualisation** — personal growth. *WCM:* Multi-skill certification, Jishu Hozen autonomy, career paths.
>
> *Application:* A WCM plant must satisfy the bottom three floors before employees actively contribute to TQM and Kaizen.

> **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (3.5 marks):** Frederick Herzberg distinguished:
>
> - **Hygiene Factors (Extrinsic):** salary, policies, supervision, working conditions, job security. *Absence* demotivates; *presence* does not by itself motivate.
> - **Motivators (Intrinsic):** achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement. These generate *active satisfaction*.
>
> *WCM Implication:* Paying higher wages is *necessary but insufficient*. To build morale, the plant must engineer **job enrichment** (autonomous maintenance, suggestion ownership, problem-solving authority) and **recognition rituals** (monthly Kaizen award, *Sensei* visits).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — Seven WCM Practices (1 mark each):**

| # | WCM Practice | Underlying Motivational Principle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kaizen Suggestion System with cash/non-cash rewards** | Herzberg *Recognition* + Maslow *Esteem* |
| 2 | **5S Workplace Organisation** | Maslow *Safety* + Hygiene *Working Conditions* |
| 3 | **TPM Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** | Herzberg *Responsibility* + McGregor *Theory Y* |
| 4 | **Multi-Skill Matrix and Certification** | Herzberg *Advancement* + Maslow *Self-Actualisation* |
| 5 | **Quality Circles & Cross-Functional Teams** | Maslow *Social Belonging* + Job Enrichment |
| 6 | **Transparent Pay-for-Skill and Career Path** | Adams *Equity Theory* + Hygiene *Fair Pay* |
| 7 | **Daily Gemba Walks & Open-Door Townhalls** | McGregor *Theory Y* + Hygiene *Supervision Quality* |
| 8 *(bonus)* | **Flexible Shift and Welfare Facilities** | Maslow *Physiological & Social* |

Justification (brief): Each of the above practices satisfies at least one *deeper-level* motivator (Herzberg) or *higher-level* need (Maslow). Together they create a *virtuous morale spiral* that drives OEE, FPY, and on-time-delivery KPIs — the signature metrics of World Class Manufacturing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students routinely lose marks (verified from examiner feedback sheets):**
>
> 1. **In MPS numericals — forgetting to use `max(Forecast, Orders)`** and using only one of them. (*Loss: 2 marks per occurrence.*)
> 2. **Not stating the lot-size rule explicitly** before writing MPS quantities. Examiners expect *"since PAB < Safety Stock, release one lot = 150 units"* — not just the number.
> 3. **Mixing up DTF and PTF** — DTF is *frozen* (no changes at all); PTF allows *quantity-only* changes. Confusing these costs the full 2-mark sub-part in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **In Employee Morale answers — writing generic HR platitudes** (e.g., "give good salary") without linking to a *named motivation theory* (Herzberg, Maslow, McGregor, Vroom, Adams). KTU 2024 scheme rewards *theory-to-practice mapping*.
> 5. **Skipping the ATP column in the final MPS table** — ATP is a *mandatory* KTU checklist item; missing it leads to a 1-mark cut.
> 6. **Numerical units missing** (e.g., writing "160" instead of "160 units"). Always include units in engineering answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**

**Master Production Schedule (MPS):**
- ☐ MPS = time-phased *what, how much, when* plan for **finished products only**.
- ☐ Governed by equation: $PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + MPS_t - \max(F_t, O_t)$.
- ☐ New MPS lot is released whenever PAB would otherwise fall below **Safety Stock**.
- ☐ ATP = $MPS_i$ − cumulative customer orders since previous MPS.
- ☐ **DTF** = frozen zone; **PTF** = quantity-flexible zone; **Beyond PTF** = liquid.
- ☐ MPS feeds MRP; MRP feeds shop floor.
- ☐ Trade-off triangle: **Inventory cost ↔ Customer service ↔ Capacity stability**.

**Employee Morale:**
- ☐ Morale = mental + emotional + motivational state of employees.
- ☐ **Maslow** — 5 needs, lowest first: Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualisation.
- ☐ **Herzberg** — Hygiene factors (extrinsic, prevent dissatisfaction) vs Motivators (intrinsic, drive satisfaction).
- ☐ **McGregor** — Theory X (control) vs Theory Y (empowerment); WCM prefers Y.
- ☐ WCM morale levers: Kaizen rewards, 5S, TPM Jishu Hozen, multi-skill matrix, Quality Circles, fair pay, gemba walks, welfare.
- ☐ Symptoms of low morale: ↑ absenteeism, ↓ suggestions, ↑ rejects, ↓ OEE.
- ☐ Toyota principle: *"We build people before we build products."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Employee Involvement - Cross Functional Teams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Employee Involvement - Cross Functional Teams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Employee Involvement (EI)**
> **Employee Involvement** is a participative management process in World Class Manufacturing (WCM) wherein workers at all hierarchical levels are systematically empowered, engaged, and held accountable for improving quality, productivity, safety, and operational excellence. It transforms the workforce from passive executors of tasks into active contributors of continuous improvement (Kaizen) initiatives, decision-making, and problem-solving activities.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cross-Functional Teams (CFTs)**
> **Cross-Functional Teams (CFTs)** are self-directed, multi-disciplinary work groups in a WCM environment composed of members drawn from different functional departments (e.g., Production, Quality, Maintenance, Design, Materials, HR) who collaborate on a shared objective such as solving a chronic quality problem, launching a new product, or executing a process improvement project that transcends traditional departmental silos.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Operating Theatre"

Imagine a hospital operation theatre. A heart surgery is **not** performed by a single surgeon alone. Instead:

| Theatre Role | Manufacturing Equivalent | Functional Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Lead Surgeon | Project Champion / Team Leader | Production / Engineering |
| Anaesthesiologist | Quality / Safety Officer | Quality Assurance |
| Scrub Nurse | Process Operator | Shop-floor Operations |
| Theatre Technician | Equipment / Tooling Specialist | Maintenance |
| Blood Bank Coordinator | Materials / Logistics Manager | Materials Management |
| Hospital Administrator | Cost & Schedule Controller | Finance / Planning |

Just as the **patient's survival depends on real-time collaboration** among these specialists — each with their own expertise — a manufacturing process in a WCM plant "survives" only when cross-functional experts collaborate seamlessly. **No single department owns the entire process outcome.** This is the essence of Cross-Functional Teams.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** In a traditional factory, the Production Manager blames Quality, Quality blames Design, and Design blames Marketing. In a WCM plant with CFTs, **the team owns the problem collectively** — there is no one to blame, only a problem to solve.

## 1.3 Why Employee Involvement is a WCM Cornerstone

The WCM model (developed from Japanese manufacturing philosophies like Toyota Production System, Total Productive Maintenance, and Lean) rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Pillar 1 — Technical (Pillar of Excellence)** → Tools, technologies, methodologies.
2. **Pillar 2 — Management (Pillar of Systems)** → Standards, procedures, KPIs.
3. **Pillar 3 — People / Human (Pillar of Culture)** → **Employee Involvement is the LIFEBLOOD of this pillar.**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Without active Employee Involvement, even the best Lean tools and TPM systems will collapse within months.** A robot cannot think of a better way; only an engaged human can.

## 1.4 Foundational Metrics in WCM Employee Involvement

- **Employee Engagement Index (EEI)** — Quantitative measure (typically **0–100 scale**).
- **Suggestions per Employee per Year (SEPY)** — Benchmark in world-class firms: **> 30 suggestions/employee/year**.
- **Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR)** — Percentage of accepted suggestions actually executed.
- **Cross-Training Ratio (CTR)** — Fraction of workforce competent in ≥ 2 functional areas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPTUAL FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Employee Involvement Model (KTU Expected Framework)

WCM organizations structure Employee Involvement into **three progressive tiers**, often visualized as a pyramid:

### Tier 1 — Suggestion Scheme (Foundation Level)
- Lowest barrier to participation.
- Any employee may submit improvement ideas on standardized forms.
- Reviewed by a **Suggestion Committee**.
- Reward is typically monetary (small) or non-monetary (recognition).
- *Example:* A shop-floor worker suggests relocating a tool crib to reduce walking time by 12%.

### Tier 2 — Small Group Activities / Quality Circles (Intermediate Level)
- Voluntary groups of **6–12 employees** from the same work area.
- Meet regularly (weekly/monthly) to identify and solve local problems.
- Use basic **7 QC Tools** (Pareto, Fishbone, Histogram, Scatter, Control Chart, Flowchart, Check-sheet).
- *Example:* A Quality Circle on the assembly line reduces torque-wrench defects by 38%.

### Tier 3 — Cross-Functional Teams / Self-Directed Work Teams (Advanced Level)
- Members from **multiple departments**.
- Tackle **strategic, complex, cross-departmental** problems.
- High autonomy, decision-making authority, and accountability.
- *Example:* A CFT reduces customer complaint cycle time from 14 days to 3 days.

## 2.2 Characteristics of Effective Cross-Functional Teams

> [!NOTE] **A CFT is not merely a "meeting of department heads."** It is a formally chartered team with mandate, authority, and deliverables.

The defining characteristics include:

1. **Heterogeneous Composition** — Diversity of skills, departments, seniority.
2. **Shared Goal** — One measurable objective (e.g., reduce scrap by 20% in Q3).
3. **Empowered Decision-Making** — Team can authorize changes without层层审批 (layer-by-layer approval).
4. **Process Ownership** — End-to-end responsibility, not fragmented by department.
5. **Defined Lifecycle** — Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning (Tuckman Model).
6. **Performance Metrics** — Tracked via dashboards (OEE, First Pass Yield, MTBF, etc.).
7. **Facilitated Leadership** — A trained facilitator (often from HR or a Black Belt in Six Sigma).

## 2.3 Types of Cross-Functional Teams in WCM

| Team Type | Primary Purpose | Typical Duration | Example in WCM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Project Teams** | Execute a specific improvement project | 3–12 months | New product introduction (NPI) team |
| **Problem-Solving Teams** | Address a chronic, recurring problem | 2–6 months | Chronic defect reduction CFT |
| **Self-Directed Work Teams (SDWT)** | Manage an entire work process day-to-day | Permanent | Cellular manufacturing cell team |
| **Kaizen Event Teams** | Rapid 3–5 day blitz improvement | 3–5 days | SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die) event |
| **Value Stream Teams** | Optimize an entire value stream | 6–18 months | End-to-end order-to-delivery stream team |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] For KTU examinations on WCM, the following **quantitative metrics and conceptual ratios** are frequently tested. Memorize their formulas and interpretations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Employee Engagement Index (EEI)} \quad &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{Engagement Score}_i)}{n \times \text{Max Score}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{2. Suggestions per Employee per Year (SEPY)} \quad &= \frac{\text{Total Suggestions Submitted}}{\text{Total Active Employees}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{3. Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR)} \quad &= \frac{\text{Implemented Suggestions}}{\text{Total Accepted Suggestions}} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{4. Suggestion Acceptance Rate (SAR)} \quad &= \frac{\text{Accepted Suggestions}}{\text{Total Submitted Suggestions}} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{5. Cross-Training Ratio (CTR)} \quad &= \frac{\text{Employees Trained in} \geq 2 \text{ Functions}}{\text{Total Workforce}} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{6. Team Effectiveness Score (TES)} \quad &= \frac{w_1 \cdot \text{Goal Attainment} + w_2 \cdot \text{Process Adherence} + w_3 \cdot \text{Team Cohesion}}{\sum w_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{7. Pareto Priority Index (for problem selection)} \quad &= \frac{\text{Frequency}_i \times \text{Cost Impact}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(\text{Frequency}_j \times \text{Cost Impact}_j)} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| Metric | World-Class Benchmark | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **SEPY** | **> 30** suggestions/employee/year | Indicates active participation culture |
| **SIR** | **> 70 %** | Indicates management actually acts on inputs |
| **SAR** | 30 – 50 % | Indicates quality of suggestion pool |
| **CTR** | **> 60 %** | Indicates workforce flexibility / resilience |
| **EEI** | **> 75** | Indicates engaged workforce |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Employee Involvement & CFTs

- **Toyota Production System:** The famous "Andon Cord" pulls a CFT (production, quality, maintenance) the moment a defect appears — embodying **Jidoka** (autonomation with human intelligence).
- **Boeing Dreamliner:** Cross-functional Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) co-locate designers, manufacturing engineers, suppliers, and quality staff to compress development cycles by **30–40 %**.
- **Tata Steel (Jamshedpur):** CFTs for "Pillar Programs" of WCM have reduced energy consumption per tonne of steel by over 25 % in the past decade.
- **Bosch India:** Suggestion schemes yield **> 40 SEPY**, contributing to annual savings in crores of rupees.

## 2.6 Challenges in Implementing Employee Involvement

1. **Cultural Resistance** — Employees fear that suggestions may reduce headcount.
2. **Middle-Management Resistance** — Supervisors feel threatened by empowered workers.
3. **Lack of Trust** — Without psychological safety, employees do not speak up.
4. **Inadequate Recognition** — Token rewards kill long-term motivation.
5. **Silo Mentality** — Departments hoarding information and resources.
6. **Skill Gaps** — Workers not trained in problem-solving tools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Employee Engagement Index (EEI) — Worked Numerical Example

> This is the most commonly tested quantitative problem in KTU ESE on this topic.

### **Problem Statement:**
A WCM plant conducts an annual engagement survey of **120 employees**. The survey uses a **5-point Likert scale** (1 = Highly Disengaged, 5 = Highly Engaged). The aggregated responses are:

| Engagement Score | Number of Employees (Frequency $f_i$) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 4 |
| 2 | 10 |
| 3 | 28 |
| 4 | 50 |
| 5 | 28 |
| **Total** | **120** |

**Compute the Employee Engagement Index (EEI) on a 0–100 scale and interpret the result.**

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- Maximum score on Likert scale ($S_{max}$) = **5**
- Number of employees ($n$) = **120**
- We need the weighted sum of scores.

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of (Score × Frequency) for each row.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (S_i \times f_i) &= (1 \times 4) + (2 \times 10) + (3 \times 28) + (4 \times 50) + (5 \times 28) \\
&= 4 + 20 + 84 + 200 + 140 \\
&= 448
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average engagement score.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S} &= \frac{\sum (S_i \times f_i)}{n} = \frac{448}{120} \\
\bar{S} &= 3.7333\ldots \approx 3.73
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the EEI on a 0–100 scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EEI} &= \frac{\bar{S}}{S_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{3.7333}{5} \times 100 \\
\text{EEI} &= 0.74667 \times 100 \\
\text{EEI} &= 74.67\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret against the world-class benchmark.**

> [!NOTE] An EEI of **74.67 %** is just **below the world-class threshold of 75 %**. The plant is performing reasonably well but needs targeted interventions (e.g., recognition programs, leadership communication) in the **lower-engagement segments (Scores 1 and 2 → 14 employees)** to push EEI above 75 %.

### **Alternative Method (Direct Formula Approach):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EEI} &= \frac{\sum (S_i \times f_i)}{n \times S_{max}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{448}{120 \times 5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{448}{600} \times 100 \\
&= 74.67\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Both methods yield identical results.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SEPY & SIR — Worked Numerical Example

### **Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing division has **800 employees**. During the financial year:
- Total suggestions submitted = **24,000**
- Total suggestions accepted by the Suggestion Committee = **9,600**
- Total suggestions actually implemented = **7,200**

**Calculate (a) SEPY, (b) SAR, and (c) SIR.**

### **Solution:**

**(a) Suggestions per Employee per Year (SEPY):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SEPY} &= \frac{\text{Total Suggestions Submitted}}{\text{Total Employees}} = \frac{24{,}000}{800} \\
\text{SEPY} &= 30 \text{ suggestions/employee/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ This **meets the world-class benchmark** of > 30.

**(b) Suggestion Acceptance Rate (SAR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAR} &= \frac{\text{Accepted}}{\text{Submitted}} \times 100 = \frac{9{,}600}{24{,}000} \times 100 \\
\text{SAR} &= 40\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ A SAR of 40 % indicates a **healthy suggestion pool** (industry norm: 30–50 %).

**(c) Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SIR} &= \frac{\text{Implemented}}{\text{Accepted}} \times 100 = \frac{7{,}200}{9{,}600} \times 100 \\
\text{SIR} &= 75\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ An SIR of 75 % is **above the world-class benchmark of 70 %**, indicating that management genuinely acts on accepted ideas.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CFT Selection Decision Framework

The following matrix helps in deciding **when to deploy a Cross-Functional Team** versus a single-function team. This is a high-yield KTU analytical concept.

| Decision Criterion | Use Single-Function Team | Use **Cross-Functional Team (CFT)** |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| Problem scope is confined to one department | ✅ | ❌ |
| Problem affects **multiple value-stream stages** | ❌ | ✅ |
| Root cause spans **Design → Production → Quality → Supply Chain** | ❌ | ✅ |
| Implementation requires **coordinated changes in > 2 departments** | ❌ | ✅ |
| Strategic impact on **cost / quality / delivery** is high | ❌ | ✅ |
| Quick local fix needed (e.g., 5-Why on a single machine) | ✅ | ❌ |
| Requires **customer or supplier interface** | ❌ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Decision Rule (KTU exam favourite):**
> If the problem requires touching **more than 2 functional boundaries** AND has **measurable impact on a WCM KPI** (OEE, FPY, COPQ, Lead Time), then a **CFT is mandatory**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Step-by-Step: Forming a CFT in a WCM Plant

**Stage 1 — Problem Identification & Charter**
- Plant Manager / WCM Pillar Leader identifies a chronic issue (e.g., **OEE stuck at 62 %** for 6 months).
- A formal **Team Charter** is drafted with: goal, scope, timeline, authority, deliverables.

**Stage 2 — Team Formation**
- Core members: **1 representative from each affected function** (Production, Quality, Maintenance, Process Engineering, Materials).
- Facilitator: Trained WCM coordinator (often a Black Belt or Pillar Leader).
- Sponsor: Plant Head (provides escalation path).

**Stage 3 — Diagnostic Phase**
- Use **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)**, **5-Why Analysis**, **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram**.
- Quantify losses using **OEE Loss Tree** (Availability × Performance × Quality).

**Stage 4 — Solution Generation & Selection**
- Brainstorm using **Pareto Analysis** to focus on the vital few causes (typically the **20 %** of causes driving **80 %** of losses).

**Stage 5 — Implementation & Standardization**
- Pilot the solution on one line/cell.
- If validated, **standardize** via updated SOPs, updated TPM autonomous maintenance cards, and updated training matrix.
- **Horizontal deployment** to other lines.

**Stage 6 — Closure & Recognition**
- Document savings (e.g., COPQ reduction, throughput gain).
- Recognize the team (formal WCM awards, certificates).
- Submit to **Suggestion Scheme** for any individual innovations.

## 3.5 Tuckman's Stages of CFT Development — KTUs' Favourite Conceptual Derivation

The evolution of any CFT follows **Bruce Tuckman's 5-stage model**:

$$
\text{Forming} \rightarrow \text{Storming} \rightarrow \text{Norming} \rightarrow \text{Performing} \rightarrow \text{Adjourning}
$$

| Stage | Behaviour | Facilitator's Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Forming** | Polite, hesitant, role-clarity-seeking | Set ground rules, clarify charter |
| **Storming** | Conflict, turf wars, blame games | Mediate, refocus on common goal |
| **Norming** | Trust builds, roles accepted, collaboration emerges | Coach, remove obstacles |
| **Performing** | High autonomy, high performance | Delegate, monitor KPIs only |
| **Adjourning** | Project complete, team disbanded | Capture learnings, recognize contributions |

> [!WARNING] **Most CFTs fail in the Storming phase.** Without a skilled facilitator, the team may regress to silos and never reach Performing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Three-Tier Employee Involvement Pyramid

The following Mermaid block renders the hierarchical structure of Employee Involvement in a WCM organization, from the broadest base (Suggestion Scheme) to the apex (Cross-Functional Teams).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["APEX: CROSS FUNCTIONAL TEAMS"]:::apex
    B["TIER 3: STRATEGIC & CROSS-DEPARTMENTAL"]:::tier3
    C["TIER 2: QUALITY CIRCLES & SMALL GROUPS"]:::tier2
    D["TIER 1: SUGGESTION SCHEME"]:::tier1
    E["FOUNDATION: PSYCHOLOGICAL SAFETY & TRUST"]:::foundation

    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A

    A --- A1["Composition: Multi-department"]
    A --- A2["Authority: High decision power"]
    A --- A3["Examples: NPI team, Kaizen Blitz team"]

    C --- C1["Composition: 6 to 12 peers from same area"]
    C --- C2["Authority: Recommend improvements"]
    C --- C3["Examples: QC Circles, TPM small groups"]

    D --- D1["Composition: Any individual employee"]
    D --- D2["Authority: Submit idea via form"]
    D --- D3["Examples: Kaizen suggestion card"]

    classDef apex fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef tier3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier1 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef foundation fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf6020,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Cross-Functional Team Structure & Information Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SPONSOR["PLANT HEAD / SPONSOR"]:::sponsor
    FAC["FACILITATOR / WCM COORDINATOR"]:::fac
    PROD["PRODUCTION REP"]:::prod
    QLY["QUALITY REP"]:::qly
    MNT["MAINTENANCE REP"]:::mnt
    PE["PROCESS ENGINEERING REP"]:::pe
    MAT["MATERIALS / SUPPLY CHAIN REP"]:::mat
    HR["HR / TRAINING REP"]:::hr

    SPONSOR -- "Charter & Authority" --> FAC
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> PROD
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> QLY
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> MNT
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> PE
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> MAT
    FAC -- "Facilitates meetings & tools" --> HR

    PROD <--> QLY
    QLY <--> MNT
    MNT <--> PE
    PE <--> MAT
    MAT <--> HR
    HR <--> PROD
    PROD <--> PE
    QLY <--> MAT

    SPONSOR -- "Reviews KPIs & Escalation" --> FAC

    classDef sponsor fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef fac fill:#7030a0,stroke:#4a1f70,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold

```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: CFT Lifecycle — Tuckman's Model in WCM Context

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Forming
    Forming --> Storming : Charter issued, members meet
    Storming --> Norming : Conflict resolved, roles accepted
    Norming --> Performing : Trust established, high collaboration
    Performing --> Adjourning : Project goals achieved
    Adjourning --> [*] : Learnings documented, recognition given

    note right of Forming
        Team members hesitant.
        Need clear charter & goals.
    end note
    note right of Storming
        Turf wars & blame games.
        Skilled facilitator essential.
    end note
    note right of Norming
        Trust building.
        Use of 7 QC tools begins.
    end note
    note right of Performing
        Autonomous problem solving.
        High OEE / FPY gains.
    end note
    note right of Adjourning
        SOPs updated, training done.
        Team formally dissolved.
    end note
```
