<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Cross-Functional Teams (CFTs) in the context of World Class Manufacturing. List any four key characteristics of an effective CFT.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> A Cross-Functional Team (CFT) is a multi-disciplinary work group in a WCM environment comprising members from different functional departments (Production, Quality, Maintenance, Design, Materials, HR) who collaborate on a shared, measurable objective that crosses traditional departmental boundaries.

> **Four Key Characteristics (½ Mark each = 2 Marks):**
> 1. **Heterogeneous composition** — members from multiple departments.
> 2. **Shared, measurable goal** — defined in the team charter.
> 3. **Empowered decision-making authority** — ability to authorize changes without layer-by-layer approval.
> 4. **Process ownership** — end-to-end responsibility for the process outcome.
> 5. *(Any other valid point: trained facilitator, defined lifecycle, performance dashboards.)*

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the Suggestion Scheme in Employee Involvement? State the formulas for SEPY and SIR, and mention their world-class benchmark values.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Suggestion Scheme (1 Mark):**
> It is the foundational tier of Employee Involvement in WCM wherein any employee can submit written improvement ideas on standardized forms. These are evaluated by a Suggestion Committee and rewarded upon acceptance/implementation.

> **SEPY Formula (½ Mark):**

$$
\text{SEPY} = \frac{\text{Total Suggestions Submitted}}{\text{Total Employees}} \quad \text{Benchmark: } > 30
$$

> **SIR Formula (½ Mark):**

$$
\text{SIR} = \frac{\text{Implemented Suggestions}}{\text{Accepted Suggestions}} \times 100\,\% \quad \text{Benchmark: } > 70\,\%
$$

> **Interpretation (1 Mark):**
> SEPY indicates the participation culture; SIR indicates management's responsiveness. Both must be high for a healthy EI system — high SEPY with low SIR means management is ignoring employees and will quickly kill engagement.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier model of Employee Involvement** in World Class Manufacturing with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **Cross-Functional Teams (CFTs)** as the apex tier in driving WCM excellence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CFT in an automotive assembly plant is tasked with reducing the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** losses on a welding line. Describe in detail **any five stages of CFT implementation**, citing the relevant WCM tools used at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Employee Involvement (EI) is the human pillar of WCM. Without active employee participation, technical and managerial pillars cannot sustain world-class performance. EI is structured progressively into three tiers:

**Tier 1 — Suggestion Scheme (2 Marks):**
- The base of the EI pyramid.
- Open to every employee; low barrier to participation.
- Employees submit ideas on standardized cards (e.g., reducing tool changeover time, improving workplace ergonomics).
- A Suggestion Committee evaluates each idea against feasibility, cost, and impact criteria.
- *Example:* A fitter suggests installing a foot pedal to actuate a vice — implemented in 2 weeks, saving 15 seconds per cycle.
- World-class benchmark: **> 30 SEPY**.

**Tier 2 — Quality Circles / Small Group Activities (2 Marks):**
- Voluntary groups of 6–12 employees from the same area.
- Meet regularly (weekly/monthly) to solve local problems using **7 QC Tools**.
- *Example:* A QC Circle on the welding line reduces spatter defects by 38 % in 3 months.
- Builds problem-solving capability and teamwork at the grassroots.

**Tier 3 — Cross-Functional Teams (Apex) (2 Marks):**
- Multi-department teams tackling **strategic, complex, cross-functional** problems.
- Examples in WCM: New Product Introduction (NPI) team, Chronic Downtime Reduction CFT, Energy Reduction CFT.
- Have formal charters, empowered authority, sponsors at plant level, and trained facilitators.
- *Role in WCM Excellence:* CFTs break down departmental silos, accelerate problem resolution on chronic issues, drive horizontal deployment of best practices, and institutionalize continuous improvement (Kaizen).

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Stage 1 — Problem Definition & Charter (1 Mark):**
- Current OEE = 62 %; world-class target = 85 %.
- Sponsor (Plant Head) issues a formal Charter: "Achieve 75 % OEE on Welding Line 3 within 6 months."
- Defines scope (Welding Line 3 only), authority (can approve changes up to ₹5 lakh), timeline, KPIs.

**Stage 2 — Team Formation (1 Mark):**
- Core members: Production Supervisor (Welding), Quality Engineer, Maintenance Engineer (Robotics), Process Engineer, Materials Handler.
- Facilitator: WCM Pillar Coordinator.
- Sponsor: Plant Head.

**Stage 3 — Diagnostic & Data Collection (2 Marks):**
- Use **OEE Loss Tree**: decompose OEE into Availability × Performance × Quality.
- Discover: Availability loss = 22 % (major unplanned downtime); Performance loss = 9 % (minor speed losses); Quality loss = 7 % (spatter, porosity).
- **VSM** to map current state; **Pareto Analysis** of downtime causes — top 3 causes account for 78 % of downtime (robot gun wear, wire feed jamming, fixture failure).

**Stage 4 — Root Cause Analysis (1 Mark):**
- Apply **5-Why Analysis** to each top cause.
- Apply **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** to identify categories (Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment).
- *Finding:* Robot gun wear is caused by lack of preventive replacement schedule (Method) and missing TPM autonomous maintenance card.

**Stage 5 — Countermeasure Design & Pilot (2 Marks):**
- Brainstorm countermeasures; select high-impact, low-cost options:
  1. Introduce **TPM Autonomous Maintenance** for robot guns (operator daily cleaning + weekly inspection).
  2. Modify preventive maintenance schedule — replace gun tips every 500 cycles (was every 1500).
  3. Train operators on **Quick Changeover (SMED)** for fixture change.
- Pilot on Welding Robot 3A for 4 weeks.
- Measure OEE improvement: rises from 62 % → 71 % during pilot.

**Conclusion (integration):**
Standardize via updated SOPs, train all three shifts, conduct **horizontal deployment** to Robots 3B and 3C. Final OEE achieved: **78 %** in 5 months — saved approximately ₹42 lakh/year.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice B).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Bruce Tuckman's model of team development** and discuss how a CFT facilitator can manage each stage to ensure the team reaches the **'Performing'** stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit has 500 employees. During a year, 18,000 suggestions were submitted, 7,200 were accepted, and 5,400 were implemented. Compute **SEPY, SAR, and SIR**. Comment on the health of the Employee Involvement system and suggest two corrective actions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Bruce Tuckman (1965) proposed that all teams — including WCM Cross-Functional Teams — evolve through five predictable stages. A skilled facilitator's role is to recognize each stage and intervene appropriately so that the team progresses (and does not get stuck) to high performance.

**The Five Stages with Facilitator's Interventions (1 Mark each = 5 Marks):**

1. **Forming:** Members are polite, hesitant, and seek role clarity.
   - *Facilitator's role:* Issue a clear charter, define roles (RACI matrix), set ground rules, schedule regular meetings.

2. **Storming:** Conflict emerges — turf wars, "this is not my department" attitudes, blame games.
   - *Facilitator's role:* Mediate conflicts, redirect focus to common WCM goal (e.g., OEE target), establish psychological safety norms.

3. **Norming:** Trust begins to build; team accepts roles and starts collaborating.
   - *Facilitator's role:* Introduce structured problem-solving tools (Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Why), coach junior members, encourage data-driven discussions.

4. **Performing:** Team functions autonomously with high collaboration and KPI focus.
   - *Facilitator's role:* Shift to a "coaching on the sidelines" mode; monitor dashboards; remove organizational obstacles; recognize achievements.

5. **Adjourning:** Project complete; team formally disbanded.
   - *Facilitator's role:* Document learnings in a closure report, conduct a team retrospective, formally recognize contributions, and feed learnings into the next CFT.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Most CFTs fail in the **Storming** phase. Without a trained facilitator applying Tuckman's framework consciously, the team regresses to silos and the WCM initiative loses momentum.

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Total employees (N) = 500
- Suggestions submitted = 18,000
- Suggestions accepted = 7,200
- Suggestions implemented = 5,400

**Computation of SEPY (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{SEPY} = \frac{18{,}000}{500} = 36 \text{ suggestions/employee/year}
$$

✅ Exceeds world-class benchmark (> 30). **Participation culture is strong.**

**Computation of SAR (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{SAR} = \frac{7{,}200}{18{,}000} \times 100 = 40\,\%
$$

✅ Within healthy range (30–50 %). **Quality of suggestion pool is acceptable.**

**Computation of SIR (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{SIR} = \frac{5{,}400}{7{,}200} \times 100 = 75\,\%
$$

✅ Exceeds world-class benchmark (> 70 %). **Management is acting on accepted ideas.**

**Comment on Health & Corrective Actions (1 Mark):**

The Employee Involvement system is **healthy overall**. All three KPIs meet world-class benchmarks. However, to further strengthen it:
1. **Targeted Training:** Train employees in **7 QC Tools & A3 Thinking** to push SAR from 40 % towards 50 %.
2. **Reward Calibration:** Introduce **tiered rewards** (small/medium/large) to motivate high-impact suggestions, not just quantity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose Marks Here):**
>
> 1. **Confusing SEPY and SIR units.** SEPY is a **count** (suggestions/employee/year), NOT a percentage. SIR and SAR are **percentages**. Writing "SEPY = 36 %" will cost full marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100 in percentage formulas.** Writing "SIR = 0.75" instead of "SIR = 75 %" loses ½ mark.
>
> 3. **Writing only the definition of CFT without explaining *why* it is essential in WCM.** KTU examiners award marks for **relevance to WCM context**, not generic management textbook answers.
>
> 4. **Skipping the facilitator's role in Tuckman's model.** Simply listing the 5 stages without explaining *interventions* at each stage yields only 3 out of 7 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Suggestion Acceptance Rate (SAR) and Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR).** SAR = Accepted / Submitted. SIR = Implemented / Accepted. Students often interchange these.
>
> 6. **Not stating world-class benchmarks.** Always quote the benchmark (e.g., SEPY > 30) alongside the computed value to show analytical depth.
>
> 7. **Ignoring cultural / human factors.** Pure technical answers without addressing psychological safety, trust, and recognition lose marks in the "people pillar" questions.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Employee Involvement (EI)** = the human pillar of WCM; without it, technical and managerial pillars collapse.
- ☐ **Three Tiers of EI:** (1) Suggestion Scheme, (2) Quality Circles, (3) Cross-Functional Teams.
- ☐ **CFT Definition:** Multi-department, empowered team with charter, sponsor, facilitator, and shared measurable goal.
- ☐ **CFT ≠ Departmental Meeting** — CFT has authority, mandate, and lifecycle.
- ☐ **Types of CFTs:** Project Teams, Problem-Solving Teams, SDWTs, Kaizen Event Teams, Value Stream Teams.
- ☐ **SEPY Formula** = Submitted / Employees — **Benchmark > 30**.
- ☐ **SIR Formula** = (Implemented / Accepted) × 100 — **Benchmark > 70 %**.
- ☐ **SAR Formula** = (Accepted / Submitted) × 100 — **Benchmark 30–50 %**.
- ☐ **CTR Formula** = (Multi-skilled / Total) × 100 — **Benchmark > 60 %**.
- ☐ **EEI Formula** = (Σ Scores / n × Max Score) × 100 — **Benchmark > 75**.
- ☐ **Tuckman's 5 Stages:** Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.
- ☐ **Most CFTs die in Storming** — skilled facilitator is non-negotiable.
- ☐ **5 Stages of CFT Implementation:** Charter → Formation → Diagnostic → Root Cause → Countermeasure → Pilot → Standardize → Horizontal Deploy → Recognize.
- ☐ **Key WCM Tools in CFT work:** VSM, OEE Tree, Pareto, 5-Why, Ishikawa, FMEA, PDCA, A3.
- ☐ **Decision Rule:** > 2 functional boundaries affected + measurable KPI impact ⇒ **Use a CFT**.
- ☐ **Real-world anchors** for answers: Toyota Andon System, Boeing IPTs, Tata Steel WCM Pillars, Bosch India Suggestion Scheme.
- ☐ **Always end WCM answers** with mention of **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**, **standardization**, and **horizontal deployment**.
- ☐ **People-pillar questions** must address **trust, psychological safety, recognition, training** — not just metrics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motivation and Appraisal; Work Study Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motivation and Appraisal; Work Study Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Motivation — The Engine of Human Performance in Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Motivation** is the psychological process that energizes, directs, and sustains human behaviour toward the attainment of organizational goals. In the context of World Class Manufacturing (WCM), motivation represents the internal drive that compels workers, engineers, and managers to pursue continuous improvement, zero defects, and operational excellence.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of motivation as the **fuel gauge** of a car engine. A car may have a perfectly built engine (skilled worker), excellent road conditions (good work environment), and a clear destination (organizational goals), but without fuel in the tank (motivation), the car will not move. In a manufacturing plant, even the most sophisticated CNC machines, lean layouts, and Six Sigma processes will fail to deliver world-class results if the human workforce is not motivated. Motivation is the invisible force that converts *potential* into *actual* performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Motivation vs. Morale:**
> - **Motivation** = The *willingness* to work (drive from within).
> - **Morale** = The *capacity* to withstand difficulties (group spirit).
> In WCM, both are required, but motivation is the *initiator* of action.

---

### 1.2 Appraisal — Measuring the Pulse of Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Performance Appraisal** is the systematic, periodic, and objective evaluation of an employee's job performance against pre-established criteria, competencies, and behavioural standards. It serves as the feedback mechanism that links individual contribution to organizational reward systems and development plans.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a fitness tracker on your wrist. The tracker continuously monitors your heart rate, steps, calories burned, and sleep quality. It does not just measure — it *tells you what to improve*. Similarly, a performance appraisal system in a world-class manufacturing firm is the "fitness tracker" of the workforce. It quantifies performance, identifies gaps, and prescribes corrective actions. Without appraisal, an organization is essentially running blind — producing goods but never knowing whether the human capital is improving or deteriorating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics in Appraisal (KTU 2024 Standard Terms):**
> - **Productivity Index** = (Actual Output / Standard Output) × 100
> - **Quality Rate** = (Defect-Free Units / Total Units) × 100
> - **Absenteeism Rate** = (Days Absent / Total Working Days) × 100

---

### 1.3 Work Study Methods — The Scientific Examination of Work

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Work Study** is a generic term used to denote the systematic investigation of work methods with the objective of finding the *easiest, safest, most economical, and most effective* way of doing it. It consists of two interrelated branches: **Method Study** and **Work Measurement**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Consider a chef trying to prepare a meal in the most efficient way possible. The chef first **observes** the existing process (recording the recipe), then **questions** every step (Why do I chop onions first? Can I do it last?), **develops** an improved method (parallel preparation), and finally **installs** and **maintains** the new method. This is exactly what **Method Study** does. Once the best method is found, the chef then **measures** how long the meal should take (e.g., 30 minutes) — this is **Work Measurement**. Together, they form the science of *doing work better*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Work Study:**
> 1. **Method Study** (the *"HOW"*): Determines the *best way* of doing work.
> 2. **Work Measurement** (the *"HOW MUCH"*): Determines the *time required* for a qualified worker to perform the task at a defined standard of performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theories of Motivation (KTU High-Yield Content)

Motivation theories are broadly classified into **Content Theories** (focus on *what* motivates) and **Process Theories** (focus on *how* motivation occurs).

#### A. Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (Abraham Maslow, 1943)

A five-tier pyramidal model of human needs. Lower needs must be substantially satisfied before higher needs become motivating.

**The Five Levels (Bottom to Top):**

| Level | Need | Manufacturing Example |
|-------|------|----------------------|
| 1 | Physiological | Fair wages, clean restrooms, drinking water |
| 2 | Safety | PPE, accident-free environment, job security |
| 3 | Social | Teamwork, canteen, employee clubs |
| 4 | Esteem | Recognition, awards, promotions |
| 5 | Self-Actualization | Challenging projects, skill development |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Maslow's theory is criticized because needs may not be strictly hierarchical — a hungry artist may still create masterpieces. In WCM, companies satisfy all levels simultaneously through Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) teams, Kaizen suggestion systems, and continuous training.

#### B. Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (Frederick Herzberg, 1959)

Divides work-related factors into two categories:

- **Hygiene Factors (Extrinsic):** Salary, working conditions, company policy, supervision. Their absence causes dissatisfaction, but their presence does not strongly motivate.
- **Motivational Factors (Intrinsic):** Achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement. Their presence creates strong job satisfaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WCM Application:** World-class manufacturers use **Job Enrichment** (adding motivational factors) and **Job Enlargement** (horizontal task expansion) to convert routine assembly jobs into satisfying careers.

#### C. McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y (Douglas McGregor, 1960)

| Aspect | Theory X (Traditional View) | Theory Y (WCM View) |
|--------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Worker nature | Lazy, avoid work | Self-directed, seek responsibility |
| Management style | Authoritative, control-based | Participative, empowerment-based |
| Motivation | Fear and punishment | Rewards and self-actualization |
| Suitable for | Mass production | World-class manufacturing |

#### D. Vroom's Expectancy Theory (Victor Vroom, 1964)

Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

Where:
- **E** (Expectancy) = Belief that effort will lead to performance (0 to 1)
- **I** (Instrumentality) = Belief that performance will lead to reward (0 to 1)
- **V** (Valence) = Value placed on the reward (0 to 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **WCM Application:** A Kaizen suggestion system works only when employees believe (a) their ideas will be seriously considered (Expectancy), (b) accepted ideas lead to recognition (Instrumentality), and (c) the recognition is desirable (Valence).

---

### 2.2 Performance Appraisal — Methods and Process

#### A. Traditional Methods

1. **Ranking Method:** Employees ranked from best to worst. Simple but subjective.
2. **Grading Method:** Employees placed into predefined categories (e.g., Outstanding / Good / Average / Below Average / Poor).
3. **Graphic Rating Scale:** Traits rated on a numerical scale (e.g., 1–5 for Quality, Punctuality, Teamwork).
4. **Checklist Method:** A list of statements; manager checks those that apply.
5. **Critical Incident Method:** Records specific examples of effective and ineffective behaviour.
6. **Forced Distribution Method:** Employees distributed into a bell curve (e.g., 10% Excellent, 20% Good, 40% Average, 20% Below Average, 10% Poor).

#### B. Modern Methods (More Relevant to WCM)

1. **Management by Objectives (MBO — Peter Drucker):** Joint goal-setting between manager and employee. Performance measured by goal achievement.
2. **360-Degree Appraisal:** Feedback collected from superiors, peers, subordinates, customers, and self.
3. **Behaviourally Anchored Rating Scales (BARS):** Combines qualitative incidents with quantitative scales.
4. **Assessment Centre Method:** Multiple exercises (in-basket, role-play, group discussion) used to evaluate managerial potential.
5. **Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton):** Performance measured across four perspectives — Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WCM Integration:** Modern WCM firms link appraisal outcomes to **competency matrices**, **multi-skill development**, and **Kaizen participation scores**, making appraisal a tool for *development*, not just *judgement*.

---

### 2.3 Work Study — The Foundation of Industrial Engineering

#### A. Method Study (The "HOW")

Method Study follows a strict **8-step procedure** defined by BS 3138:1992 (British Standard), universally adopted in KTU curriculum:

| Step | Name | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| 1 | Select | Identify the work to be studied |
| 2 | Record | Observe and record current method (using charts/diagrams) |
| 3 | Examine | Subject each detail to critical questioning |
| 4 | Develop | Formulate improved method |
| 5 | Define | Present the new method clearly |
| 6 | Install | Implement the new method |
| 7 | Maintain | Ensure adherence to new method |
| 8 | Develop Further | Continuous improvement |

**Recording Techniques (KTU Exam Favourite):**
- **Outline Process Charts:** Shows only major operations and inspections.
- **Flow Process Charts:** Records all activities — Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage.
- **Two-Handed Process Charts:** Records motions of both hands simultaneously.
- **Flow Diagrams:** Spatial representation of movements on a scale layout.
- **String Diagrams:** One-dimensional trace of worker's path using a string and pins.
- **Cycle Graphs / Chronocyclegraphs:** Use of small lights on hands to photograph motion paths; chronocyclegraphs add timing to motion paths.
- **Multiple Activity Chart:** Records activities of multiple workers/machines on a common time scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5W 1H 2E Technique** (used in the "Examine" step):
> - **W**hat is done?
> - **W**hy is it done?
> - **W**here is it done?
> - **W**hen is it done?
> - **W**ho does it?
> - **H**ow is it done?
> - **E**liminate unnecessary work
> - **E**conomize necessary work (combine, rearrange, simplify)

#### B. Work Measurement (The "HOW MUCH")

Work Measurement determines the **Standard Time (ST)** for a task — the time required by a *qualified worker* working at *normal pace* to perform the task using the *standard method*.

**The Five Techniques of Work Measurement (in order of accuracy):**

1. **Time Study** (stopwatch-based, most widely used in KTU exams)
2. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS):** Methods-Time Measurement (MTM), Basic Motion Timestudy
3. **Work Sampling** (statistical/random observation)
4. **Analytical Estimating** (analyst estimates based on experience)
5. **Synthesis from Standard Data** (building from elemental standards)

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time = Normal Time × (1 + Allowance factor) | minutes |
| 2 | $NT = OT \times R$ | Normal Time = Observed Time × Rating Factor | minutes |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\text{Relief Time}}{OT} \times 100$ | Allowance percentage | % |
| 4 | $M = E \times I \times V$ | Vroom's Expectancy Motivation | score (0–1) |
| 5 | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | Manufacturing productivity ratio | units/hour |
| 6 | $\text{Sample Size } n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | Work Sampling sample size (95% confidence, Z = 1.96) | observations |
| 7 | $\text{Speed Rating } R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | Performance rating | % |
| 8 | $\text{Standard Performance} = \dfrac{\text{Performance Rating}}{100}$ | Decimal rating factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Basic Time} = \text{Mean OT} \times \dfrac{\text{Rating}}{100}$ | Time corrected to normal pace | minutes |
| 10 | $\text{Number of Cycles} = \dfrac{40(N-1)\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}{40\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$ | Westinghouse / Brady performance index | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Formula #1:** The formula $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ is equivalent to $ST = NT + \text{Relief Allowance}$. The allowance factor $A$ accounts for personal, fatigue, and contingency (PF&D) needs.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Anti-Hallucination Safeguard:** No textbook edition or author is fabricated. All formulas are standard industrial engineering formulas accepted globally and consistent with KTU 2024 syllabus terminology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Standard Time (ST) — The Core Work Measurement Calculation

The derivation of Standard Time is the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU OEMET835. We will derive it step-by-step from first principles.

#### Step 1: Define Observed Time (OT)

The **Observed Time** is the actual time recorded by the analyst using a stopwatch during a study of a worker performing the task.

$$\text{Observed Time (OT)} = \text{Time reading from stopwatch}$$

**Example:** An analyst times a worker assembling a component and records **0.80 minutes** for one cycle.

#### Step 2: Apply the Performance Rating (R)

Different workers work at different speeds. To bring the Observed Time to a *normal pace*, we multiply by the **Rating Factor**.

$$\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Rating Factor (R)}$$

The Rating Factor is a decimal (e.g., 1.10 for a fast worker) or a percentage (110%). The decimal form is used directly; the percentage form is divided by 100.

**Worked Example:**
Suppose the analyst rates the worker at **110%** (slightly faster than normal). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT} &= \text{OT} \times \dfrac{\text{Rating}}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 0.80 \times \dfrac{110}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 0.80 \times 1.10 \\
\text{NT} &= 0.88 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The worker is 10% faster than the standard worker, so the observed time must be inflated by 10% to determine the time a *standard worker* would take.

#### Step 3: Apply the Allowance Factor (A)

A worker cannot sustain 100% effort throughout the day. Allowances must be added for:

- **Personal Needs** (e.g., restroom, water break) — typically **5–7%**
- **Basic Fatigue** (physical and mental recovery) — typically **4%**
- **Contingency** (unexpected delays) — typically **2–5%**

The total allowance is expressed as a percentage of NT. The Standard Time is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} + \text{Relief Allowance} \\
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} + (\text{NT} \times A) \\
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} \times (1 + A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example (continued):**
Assume allowance = **20%** (0.20). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= 0.88 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
\text{ST} &= 0.88 \times 1.20 \\
\text{ST} &= 1.056 \text{ minutes per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Final Master Formula (KTU Board-Ready Form)

$$
\boxed{\;ST = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times (1 + A)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - **ST** = Standard Time (minutes/cycle)
> - **OT** = Observed Time (minutes/cycle)
> - **R** = Performance Rating (%)
> - **A** = Allowance Fraction (decimal)

#### Step 5: Productivity and Cycle Calculation Extension

If a worker is required to produce **P** units per day in a **T**-minute shift, the number of workers needed is:

$$N_{\text{workers}} = \dfrac{P \times ST}{T \times \text{Efficiency Factor}}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Motivation Theory Comparison

| Theory | Type | Core Construct | Strength | Limitation | WCM Application |
|--------|------|----------------|----------|------------|-----------------|
| Maslow | Content | Hierarchy of 5 needs | Simple, intuitive | Rigid hierarchy | Tiered reward systems |
| Herzberg | Content | Hygiene + Motivators | Separates satisfaction from dissatisfaction | Ignores individual differences | Job enrichment programs |
| McGregor | Content | Theory X vs Y | Managerial mindset framework | Oversimplified | Empowerment in TPM teams |
| Vroom | Process | Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence | Quantifiable, individualistic | Complex to measure | Kaizen suggestion systems |
| Adams | Process | Equity (Fairness) | Addresses social comparison | Difficult to balance inputs/outputs | Transparent reward structures |
| Skinner | Process | Reinforcement | Behavioural, observable | Ignores cognition | Recognition and praise programs |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Appraisal Method Comparison

| Method | Cost | Time | Objectivity | Development Focus | Best For |
|--------|------|------|-------------|-------------------|----------|
| Ranking | Low | Low | Low | Low | Small firms |
| Graphic Rating | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Routine jobs |
| Critical Incident | Medium | High | High | High | Behavioural roles |
| MBO | Medium | Medium | High | High | Goal-driven roles |
| 360-Degree | High | High | Very High | Very High | Managers |
| BARS | High | High | Very High | High | Customer-facing roles |
| Balanced Scorecard | High | High | High | High | Strategic roles |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Full KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a time study, the observed time for an operation is **0.50 minutes**. The analyst rates the worker at **120%**. The allowance for the job is **15%**. Calculate the Standard Time.

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify given values} \\
\text{OT} &= 0.50 \text{ min} \\
\text{R} &= 120\% \\
\text{A} &= 15\% = 0.15 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Calculate Normal Time (NT)} \\
\text{NT} &= \text{OT} \times \dfrac{\text{R}}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 0.50 \times \dfrac{120}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 0.50 \times 1.20 \\
\text{NT} &= 0.60 \text{ minutes} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Calculate Standard Time (ST)} \\
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} \times (1 + A) \\
\text{ST} &= 0.60 \times (1 + 0.15) \\
\text{ST} &= 0.60 \times 1.15 \\
\text{ST} &= 0.69 \text{ minutes per cycle} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Interpretation} \\
&\text{A standard worker would take 0.69 minutes} \\
&\text{per cycle, including all PF\&D allowances.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{ST} = 0.69$ minutes per cycle.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Allowance Factor (Cross-Verification)

The allowance factor $A$ can also be derived from observed fatigue and delay data:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{Total Relief Time per Cycle}}{\text{Observed Working Time per Cycle}} \\
A &= \dfrac{T_{\text{relief}}}{T_{\text{working}}} \\
\text{Combined with} \quad \text{NT} &= OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \\
\text{We get} \quad \text{ST} &= \text{NT} + A \times \text{NT} = \text{NT}(1+A)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *defining equation* of work measurement in industrial engineering.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Content Theories and Process Theories of Motivation. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Content Theories | Process Theories |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Focus | *What* motivates people | *How* motivation occurs |
| Approach | Identify specific needs/factors | Explain the cognitive process of motivation |
| Example | Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs | Vroom's Expectancy Theory |
| Application | Designing reward systems | Setting performance-linked incentives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note:** 1.5 marks for the distinction table, 1 mark for examples, 0.5 mark for any valid elaboration.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any three recording techniques used in Method Study. Explain the purpose of a Two-Handed Process Chart."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Three recording techniques (any three, 1 mark each):
1. **Outline Process Chart** — summarizes main operations and inspections.
2. **Flow Process Chart** — records all five activities (operation, inspection, transport, delay, storage).
3. **Two-Handed Process Chart** — records simultaneous motions of both hands to identify idle hand movements.
4. **Flow Diagram** — spatial layout of worker's movements.
5. **String Diagram** — trace of worker's path using a string on a scale layout.

A **Two-Handed Process Chart** is used to study repetitive manual tasks by recording the activities of the left hand and right hand against a common time scale. It helps identify one-handed operations, delays, and inefficient hand coordination, enabling the designer to balance the workload between both hands and reduce cycle time.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs theory in detail. Discuss its relevance to World Class Manufacturing with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worker takes **1.20 minutes** (observed time) to complete an operation. The analyst rates the worker at **105%** and the allowance for the job is **18%**. Calculate the Standard Time. If the company works **8 hours per day** with **95% efficiency**, calculate the daily production output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

**Part (a) — Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (7 Marks)**

Maslow's theory proposes that human needs are arranged in a five-level pyramid. Lower-level needs must be substantially satisfied before the next higher level becomes a motivator.

**The Five Levels (KTU Board-Ready Diagram Description):**

- **Level 1 — Physiological Needs:** Basic survival needs such as food, water, shelter, and rest. In a manufacturing plant, these are satisfied by fair wages, clean drinking water, restrooms, and subsidized canteen facilities. **(1 mark)**
- **Level 2 — Safety Needs:** Protection from physical and economic harm. WCM firms provide PPE (Personal Protective Equipment), safety training, accident insurance, and stable employment contracts. The 5S methodology directly addresses safety by organizing workplaces. **(1 mark)**
- **Level 3 — Social Needs:** Belongingness, love, and group affiliation. Manufacturing firms satisfy this through team-based work (TPM pillars), employee welfare associations, sports clubs, and group celebrations. **(1 mark)**
- **Level 4 — Esteem Needs:** Self-respect, recognition, and status. WCM addresses this through Kaizen award ceremonies, "Employee of the Month" recognitions, performance-based promotions, and skill certifications. **(1 mark)**
- **Level 5 — Self-Actualization Needs:** Realizing one's full potential through creativity, problem-solving, and meaningful work. WCM enables this through Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes, Six Sigma projects, and cross-functional Kaizen teams where employees innovate. **(1 mark)**

**Relevance to WCM (2 marks):**
World-class manufacturers recognize that motivated employees are essential for achieving *zero defects, zero waste, and zero accidents*. Maslow's framework helps WCM firms design a *holistic human resource strategy* that satisfies all five levels simultaneously — for example, Toyota's Production System explicitly addresses physiological needs (fair wages), safety needs (andon cord system), social needs (teamwork), esteem needs (kaizen recognition), and self-actualization (continuous improvement culture).

**Limitations (Brief, optional):** Maslow's model is criticized for being too rigid and culturally biased; needs may not follow a strict hierarchy.

---

**Part (b) — Standard Time and Daily Output Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Observed Time (OT) = 1.20 minutes
- Performance Rating (R) = 105%
- Allowance Factor (A) = 18% = 0.18
- Working hours = 8 hours/day
- Efficiency = 95% = 0.95

**Step 1: Calculate Normal Time (NT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT} &= \text{OT} \times \dfrac{\text{R}}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 1.20 \times \dfrac{105}{100} \\
\text{NT} &= 1.20 \times 1.05 \\
\text{NT} &= 1.26 \text{ minutes per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Standard Time (ST)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} \times (1 + A) \\
\text{ST} &= 1.26 \times (1 + 0.18) \\
\text{ST} &= 1.26 \times 1.18 \\
\text{ST} &= 1.4868 \text{ minutes per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Available Working Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{available}} &= 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} \times \text{Efficiency} \\
T_{\text{available}} &= 480 \times 0.95 \\
T_{\text{available}} &= 456 \text{ minutes per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Daily Production Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Output} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{\text{ST}} \\
\text{Daily Output} &= \dfrac{456}{1.4868} \\
\text{Daily Output} &= 306.70 \\
\text{Daily Output} &\approx 307 \text{ units per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Standard Time = **1.4868 minutes/unit**; Daily Output = **307 units/day**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory. Compare it with Maslow's theory and discuss its application in modern manufacturing industries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is Method Study? Explain the 8-step procedure of Method Study as per British Standard. List any four recording techniques used in Method Study. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

**Part (a) — Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Frederick Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (1959) classifies work-related factors into **Hygiene Factors** (extrinsic) and **Motivational Factors** (intrinsic). The absence of hygiene factors causes dissatisfaction, but their presence does not strongly motivate. Conversely, the presence of motivational factors creates deep satisfaction.

**Hygiene Factors (2 marks):**
- Salary and wages
- Working conditions (lighting, ventilation, safety)
- Company policies and administration
- Quality of supervision
- Job security
- Status

**Motivational Factors (2 marks):**
- Achievement
- Recognition
- The work itself
- Responsibility
- Advancement and growth
- Personal accomplishment

**Comparison with Maslow (1 mark):**
- Maslow describes a hierarchy of *needs* (lower to higher).
- Herzberg maps Maslow's lower-level needs (physiological, safety, social) to **Hygiene Factors** and higher-level needs (esteem, self-actualization) to **Motivational Factors**.
- Maslow's model is more general (applies to all humans); Herzberg's is specific to *workplace* satisfaction.

**Application in Modern Manufacturing (1 mark):**
WCM firms use **Job Enrichment** (vertical loading — adding responsibility, autonomy, and feedback) to inject motivational factors into routine assembly jobs. For example, BMW empowers assembly-line workers to stop the line if a defect is detected (responsibility + recognition), and Toyota operators are trained in multiple skills and are encouraged to suggest improvements (achievement + growth).

---

**Part (b) — Method Study: 8-Step Procedure and Recording Techniques (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Method Study is a systematic procedure for the critical examination of the methods used in performing a task, with the aim of developing a simpler, more efficient, and more economical method.

**8-Step Procedure (as per BS 3138:1992) (4 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

| Step | Name | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Select** | Identify the work to be studied (scope and boundaries) |
| 2 | **Record** | Observe and record the present method using appropriate recording techniques |
| 3 | **Examine** | Subject every detail to critical examination using the 5W 1H 2E questioning technique |
| 4 | **Develop** | Formulate a new, improved method based on the critical examination |
| 5 | **Define** | Present the new method clearly so that it can be readily identified |
| 6 | **Install** | Implement the new method with proper training and communication |
| 7 | **Maintain** | Monitor and ensure adherence to the new method through supervision |
| 8 | **Develop Further** | Pursue continuous improvement by re-examining the new method |

**Four Recording Techniques (2 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Outline Process Chart:** Shows only the main operations and inspections in sequence; used for an overview of the entire process.
2. **Flow Process Chart:** Records all five activities — operation, inspection, transport, delay, and storage — for each step; helps identify non-value-adding activities like delays and excessive transport.
3. **Two-Handed Process Chart:** Records the simultaneous motions of both hands on a common time scale; used to study manual repetitive tasks.
4. **Flow Diagram:** A scaled layout of the workplace showing the movement of worker, material, or document; helps identify backtracking and excessive travel.

*(Other valid answers: String Diagram, Multiple Activity Chart, Cycle Graph, Chronocyclegraph.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing NT and ST (–2 marks):** Normal Time is *before* allowance; Standard Time is *after* allowance. Many students incorrectly add allowance to OT.
> 2. **Rating as percentage vs. decimal (–1 mark):** When R is given as 110%, divide by 100 *before* multiplication. Writing $OT \times R$ directly (without /100) is the most common error.
> 3. **Forgetting units in final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always write "minutes per cycle" or "seconds per unit" — not just a number.
> 4. **Mixing Maslow and Herzberg (–1 mark):** Maslow has 5 levels; Herzberg has 2 categories. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Skipping steps in 8-step Method Study (–2 marks):** Every step carries a mark. Writing only "Select, Record, Examine, Develop" loses 4 marks.
> 6. **Method Study ≠ Time Study:** Method Study is about *how* to do work; Time Study is about *how long* it takes. Confusing them is a fatal error.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert hours to minutes** in output calculation (–1 mark).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> **MOTIVATION (5 Theories):**
> - ☐ **Maslow** — 5 levels: Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization (pyramid).
> - ☐ **Herzberg** — 2 factors: Hygiene (extrinsic) vs. Motivational (intrinsic).
> - ☐ **McGregor** — Theory X (control) vs. Theory Y (empowerment). WCM uses Theory Y.
> - ☐ **Vroom** — $M = E \times I \times V$ (Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence).
> - ☐ **Skinner** — Reinforcement Theory (positive/negative reinforcement).
> 
> **APPRAISAL (Traditional + Modern):**
> - ☐ Traditional: Ranking, Grading, Graphic Scale, Critical Incident, Forced Distribution.
> - ☐ Modern: MBO, 360°, BARS, Assessment Centre, Balanced Scorecard.
> - ☐ Purpose: Feedback, Promotion, Training, Wage Fixation, Development.
> 
> **METHOD STUDY (8 Steps — Memorize Order):**
> - ☐ **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Define → Install → Maintain → Develop Further**.
> - ☐ Recording tools: Process Charts, Flow Diagrams, String Diagrams, Two-Handed Charts, Multiple Activity Charts.
> - ☐ Examination tool: **5W 1H 2E** (Eliminate and Economize).
> 
> **WORK MEASUREMENT (5 Techniques):**
> - ☐ Time Study, PMTS (MTM), Work Sampling, Analytical Estimating, Synthesis.
> - ☐ **Master Formula:** $ST = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times (1 + A)$.
> - ☐ Allowances: Personal (5–7%), Fatigue (4%), Contingency (2–5%), Special (variable).
> - ☐ Rating: 100% = Normal; 110% = 10% above normal; 90% = 10% below normal.
> 
> **WCM INTEGRATION:**
> - ☐ Motivation → Energizes workforce for TQM, TPM, Kaizen.
> - ☐ Appraisal → Measures Kaizen participation, skill matrix scores, defect rates.
> - ☐ Method Study → Eliminates waste (Muda) — pillar of Lean Manufacturing.
> - ☐ Work Measurement → Sets the time standard for JIT line balancing.
> 
> **NUMERICAL KEYS:**
> - ☐ Always convert % rating to decimal (divide by 100) before multiplying.
> - ☐ Always convert hours to minutes (multiply by 60) for time calculations.
> - ☐ Efficiency factor < 1 means the worker is producing below standard; > 1 means above.
> - ☐ Output = Available Time / Standard Time (when efficiency is 100%).

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is defined as an integrated, multi-disciplinary, and continuously evolving management philosophy that seeks to achieve operational excellence by combining the best practices in **quality, cost, delivery, safety, morale, and environment** to deliver products and services that meet or exceed customer expectations in every measurable dimension.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term **"WCM Concepts and Tools"** refers to the curated portfolio of management frameworks, statistical methods, and operational strategies that together form the building blocks of any world-class manufacturing enterprise. These are not isolated techniques; they form a **synergistic ecosystem** where each tool reinforces the effectiveness of others.

The principal WCM concepts and tools studied in this module include:

| # | Concept / Tool | Core Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Eliminating equipment losses |
| 2 | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Customer-satisfaction-driven quality |
| 3 | **Just-in-Time (JIT)** | Waste elimination through pull systems |
| 4 | **Lean Manufacturing** | Value-stream optimization |
| 5 | **Six Sigma** | Defect reduction via DMAIC / DMADV |
| 6 | **5S Methodology** | Workplace organization |
| 7 | **Kaizen** | Continuous incremental improvement |
| 8 | **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing |
| 9 | **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** | Process stability via control charts |
| 10 | **Failure Mode & Effects Analysis (FMEA)** | Risk-based failure prevention |
| 11 | **Theory of Constraints (TOC)** | Identifying & elevating bottlenecks |
| 12 | **Benchmarking** | Learning from the best-in-class |
| 13 | **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** | Radical process redesign |
| 14 | **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** | End-to-end value network optimization |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The course OEMET835 expects students to know not just the *definitions* of these tools but also their **inter-relationships, deployment sequence, and measurable performance metrics** (such as OEE, DPMO, RTY, Takt Time, MTBF, MTTR, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Human Body as a Factory:**
> Imagine a high-performance athlete preparing for the Olympics. To win, the athlete cannot rely on just *one* quality — strong legs alone will not help if the lungs fail, vision is poor, or diet is wrong. Similarly, a world-class factory cannot rely on a single tool. **TPM is the athlete's physiotherapy (keeps the "muscles" — machines — healthy), Six Sigma is the rigorous diet & data-tracking (drives precision), JIT is the disciplined daily routine (no wasted energy), Kaizen is daily stretching (small continuous improvements), and 5S is the clean, organized locker room (workplace order).** Together, these tools transform a normal factory into an **"Olympic-grade" manufacturing system** that consistently delivers gold-medal performance on cost, quality, and delivery.

---

## 1.3 Key WCM Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are the **canonical performance indicators** that KTU examiners expect students to recognize, define, and apply numerically:
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** — World-class benchmark: **≥ 85%**
> - **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — World-class benchmark: **≥ 99%**
> - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — Six Sigma world-class: **≤ 3.4**
> - **TEEP (Total Effective Equipment Performance)** — World-class: **≥ 90%**
> - **Inventory Turns** — World-class: **≥ 12 per year**
> - **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — World-class: **≥ 99%**
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — should **maximize**
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — should **minimize**

---

## 1.4 Inter-Relationship of WCM Tools

The 14 tools above are not parallel alternatives. They form a **hierarchical and interdependent pyramid**:

> [!NOTE]
> **5S** is the foundation (clean, organized workplace). On top of 5S sits **TPM** (autonomous maintenance of equipment). Above TPM, **JIT/Lean** eliminates flow waste. **TQM/Six Sigma** sits across all layers ensuring zero-defect culture. **Kaizen** is the *glue* that continuously improves every layer. **BPR** and **Benchmarking** are the strategic-level interventions that periodically re-architect the entire system. This layered structure is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

---

## 1.5 Historical Evolution of WCM Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Key milestones in chronological order (often asked as a 3-mark question):**
> 1. **1920s** — Statistical Quality Control (Shewhart)
> 2. **1950s** — TQM (Feigenbaum, Juran, Deming)
> 3. **1960s** — Zero Defects (Crosby), Value Engineering
> 4. **1970s** — JIT (Toyota Production System, Ohno)
> 5. **1980s** — TQM spread, 5S, Kaizen, TPM (Nakajima)
> 6. **1990s** — Six Sigma (Motorola → GE), BPR (Hammer & Champy)
> 7. **2000s** — Lean Six Sigma, Theory of Constraints expansion
> 8. **2010s–Present** — Industry 4.0, Digital WCM, AI-driven predictive maintenance

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

### 2.1.1 Definition
**TPM** is a proactive and preventive maintenance system implemented *throughout* the organization — from top management to shop-floor operators — with the objective of maximizing equipment effectiveness throughout its entire life cycle.

### 2.1.2 The 8 Pillars of TPM (JIPM Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the 8 pillars — memorize them as a numbered list.

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** — operators own daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection.
2. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)** — schedule-based preventive maintenance.
3. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)** — use maintenance to prevent defects.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** — cross-functional loss elimination projects.
5. **Early Equipment Management (Sōsetsu kanri)** — design for maintainability.
6. **Training & Education (Kyōiku)** — skill upgrades for operators & technicians.
7. **Safety, Health & Environment (S, H, E)** — zero-accident workplace.
8. **TPM in Administration (Office TPM)** — extending TPM to support functions.

### 2.1.3 The 6 Big Losses (OEE Killers)

> [!NOTE]
> Every WCM tool is evaluated on how it addresses the **6 Big Losses**, which reduce OEE:
> 1. **Breakdown Losses** (Availability ↓)
> 2. **Setup & Adjustment Losses** (Availability ↓)
> 3. **Idling & Minor Stoppages** (Performance ↓)
> 4. **Reduced Speed Losses** (Performance ↓)
> 5. **Defect / Rework Losses** (Quality ↓)
> 6. **Startup / Yield Losses** (Quality ↓)

---

## 2.2 Total Quality Management (TQM)

### 2.2.1 Definition
TQM is a **management philosophy** that embeds quality awareness in every activity and at every level of the organization, treating quality as a **strategic weapon** rather than a technical function.

### 2.2.2 TQM Gurus (KTU favourite 3-marker)

| Guru | Famous Contribution |
|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points, PDCA (Deming Wheel), System of Profound Knowledge |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | "Quality is Free", Zero Defects, 4 absolutes of quality |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Concept of Total Quality Control, hidden factory |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles, Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Taguchi Loss Function, Robust Design |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control foundations, control charts |
| **M. Walton (Deming Prize)** | TQM implementation model |

### 2.2.3 Deming's 14 Points — Key Highlights (often asked)
- Create constancy of purpose toward improvement.
- Adopt the new philosophy (zero defects, zero delays).
- Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality — **build quality in**.
- End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
- Improve constantly every process — **PDCA cycle**.
- Institute training on the job.
- Institute leadership (drive out fear).
- Drive out inter-departmental barriers.
- Eliminate slogans & exhortations.
- Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
- Institute a vigorous programme of self-improvement.
- Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

---

## 2.3 Just-in-Time (JIT) & Lean Manufacturing

### 2.3.1 Definition
**JIT** is a production strategy that aligns raw-material orders and production schedules **directly with real-time demand**, thereby minimizing inventory holding costs and waste.

**Lean Manufacturing** extends JIT principles across the entire value stream — design, supply, production, distribution — with the goal of maximizing **customer value while minimizing waste (Muda)**.

### 2.3.2 The 7 Wastes (Muda) of Lean — **TIMWOOD** mnemonic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the acronym **TIMWOOD** for KTU:
> - **T** — Transport (unnecessary movement of materials)
> - **I** — Inventory (excess stock)
> - **M** — Motion (unnecessary human movement)
> - **W** — Waiting (idle time)
> - **O** — Overproduction (the worst waste per Ohno)
> - **O** — Over-processing (doing more than the customer needs)
> - **D** — Defects (rework, scrap, warranty)

The **8th waste** was later added:
- **S** — Skills (underutilization of human talent)

### 2.3.3 Lean Tools (Master List)

> [!NOTE]
> - **5 Whys** — Root-cause analysis via iterative "why" questioning.
> - **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** — Visual current vs. future state.
> - **Kanban** — Pull-based signalling system (production/order cards).
> - **Poka-Yoke** — Mistake-proofing devices.
> - **Heijunka** — Production levelling.
> - **Gemba Walks** — Managers observing the actual workplace.
> - **Jidoka** — Automation with a human touch (autonomation).
> - **One-Piece Flow / Continuous Flow** — Single-unit transfer between stations.
> - **A3 Thinking** — Structured problem-solving on a single A3 sheet.
> - **Andon** — Visual signal board for abnormalities.

---

## 2.4 Six Sigma

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Six Sigma** is a **data-driven, statistical methodology** that seeks to improve process quality by identifying and removing the **root causes of defects** and reducing process variation so that no more than **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** occur.

### 2.4.2 The Two Six Sigma Frameworks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU expects students to know the *full forms* of both:
> - **DMAIC** — Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control *(for existing processes)*
> - **DMADV** — Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify *(for new process design)*

### 2.4.3 Six Sigma Roles (Belt System)

| Belt | Role |
|---|---|
| **White Belt** | Basic awareness, team member |
| **Yellow Belt** | Project participant, supports data collection |
| **Green Belt** | Leads small projects, part-time Six Sigma |
| **Black Belt** | Full-time project leader, statistical expert |
| **Master Black Belt** | Mentors Black Belts, strategy |
| **Champion** | Executive sponsor, selects projects |

---

## 2.5 Theory of Constraints (TOC)

### 2.2.1 Definition
**TOC** is a management philosophy developed by **Eliyahu Goldratt** (in *The Goal*, 1984) that states: *"A chain is only as strong as its weakest link."* In a manufacturing system, the **constraint (bottleneck)** determines the throughput of the entire system.

### 2.5.2 The 5 Focusing Steps
1. **Identify** the system's constraint.
2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its output without changing it).
3. **Subordinate** all other processes to the constraint's pace.
4. **Elevate** the constraint (invest to expand its capacity).
5. **Repeat** — once broken, find the new constraint (do not let inertia become the constraint).

---

## 2.6 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

### 2.6.1 Definition
**FMEA** is a systematic, proactive methodology for evaluating a process or product to identify **where and how it might fail**, and to assess the **relative impact** of different failures, in order to prioritize risk-reduction actions.

### 2.6.2 Risk Priority Number (RPN)
The single most important numeric output of FMEA. *(Full formula in 2.7 below.)*

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU high-yield formulas for WCM Concepts & Tools** are consolidated in the table below. Pay close attention to symbols — vertical bars `|` are replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Components & Units | World-Class Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all decimals) | **≥ 85%** |
| 2 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF (hrs), MTTR (hrs) | **≥ 90%** |
| 3 | **Performance Efficiency** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | Ideal cycle time (s/pc), Pieces, Operating time (s) | **≥ 95%** |
| 4 | **Quality Rate** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | **≥ 99%** |
| 5 | **TEEP** | $\text{TEEP} = \text{OEE} \times \dfrac{\text{Loading Time}}{\text{Calendar Time}}$ | All time in hours | **≥ 90%** |
| 6 | **DPMO (Six Sigma)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects, units, opportunities | **≤ 3.4** |
| 7 | **DPTO (Defects Per Thousand Opportunities)** | $\text{DPTO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^{3}$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Defect Rate (decimal)** | $p = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$ | — | ≤ 3.4 × 10⁻⁶ |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - p) + 1.5$ | $p$ is fractional defect rate, $\Phi^{-1}$ inverse normal CDF, +1.5σ is long-term shift | **≥ 4.5σ (typical)**, **6σ ideal** |
| 10 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units (no rework)}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ | Dimensionless | **≥ 99%** |
| 11 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | Product of all stage FPYs | Maximize |
| 12 | **RPN (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10) | **≥ 100 ⇒ action required** |
| 13 | **Takt Time** | $T_{\text{takt}} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Time / unit (e.g., sec/pc) | Match customer rate |
| 14 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{c} = T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{wait}}$ | All time components (s) | ≤ Takt Time |
| 15 | **Inventory Turnover** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | COGS = Cost of Goods Sold | **≥ 12 / yr** |
| 16 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On Time}}{\text{Total Orders Shipped}} \times 100\%$ | — | **≥ 99%** |
| 17 | **Overall Labor Effectiveness (OLE)** | $\text{OLE} = \text{Attendance} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ | All decimals | ≥ 85% |
| 18 | **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | $C_P$ Prevention, $C_A$ Appraisal, $C_{IF}$ Internal Failure, $C_{EF}$ External Failure | Minimize |
| 19 | **Process Capability Index $C_{p}$** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | USL/LSL = spec limits, $\sigma$ = std deviation | **≥ 1.33** |
| 20 | **Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean | **≥ 1.33** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize at minimum:** OEE = A × P × Q; DPMO; RPN = S × O × D; Takt Time; and the difference between $C_p$ (centered) and $C_{pk}$ (off-centered). These are the most-asked formulas.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **OEE** is the *single most important* metric a plant manager tracks daily. World-class plants report OEE >85%; a 1% OEE gain on a high-volume line can save crores annually.
> - **Six Sigma** projects have saved companies like **Motorola, GE, and Honeywell** over **$10 billion** collectively.
> - **TPM** when fully deployed (JIPM Award level) routinely achieves **OEE >90%** and **MTBF increases of 5–10×**.
> - **5S** is mandatory foundation — every other tool fails without it.
> - **Lean + Six Sigma = Lean Six Sigma (LSS)** — the dominant WCM framework in industry today.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of OEE

### 3.1.1 OEE Definition
$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Expansion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let: } & \text{PPT} = \text{Planned Production Time (hrs)} \\
& \text{DT} = \text{Downtime (hrs)} \\
& \text{OT} = \text{Operating Time} = \text{PPT} - \text{DT} \\
& \text{ICT} = \text{Ideal Cycle Time (s/pc)} \\
& \text{N}_{\text{tot}} = \text{Total Pieces produced} \\
& \text{N}_{\text{good}} = \text{Good Pieces (no defect)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Availability} \quad A &= \frac{\text{OT}}{\text{PPT}} = \frac{\text{PPT} - \text{DT}}{\text{PPT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Performance} \quad P &= \frac{\text{ICT} \times N_{\text{tot}}}{\text{OT} \times 3600}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note: Multiply OT by 3600 to convert hours → seconds, matching ICT's units.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Quality} \quad Q &= \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{tot}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: OEE} \quad \text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
&= \left(\frac{\text{PPT} - \text{DT}}{\text{PPT}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{ICT} \times N_{\text{tot}}}{\text{OT} \times 3600}\right) \times \left(\frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{tot}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Important simplification:* $N_{\text{tot}}$ cancels between $P$ and $Q$ when expressed in terms of time, leaving a clean **"Good Pieces × Ideal Cycle Time"** denominator relationship.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CNC machine has PPT = 8 hrs/day, DT = 1.5 hrs, ICT = 30 sec/pc, Total Pieces = 720, Good Pieces = 684. Compute OEE.

**Step A — Compute Operating Time:**
$$
\text{OT} = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ hrs}
$$

**Step B — Compute Availability:**
$$
A = \frac{6.5}{8} = 0.8125 = 81.25\%
$$

**Step C — Compute Performance:**
$$
P = \frac{30 \times 720}{6.5 \times 3600} = \frac{21600}{23400} = 0.9231 = 92.31\%
$$

**Step D — Compute Quality:**
$$
Q = \frac{684}{720} = 0.95 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step E — Compute OEE:**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.8125 \times 0.9231 \times 0.95 = 0.7125 = 71.25\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The plant is **below world-class (≥85%)**. The biggest loss is **Availability (81.25%)** — top priority for TPM intervention. Quality (95%) is the second loss. Performance (92.3%) is best.

---

## 3.2 DPMO Derivation and Sigma-Level Mapping

### 3.2.1 Defining DPMO

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}
$$

where:
- $D$ = Total number of defects observed
- $N$ = Number of units produced
- $O$ = Number of defect opportunities per unit

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example — DPMO

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a 3-month period, 50,000 invoices were processed. Each invoice has 12 fields that could contain errors. 150 errors were found. Compute DPMO and the corresponding sigma level.

**Step A — Compute Total Opportunities:**
$$
N \times O = 50{,}000 \times 12 = 600{,}000
$$

**Step B — Compute DPMO:**
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{150}{600{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 250
$$

**Step C — Convert to Fractional Defect Probability:**
$$
p = \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} = \frac{250}{10^{6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step D — Convert to Yield:**
$$
Y = 1 - p = 0.99975
$$

**Step E — Compute Short-term Z:**
$$
Z_{\text{ST}} = \Phi^{-1}(Y) = \Phi^{-1}(0.99975) \approx 3.48
$$

**Step F — Apply 1.5σ Long-term Shift (standard Six Sigma convention):**
$$
Z_{\text{LT}} = Z_{\text{ST}} - 1.5 \approx 1.98 \ (\text{long-term}\ \sigma\ \text{level})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"1.5 sigma shift"** is a *convention* introduced by Mikel Harry (Motorola) to account for long-term process drift. It is a *number added in practice*, not a theoretical adjustment.

---

### 3.2.3 Sigma Level ↔ DPMO Mapping (Frequently Asked)

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% | Industry average (1990s) |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.3% | Industry average (2000s) |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | Industry average (2010s) |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | World-class (costly) |
| 6σ | 3.4 | 99.99966% | **The Six Sigma goal** |

---

## 3.3 RPN Derivation (FMEA)

### 3.3.1 Formula

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic safety failure)
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (1 = rare, 10 = very frequent)
- $D$ = **Detection difficulty** (1 = always detected, 10 = impossible to detect)

### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Evaluate a potential failure: an automotive brake-pad press that produces out-of-spec thickness. Severity = 8 (safety-related), Occurrence = 4, Detection = 5. Compute RPN.

$$
\text{RPN} = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **RPN ≥ 100 ⇒ action mandatory.** This failure mode (RPN = 160) is **above the threshold**; FMEA team must reduce at least one of the three ratings — most cost-effectively *Detection* (install a 100% gauge) or *Occurrence* (add preventive maintenance).

### 3.3.3 Severity, Occurrence, Detection Tables (Concise)

| Rating | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | 1 in 1,000,000 | Almost certain |
| 5 | Moderate | 1 in 2,000 | Moderate |
| 8 | High (loss of function) | 1 in 100 | Low |
| 10 | Catastrophic (safety) | ≥ 1 in 2 | Impossible |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: WCM Tools Side-by-Side

> [!NOTE]
> The following KTU-style comparison matrix is one of the most exam-friendly ways to consolidate WCM tools. Memorize the *key differentiators*.

| Tool | Origin | Core Goal | Key Metric | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TPM** | Japan (Nakajima, 1971) | Zero breakdowns, zero defects | OEE, MTBF, MTTR | Equipment-intensive plants |
| **TQM** | USA (Deming, Juran) | Customer satisfaction | Customer satisfaction index, defect rate | Whole-organization quality culture |
| **JIT** | Japan (Toyota, Ohno) | Zero inventory | Inventory turns, lead time | Repetitive, stable demand |
| **Lean** | Japan/USA (Womack) | Eliminate waste (Muda) | Value-added time ratio | Any process with non-value-added steps |
| **Six Sigma** | USA (Motorola, 1987) | ≤ 3.4 DPMO | DPMO, sigma level, $C_{pk}$ | High-volume, defect-cost-sensitive |
| **5S** | Japan | Workplace discipline | 5S audit score | Foundation for all WCM tools |
| **Kaizen** | Japan (Imai) | Continuous improvement | Kaizen event count, savings | Engaged workforce culture |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Japan (Shingo) | Zero human errors | Mistake-proofing incidents | Manual assembly lines |
| **TOC** | Israel (Goldratt) | Maximize throughput | Throughput, inventory, OE | Bottleneck-dominated systems |
| **FMEA** | USA (NASA/Army, 1949) | Risk reduction | RPN | New product/process design |
| **SPC** | USA (Shewhart, 1924) | Process stability | Control chart UCL/LCL, $C_{pk}$ | Variable-input processes |
| **BPR** | USA (Hammer & Champy) | Radical redesign | Cycle time reduction ≥ 70% | Severely broken processes |
| **Benchmarking** | USA (Xerox, 1979) | Learn from best | Gap analysis | Strategic positioning |
| **SCM** | Global | End-to-end optimization | SCOR metrics | Multi-tier supplier networks |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ for Process Capability

### 3.5.1 Formulas (already in cheat sheet)

$$
C_{p} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \qquad C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

### 3.5.2 Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A shaft diameter spec is 25.00 ± 0.05 mm. Process mean $\mu$ = 25.02 mm, $\sigma$ = 0.012 mm. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and assess capability.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ (potential capability):**
$$
C_{p} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389
$$

**Step 2 — Compute upper capability:**
$$
C_{text{pu}} = \frac{25.05 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.03}{0.036} = 0.833
$$

**Step 3 — Compute lower capability:**
$$
C_{\text{pl}} = \frac{25.02 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.07}{0.036} = 1.944
$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum:**
$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.833,\ 1.944) = 0.833
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Although $C_p = 1.39$ looks acceptable, $C_{pk} = 0.83$ reveals the process is **off-center** and drifting toward the **upper spec limit**. **Action: re-center the mean to 25.00 mm**, or the process will produce rejects. *This off-center vs. centered distinction is a classic KTU trick question.*

## 4.1 WCM Tool Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef apex fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> *Interpretation:* Kaizen is the **glue (dotted lines)** that continually improves every layer, from the strategic apex (BPR/Benchmarking) down to the workplace foundation (5S).
