# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Define World Class Manufacturing (WCM). List any four pillars / tools commonly used to achieve WCM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** WCM is an integrated management philosophy aimed at achieving operational excellence across quality, cost, delivery, safety, and morale by combining best practices such as TQM, TPM, JIT, Six Sigma, Lean, 5S, and Kaizen into a coherent system.
- **Four tools (0.375 each = 1.5 marks):**
  1. **TPM** — for equipment effectiveness.
  2. **JIT / Lean** — for waste elimination.
  3. **Six Sigma** — for defect reduction.
  4. **5S** — for workplace organization.

> [!TIP]
> For a 3-marker, allocate 1.5 marks to the definition and 1.5 marks to the list with one-line explanations.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: Explain the concept of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). What is the world-class benchmark value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** OEE is a composite metric that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, defined as the product of **Availability × Performance × Quality**.
- **Components (1 mark):** Availability = (Operating Time / Planned Time); Performance = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time; Quality = Good Pieces / Total Pieces.
- **World-class benchmark (0.5 marks):** **OEE ≥ 85%** is considered world-class (SEMI E10 standard).

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. KTU provides an internal choice — **either (a) OR (b)** of the same module/topic. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two independent alternatives.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEMET835]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** concept in detail. List and briefly describe its **eight pillars**. What are the **six big losses** that TPM targets?

**(b) [7 marks]** A CNC turning machine operates in a 2-shift plant (16 hrs/day). Data: 
- Planned Production Time = 16 hrs
- Downtime = 2.5 hrs
- Ideal Cycle Time = 45 sec/pc
- Total Pieces Produced = 900
- Good Pieces = 855

Compute (i) Availability, (ii) Performance, (iii) Quality, (iv) OEE. Identify the **biggest loss** and recommend a TPM-based intervention.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — TPM Eight Pillars [7 marks]

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** *(1 mark)* — Operators take daily ownership of cleaning, lubrication, and inspection of their own machines, preventing minor issues from escalating.

2. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)** *(1 mark)* — A scheduled preventive-maintenance program that addresses MTBF and MTTR via time-based, condition-based, and predictive tasks.

3. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)** *(0.5 mark)* — Designing maintenance to prevent defects (e.g., setting machines so they cannot produce out-of-spec parts).

4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** *(0.5 mark)* — Cross-functional project teams targeting the **six big losses** using P-M analysis and root-cause methods.

5. **Early Equipment Management** *(0.5 mark)* — Designing new equipment for reliability, maintainability, and operability from the start.

6. **Training and Education** *(0.5 mark)* — Continuous skill-upgradation of operators and maintenance staff.

7. **Safety, Health, and Environment (SHE)** *(0.5 mark)* — Zero-accident workplace culture.

8. **TPM in Administration (Office TPM)** *(0.5 mark)* — Extending loss-elimination to non-production functions (HR, finance, planning).

**The Six Big Losses (2 marks, 0.33 each):**
1. Breakdown Losses
2. Setup and Adjustment Losses
3. Idling and Minor Stoppages
4. Reduced Speed Losses
5. Defect / Rework Losses
6. Startup / Yield Losses

> [!NOTE]
> The six big losses are grouped under the three OEE factors: **Availability** (1, 2), **Performance** (3, 4), **Quality** (5, 6). Mapping losses to OEE is a high-value KTU point.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — OEE Computation [7 marks]

**Given:**
PPT = 16 hrs, DT = 2.5 hrs, ICT = 45 s/pc, $N_{\text{tot}}$ = 900, $N_{\text{good}}$ = 855.

**Step 1 — Operating Time (1 mark):**
$$
\text{OT} = \text{PPT} - \text{DT} = 16 - 2.5 = 13.5 \text{ hrs}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability (1 mark):**
$$
A = \frac{13.5}{16} = 0.84375 = 84.38\%
$$

**Step 3 — Performance (1.5 marks):**
$$
P = \frac{45 \times 900}{13.5 \times 3600} = \frac{40500}{48600} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

> *Conversion note: 13.5 hrs × 3600 s/hr = 48,600 seconds.*

**Step 4 — Quality (1 mark):**
$$
Q = \frac{855}{900} = 0.95 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 5 — OEE (1 mark):**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.84375 \times 0.8333 \times 0.95 = 0.6678 = 66.78\%
$$

**Step 6 — Biggest Loss & Recommendation (1.5 marks):**
- All three factors are below world-class, but **Performance (83.3%)** is the worst, followed by Availability (84.4%).
- **TPM-based intervention (Autonomous Maintenance + Planned Maintenance)** to address minor stoppages and speed losses, plus **setup-time reduction (SMED)** to recover availability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write OEE = 66.78% and stop. **You must interpret** — the plant is well below 85% world-class, and *which loss* is the largest *must* be stated. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing interpretation.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEMET835]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Six Sigma methodology** in detail. Describe the **DMAIC** framework with the purpose of each phase. State the world-class DPMO target.

**(b) [7 marks]** During a Six Sigma study, a printed-circuit-board assembly line processed **20,000 boards** in one month. Each board has **25 defect opportunities**. **120 defects** were observed. Compute:
(i) DPMO,
(ii) Process sigma level (using the standard 1.5σ long-term shift),
(iii) Rolled-Throughput Yield (RTY) if the line has 3 sequential stages with FPYs of 0.95, 0.92, and 0.97.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Six Sigma & DMAIC [7 marks]

**Six Sigma Definition (1 mark):** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology for eliminating defects in any process — from manufacturing to transactions — by reducing variation so that the long-term defect rate is ≤ 3.4 DPMO.

**DMAIC Phases (5 marks, 1 each):**
1. **D — Define** the problem, customer (VOC), CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) characteristics, and project scope via a project charter.
2. **M — Measure** the current process performance; collect baseline data, validate the measurement system (Gage R&R), and quantify the existing sigma level.
3. **A — Analyze** the data to find root causes of variation and defects using tools like Pareto, fishbone, hypothesis testing, regression.
4. **I — Improve** by piloting solutions using Design of Experiments (DOE); select the optimal solution; verify gain in sigma.
5. **C — Control** the gains with SPC charts, standard operating procedures, and a handover plan to the process owner.

**World-class DPMO target (1 mark):** **≤ 3.4 DPMO** corresponding to a **6σ** process capability.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — DPMO, Sigma, RTY [7 marks]

**Given:** $N = 20{,}000$ boards, $O = 25$ opportunities/board, $D = 120$ defects, FPY₁ = 0.95, FPY₂ = 0.92, FPY₃ = 0.97.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Opportunities (1 mark):**
$$
N \times O = 20{,}000 \times 25 = 500{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{120}{500{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 240
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Yield (0.5 mark):**
$$
Y = 1 - \frac{240}{10^{6}} = 0.99976
$$

**Step 4 — Short-term Z (0.5 mark):**
$$
Z_{\text{ST}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.99976) \approx 3.56\sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Long-term Z (1 mark):**
$$
Z_{\text{LT}} = 3.56 - 1.5 = 2.06\sigma
$$

> *Conclusion: The line is operating at about 2.06σ — far below Six Sigma. The line is roughly at "3σ quality" with significant DPMO (240 vs 3.4).*

**Step 6 — Compute RTY (2 marks):**
$$
\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_{1} \times \text{FPY}_{2} \times \text{FPY}_{3} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.874 \times 0.97 \\
\text{RTY} &= 0.8478 = 84.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Even though each stage is 92–97% efficient, the *end-to-end* FPY is only 84.78% — illustrating the **multiplicative penalty** of multi-stage processes.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks in WCM Concepts & Tools questions:**
> 1. **OEE Confusion:** Writing OEE = A + P + Q instead of **A × P × Q**. → Lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **DPMO vs. DPM:** Some students interchange DPM (per thousand) and DPMO (per million). **Remember the "M" stands for Million.**
> 3. **Six Sigma shift:** Forgetting the **+1.5σ shift** is acceptable only if the question *explicitly* says "short-term". Default = long-term → subtract 1.5.
> 4. **$C_p$ vs. $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ assumes the process is **centered**; $C_{pk}$ accounts for **off-center** processes. Mixing them = full loss.
> 5. **Six pillars of TQM confused with 8 pillars of TPM.** Do NOT mix them in the answer.
> 6. **Muda ≠ Mura ≠ Muri:** Mudа = waste, Mura = unevenness, Muri = overburden. *WCM often tests all three.*
> 7. **5S is NOT a "tool" but a "foundation".** Examiners expect students to use the term *foundation* or *prerequisite*.
> 8. **FMEA table not drawn.** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the FMEA scoring table; drawing it in a 14-marker is mandatory.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist for WCM Concepts & Tools:**
> - **OEE = A × P × Q**, world-class ≥ 85%; TEEP ≥ 90%.
> - **DPMO = (D / (N × O)) × 10⁶**; **world-class target ≤ 3.4** at 6σ.
> - **6σ = 3.4 DPMO** (long-term, with 1.5σ shift).
> - **Six Big Losses** of TPM = 2 Availability + 2 Performance + 2 Quality losses.
> - **Eight Pillars of TPM:** Auto-Maintenance, Planned-Maintenance, Quality-Maintenance, Focused-Improvement, Early-Equipment-Mgmt, Training, SHE, Admin TPM.
> - **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control (existing processes); **DMADV** = same with *Design, Verify* (new processes).
> - **Six Sigma Belts:** White → Yellow → Green → Black → Master Black → Champion.
> - **RPN = S × O × D**, threshold = 100.
> - **Takt Time = Available Time / Customer Demand.**
> - **5S = Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain** — the foundation.
> - **Kaizen = continuous small improvement**; PDCA = Plan, Do, Check, Act.
> - **Lean wastes (TIMWOODS):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, (Skills).
> - **TOC 5 steps:** Identify → Exploit → Subordinate → Elevate → Repeat.
> - **$C_{pk}$ is always ≤ $C_p$**; if $C_{pk} = C_p$, the process is centered.
> - **BPR** is *radical redesign* (Hammer & Champy, 1993), not incremental.
> - **Benchmarking** types: Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic.
> - **Deming's 14 Points** are *philosophical*; Juran's trilogy is *operational*; Crosby's 4 Absolutes are *behavioral*.
> - **Taguchi Loss Function:** Loss increases quadratically as parts deviate from target — a key WCM philosophy.
> - **FMEA Origin:** US Military (1949), later adopted by automotive (AIAG-VDA FMEA 2019).
> - **5S is NOT optional** — without it, TPM, Lean, Six Sigma all collapse.
> - **Lean Six Sigma (LSS) = Lean Speed + Six Sigma Quality** = today's dominant WCM framework.
> - **Famous KTU one-liner:** *"WCM is not a destination but a journey of continuous improvement."*

---

*End of Module 3 — WCM Concepts and Tools notes. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!* 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AMBITE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AMBITE (Ambidextrous Manufacturing Strategy)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMBIDEXTROUS MANUFACTURING (AMBITE)** is the strategic capability of a manufacturing organization to **simultaneously pursue two paradoxical objectives** — *Exploitation* (refinement, efficiency, incremental improvement of existing operations) and *Exploration* (innovation, flexibility, discovery of new opportunities, radical change) — without compromising long-term competitiveness.

In the canonical WCM framework formalized by **O'Reilly & Tushman (2004)**, ambidexterity is defined as *“the ability of an organization to simultaneously exploit existing competencies and explore new opportunities.”* When applied to a manufacturing context, it becomes the operational philosophy by which a firm aligns its **operational excellence programs (Lean, Six Sigma, TPM)** with **innovation pipelines (new product development, smart manufacturing, Industry 4.0)** within a single, coherent system.

### Key Lexical Anchors (KTU 2024 Terminology)

| Term | Formal Meaning in WCM Context |
|---|---|
| **Exploitation** | Leveraging known capabilities — high efficiency, low cost, reliability |
| **Exploration** | Building new capabilities — experimentation, R&D, agility |
| **Ambidexterity Index (AI)** | Composite measure of dual capability balance |
| **Structural Separation** | Physical/organizational split between exploitation & exploration units |
| **Contextual Ambidexterity** | Same individuals/teams switching between modes through behavioural design |
| **Dynamic Capability** | The firm's ability to reconfigure resources for change |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ambidextrous Chef & The Restaurant**
>
> Imagine a world-class restaurant kitchen. The **left hand** (exploitation) runs the perfectly standardized production line for the signature dish — every plate identical, every gram measured, every second timed. This is the **Lean** side: TPM machines, Six Sigma quality, JIT ingredients.
>
> The **right hand** (exploration) experiments in the R&D corner — fusion recipes, molecular gastronomy, new menu ideas, Industry 4.0 connected tasting feedback. This is the **Agile** side: prototypes, pilot runs, customer co-creation.
>
> The chef is **ambidextrous** — neither hand stops the other. If the kitchen runs *only* the standard line → loses relevance in 5 years. If it runs *only* experiments → goes bankrupt next month. **AMBITE is the disciplined coordination of both hands.** A factory that is ambidextrous is one that delivers today's product perfectly *and* invents tomorrow's product simultaneously.

---

## 1.3 Why AMBITE is Central to World Class Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU's 2024 scheme, **WCM is defined as a state of perpetual, simultaneous excellence on multiple competitive dimensions**: cost, quality, delivery, flexibility, and innovation. AMBITE is the **only** conceptual lens that reconciles the *inherent tension* between **Operational Excellence (cost-out, defect-out)** and **Strategic Renewal (innovation-in, capability-in)**. It is, therefore, a **Module-3 level integrative concept** rather than a stand-alone tool.

**Industry Recognition**: Studies by McKinsey (2020) and the World Economic Forum's *Global Lighthouse Network* (2023) reveal that **~70% of "Lighthouse" smart factories** explicitly cite ambidextrous operating models as a core enabler of their Industry 4.0 transitions.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> Three metrics must be memorized verbatim for the KTU examination:
> - **OTD (On-Time Delivery):** Must be ≥ **98%** in WCM benchmarks
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** World-class threshold ≥ **85%**
> - **NPI (New Product Introduction) Cycle Time:** World-class threshold ≤ **6 months** for high-variety manufacturers
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exploitation–Exploration Tension — Structured Logic

The AMBITE concept rests on one central paradox: **the same organizational routines that make a factory excellent today, make it obsolete tomorrow.** The theory is built in three nested layers.

### Layer 1 — March's Foundational Theory (1991)

James G. March formalized the trade-off in *Organization Science*:

1. **Exploitation** includes *refinement, choice, production, efficiency, implementation, execution*.
2. **Exploration** includes *search, variation, risk-taking, experimentation, discovery, innovation*.
3. **Both compete for the same scarce resources** — managerial attention, capital, talent, and time.
4. **Over-specialization in either is self-destructive** — pure exploitation leads to *competency traps*; pure exploration leads to *failure traps*.
5. **Solution**: Build structural and contextual mechanisms that allow **both to coexist**.

### Layer 2 — O'Reilly & Tushman's Dual-Architecture Model (2004)

Three pre-conditions for ambidexterity are required:

1. **Strategic Intent** — a *common vision* shared by both halves (e.g., the WCM "One-Factory" north star).
2. **Senior Team Cohesion** — a *single executive team* with shared rewards and metrics, so the two halves do not become rival fiefdoms.
3. **Architectural Separation** — distinct *operating models, processes, cultures, and reward systems* for the exploit and explore units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Exam Tip: The phrase *"Senior Team Cohesion"* is a direct 3-marker — students often forget the third leg and only write two.

### Layer 3 — Types of Ambidexterity (Decision Logic Tree)

| Type | Mechanism | Best Fit When |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Ambidexterity** | Two semi-autonomous units (exploit unit + explore unit) housed in one firm | Large enterprises, mature industries, disruptive tech pressure |
| **Contextual Ambidexterity** | Same individuals/teams oscillate between modes; enabled by culture & stretch assignments | SMEs, knowledge-intensive firms, R&D-led organizations |
| **Temporal (Sequential) Ambidexterity** | Firm cycles: explore phase → exploit phase → explore phase | Project-based manufacturers, capital-goods EPC firms |
| **Network Ambidexterity** | Exploit done in-house; explore outsourced to start-ups, universities, consortia | Firms with strong open-innovation strategies |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Definition of Variables | Units | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | A = Availability, P = Performance, Q = Quality | Dimensionless (0–1) | Exploitation metric |
| 2 | $\text{NPI Cycle Time} = T_{\text{concept}} - T_{\text{launch}}$ | Calendar days from concept gate to launch | Days / Months | Exploration metric |
| 3 | $\text{AI} = \alpha \cdot E_{\text{explore}} + \beta \cdot E_{\text{exploit}}$ | AI = Ambidexterity Index, E = capability score, $\alpha, \beta$ = weights ($\alpha + \beta = 1$) | Score (0–10) | Balance measurement |
| 4 | $\text{ROIC}_{\text{ambidex}} = \frac{\text{NOPAT} - \text{Innovation Tax}}{\text{Invested Capital}}$ | NOPAT = Net Operating Profit After Tax; Innovation Tax = cost of foregone short-term efficiency | % | Strategic valuation |
| 5 | $\text{Innovation Index} = \frac{N_{\text{new}}}{\sum N} \times 100\%$ | $N_{\text{new}}$ = new products < 3 yrs, $\sum N$ = total SKUs | % | Exploration vitality |
| 6 | $\text{Innovation Tax} = \Delta\eta_{\text{loss}} \cdot C_{\text{unit}}$ | $\Delta\eta_{\text{loss}}$ = efficiency loss during exploration, $C_{\text{unit}}$ = unit cost | Currency | Trade-off cost |
| 7 | $\text{Cycle Ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{explore}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | $T_{\text{explore}}$ = time on explore activities | Dimensionless | Contextual ambidexterity |
| 8 | $P_{\text{firm}} = f(\text{Efficiency}, \text{Flexibility}, \text{Innovation})$ | Multivariate performance function | Score | WCM dashboard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting note**: All absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser intact.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the AMBITE Framework

| Engineering Field | Application of AMBITE |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (e.g., Toyota, Tesla)** | Exploit: TPS / Lean lines; Explore: EV + autonomy R&D centres |
| **Discrete Manufacturing (e.g., Siemens Amberg)** | Exploit: Lights-out production; Explore: Digital twin exploration |
| **Process Industry (e.g., refineries)** | Exploit: Six Sigma yield optimization; Explore: Bio-feedstock pilots |
| **Aerospace (e.g., Boeing, HAL)** | Temporal AMBITE: alternate between ramp-up and next-gen programs |
| **Medical Devices (e.g., Johnson & Johnson)** | Network AMBITE: JLABs for explore, in-house for exploit |

---

## 2.4 The "Why & How" — Six Causal Pillars

1. **Why ambidexterity?** Because the *half-life of a competitive advantage* in manufacturing has fallen from ~30 years (1970s) to under 5 years (2024).
2. **How structural?** By creating a **P&L-split dual architecture** with shared top-team metrics.
3. **How contextual?** By designing stretch assignments that force engineers to oscillate between A3 problem-solving (exploit) and design-thinking sprints (explore).
4. **Why not just two separate firms?** Because *synergies from shared resources, brand, and customer base* exceed the costs of separation.
5. **How measured?** By tracking **AI, OEE, NPI Cycle Time, Innovation Index** as a balanced scorecard.
6. **Why is it a WCM pillar?** Because the *WCM definition itself* demands simultaneous excellence — ambidexterity is the *operational meaning* of that demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ambidexterity Index (AI) — Normalized Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Ambidexterity Index quantifies the balance between explore and exploit activities. A *healthy* firm sits between **0.3 and 0.7 on the exploration share**; outside this band, the firm is at strategic risk.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define capability scores**
Let $E_e$ = exploration capability score and $E_x$ = exploitation capability score, each measured on a 0–10 scale via employee surveys, audit data, or process maturity models (e.g., CMMI for explore, WCM Audit for exploit).

**Step 2 — Define share of resources allocated**
Let $r_e$ = fraction of total operating budget, headcount, or capital allocated to explore activities, where $0 \le r_e \le 1$. Then the exploit share is $r_x = 1 - r_e$.

**Step 3 — Geometric mean formulation**

The Ambidexterity Index is most rigorously defined as the **geometric mean** of the two capability scores weighted by their resource shares:

$$
\text{AI} = (r_e \cdot E_e)^{0.5} \cdot (r_x \cdot E_x)^{0.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Expansion to show structure**

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{r_e \cdot r_x \cdot E_e \cdot E_x}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum-ambidexterity condition**

For a fixed total capability $E_e + E_x = C$, AI is maximized when:

$$
r_e = r_x = 0.5
\quad \text{and} \quad
E_e = E_x
$$

This is the *balanced-ambidexterity sweet spot*. Substitution gives the theoretical maximum:

$$
\text{AI}_{\max} = \sqrt{0.25 \cdot E_e^2} = 0.5 \cdot E_e
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule for KTU exam**

- If $\text{AI} < 0.3 \cdot E_{\text{mean}}$ → firm is **exploration-starved** (competency trap).
- If $\text{AI} > 0.7 \cdot E_{\text{mean}}$ → firm is **exploitation-starved** (failure trap).
- If $0.3 \le \text{AI} \le 0.7$ → firm is in the **ambidextrous zone**.

---

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem**: A precision-engineering firm has exploration capability $E_e = 7.2$ and exploitation capability $E_x = 8.8$. It allocates $r_e = 0.45$ of resources to explore and $r_x = 0.55$ to exploit. Evaluate the Ambidexterity Index and diagnose the firm's state.

**Step 1 — Substitute the values into the AI formula**

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{0.45 \times 7.2 \times 0.55 \times 8.8}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the two resource fractions**

$$
0.45 \times 0.55 = 0.2475
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the two capability scores**

$$
7.2 \times 8.8 = 63.36
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the partial products**

$$
0.2475 \times 63.36 = 15.6816
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root**

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{15.6816} \approx 3.96
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the mean capability**

$$
E_{\text{mean}} = \frac{7.2 + 8.8}{2} = 8.0
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the diagnostic band**

$$
0.3 \times 8.0 = 2.4
\quad ; \quad
0.7 \times 8.0 = 5.6
$$

**Step 8 — Decision**

Since $2.4 \le 3.96 \le 5.6$, the firm is **in the ambidextrous zone**, but skewed mildly toward exploitation. **Recommendation**: increase $r_e$ from 0.45 to ~0.50 to maximize AI.

**Step 9 — Maximum-AI projection if balanced**

$$
\text{AI}_{\max} = 0.5 \times 8.0 = 4.0
$$

Improvement potential: $4.0 - 3.96 = 0.04$ (i.e., marginal — the firm is already close to optimal). The lever is now **capability development**, not allocation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Innovation Tax — Trade-off Equation

When a unit moves from pure exploitation toward exploration, it sacrifices short-term efficiency. That opportunity cost is the *Innovation Tax*.

**Step 1 — Define base efficiency**

Let $\eta_0$ = baseline OEE of a pure-exploitation line.

**Step 2 — Define efficiency loss**

Let $\Delta\eta$ = drop in OEE during explore mode (e.g., due to small batches, new tooling, operator learning curves).

**Step 3 — Define unit cost**

Let $C_u$ = unit production cost in pure-exploit mode.

**Step 4 — Innovation Tax per unit**

$$
I_{\text{tax}} = \Delta\eta \cdot C_u
$$

**Step 5 — Total Innovation Tax per year**

$$
I_{\text{tax,annual}} = I_{\text{tax}} \cdot Q
$$

where $Q$ = annual units produced.

**Step 6 — Strategic decision rule**

A firm should pursue exploration if the **expected Net Present Value of new capability** exceeds the discounted Innovation Tax over the planning horizon.

$$
\text{NPV}_{\text{explore}} \;>\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{I_{\text{tax,annual}}}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

where $r$ = cost of capital and $T$ = horizon in years.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ambidexterity Types

| Dimension | Structural | Contextual | Temporal | Network |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coordination Cost** | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Knowledge Spillover** | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Speed of Response** | Slow | Fast | Cyclical | Fast |
| **Cultural Coherence** | Two cultures | One culture | One culture | Hybrid |
| **Best-Fit Firm Size** | Large | Small–Medium | Project-based | Any |
| **Risk of Silos** | High | Low | Medium | High |
| **KTU 2024 Marks Weightage** | 4 marks | 3 marks | 3 marks | 2 marks |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Choosing the Right Ambidexterity Type

**Problem**: A medium-sized Coimbatore-based pump manufacturer with 1,200 employees faces disruption from IoT-based smart-pump competitors. Should it adopt structural, contextual, or network ambidexterity? Justify with one quantitative metric.

**Solution Logic**:

1. **Firm size check** — 1,200 employees → falls in *medium-SME* range → *Contextual* or *Network* preferred (Structural is over-engineered).
2. **Disruption speed** — IoT is fast-moving → *Contextual* is too slow if only internal → prefer *Network*.
3. **Resource check** — IoT talent scarce in-house → outsource explore to IIT-Madras research park via *Network AMBITE*.
4. **Quantitative check** — Compute AI for a hypothetical explore-allocation of $r_e = 0.30$:

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{0.30 \times 6.0 \times 0.70 \times 8.5} = \sqrt{10.71} \approx 3.27
$$

This is within the ambidextrous band $(2.4 \le 3.27 \le 5.6)$, confirming the strategy is viable. **Final answer: Network AMBITE with contextual overlay in-house.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Firm Strategy and WCM Vision] --> B[Ambidextrous Operating Model]

    B --> D[Exploration Engine]

```

```mermaid
    quadrant-3 Reactive
    quadrant-4 Explorer
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Ambidextrous Manufacturing. List the two strategic capabilities it reconciles.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Ambidextrous Manufacturing (AMBITE) is the strategic capability of a manufacturing organization to *simultaneously* pursue two paradoxical objectives: **Exploitation** (refining existing competencies for efficiency, cost, and quality) and **Exploration** (building new competencies through innovation, R&D, and experimentation). The two capabilities it reconciles are, therefore, **(i) Operational Excellence** and **(ii) Strategic Renewal / Innovation**.
> *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – exploit, 1 mark – explore with one-line description each.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
*State the three pre-conditions for ambidexterity given by O'Reilly and Tushman.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Strategic Intent** – a shared, aspirational vision that unites both halves of the firm. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Architectural Separation** – distinct processes, structures, and cultures for the exploit and explore units. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **Senior Team Cohesion** – a single executive team with shared rewards and metrics binding the two units. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Slot]**
*AMBITE strategy is the backbone of any World Class Manufacturing transformation. With neat architecture, discuss the four types of ambidexterity. For each type, give **one industry example** and **one limitation**.*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) *Discuss the four types of ambidexterity with architecture.* **(7 marks)**

**1. Structural Ambidexterity (2 marks)**
The firm creates *two semi-autonomous units* — a core exploitation unit that runs the production line under lean/six-sigma discipline, and a separate exploration unit (often called "New Ventures" or "Advanced Manufacturing Group") that experiments with new products and processes. Each has its own P&L, KPIs, and reward system, but both report to a shared top team. **Industry example**: 3M's *core manufacturing* and *Skunk Works*-style innovation labs. **Limitation**: risk of creating two rival cultures and slow knowledge transfer.

**2. Contextual Ambidexterity (2 marks)**
The *same* individuals/teams oscillate between exploitation and exploration, enabled by stretch assignments, ambidextrous behaviours, and a culture of learning. There is no structural split. **Industry example**: Toyota, where engineers rotate between Kaizen problem-solving (exploit) and genchi-genbutsu new-product study (explore). **Limitation**: requires extremely mature culture and high-trust leadership; not scalable to very large firms.

**3. Temporal (Sequential) Ambidexterity (2 marks)**
The firm alternates between dedicated explore phases and exploit phases. Boeing's defence division, for example, dedicates years to one new platform (explore) followed by decades of production ramp-up (exploit). **Industry example**: Civil aviation OEMs (Boeing 787 → 737 MAX cycles). **Limitation**: discontinuous; difficult to maintain exploration momentum between cycles.

**4. Network Ambidexterity (1 mark)**
Exploitation is done in-house while exploration is co-created with start-ups, universities, and consortia. **Industry example**: Siemens' *next47* innovation unit partnering with start-ups while its core factories run lean production. **Limitation**: IP leakage risk and weaker internal capability building.

#### (b) *Design an AMBITE balanced scorecard for a Tier-1 automotive supplier. Specify one metric per perspective with a target value.* **(7 marks)**

**Solution Framework** (4 perspectives × 1.75 marks each):

| Perspective | Metric | Target | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Financial** | Revenue from products < 3 years old | **≥ 30%** of total | Confirms explore pipeline converts to cash |
| **Customer** | On-Time Delivery (OTD) | **≥ 98%** | Confirms exploit side is reliable |
| **Internal Process** | NPI Cycle Time | **≤ 9 months** | Confirms explore side is fast |
| **Learning & Growth** | Patents filed per year | **≥ 10** | Confirms innovation output |

**Cross-check using the AI formula** — if the firm scores 7 on both $E_e$ and $E_x$ and allocates $r_e = 0.4$:

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{0.4 \times 7 \times 0.6 \times 7} = \sqrt{11.76} \approx 3.43
$$

Since $0.3 \times 7 = 2.1 \le 3.43 \le 0.7 \times 7 = 4.9$, the firm is **in the ambidextrous band**, validating the scorecard choice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Slot]**
*Ambidexterity fails when firms over-specialize. With a numerical illustration, derive the Ambidexterity Index (AI) and explain the *competency-trap* and *failure-trap* failures of an unbalanced firm.*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) *Derive the Ambidexterity Index in normalized form and show the sweet-spot condition.* **(7 marks)**

**Step 1** – Define $E_e, E_x \in [0, 10]$ and $r_e \in [0, 1]$ such that $r_x = 1 - r_e$.

**Step 2** – Write the geometric mean form:

$$
\text{AI} = \sqrt{r_e \cdot r_x \cdot E_e \cdot E_x}
$$

**Step 3** – Expand: $\text{AI} = \sqrt{r_e(1-r_e) \cdot E_e \cdot E_x}$

**Step 4** – Maximize over $r_e$ by taking derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dr_e}\left[r_e(1-r_e)\right] = 1 - 2r_e = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; r_e^\star = 0.5
$$

**Step 5** – For fixed total capability $E_e + E_x = C$, by AM-GM:

$$
E_e \cdot E_x \le \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)^2
$$

with equality at $E_e = E_x = C/2$.

**Step 6** – Therefore:

$$
\text{AI}_{\max} = \sqrt{0.25 \cdot (C/2)^2} = \frac{C}{4}
$$

**Step 7** – Interpretation: AI is maximized when the firm allocates **50% to each mode** AND develops **equal capability in both**.

#### (b) *Illustrate the competency-trap and failure-trap with a numerical example, and recommend a corrective action.* **(7 marks)**

**Numerical Setup** (3.5 marks):
- *Firm A* (competency-trapped): $E_e = 2$, $E_x = 9$, $r_e = 0.10$, $r_x = 0.90$.

$$
\text{AI}_A = \sqrt{0.10 \times 0.90 \times 2 \times 9} = \sqrt{1.62} \approx 1.27
$$

Mean capability $= 5.5$, lower threshold $= 0.3 \times 5.5 = 1.65$. Since $1.27 < 1.65$, **Firm A is exploration-starved** → *competency trap* → long-run extinction.

- *Firm B* (failure-trapped): $E_e = 9$, $E_x = 2$, $r_e = 0.90$, $r_x = 0.10$.

$$
\text{AI}_B = \sqrt{0.10 \times 0.90 \times 9 \times 2} = \sqrt{1.62} \approx 1.27
$$

Same AI numerically (symmetric formula), but now **Firm B is exploitation-starved** → *failure trap* → short-run bankruptcy.

**Corrective Action** (3.5 marks):

1. For **Firm A**: launch a *Network AMBITE* pilot — partner with two start-ups and one university to inject $E_e$ rapidly. Rebalance to $r_e = 0.45$ over 18 months.
2. For **Firm B**: deploy *TPM and OEE* program on the production line; freeze new launches; appoint a Chief Operating Officer with P&L responsibility for the exploit side. Rebalance to $r_e = 0.45$ over 12 months.
3. For both: install a *WCM-balanced scorecard* with the four metrics from Question A.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. Students often write *“ambidextrous = Lean + Six Sigma”*. This is wrong — Lean/Six Sigma are *exploitation* tools only; ambidexterity *adds* exploration.
> 2. The AI formula is *geometric mean*, not arithmetic mean. Arithmetic mean gives a falsely high AI even for unbalanced firms.
> 3. Students frequently skip the **Senior Team Cohesion** leg of the O'Reilly–Tushman model. Examiners specifically test this — 1 mark lost.
> 4. Do **not** confuse AMBITE with **Agile Manufacturing**. Agile is *one tool* for the explore side; AMBITE is the *integrative umbrella*.
> 5. In 14-mark answers, examiners want **numerical illustration** at least once. A pure conceptual answer caps at ~9 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMBITE — Top 15 Things to Remember Before the KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Definition**: simultaneous exploitation + exploration (O'Reilly & Tushman).
- [ ] **March (1991)** formalized the *exploitation–exploration* trade-off.
- [ ] **O'Reilly & Tushman (2004)** — three pre-conditions: Strategic Intent, Architectural Separation, Senior Team Cohesion.
- [ ] **Four types**: Structural, Contextual, Temporal, Network.
- [ ] **Ambidexterity Index** = $\sqrt{r_e \cdot r_x \cdot E_e \cdot E_x}$ — **geometric mean** is mandatory.
- [ ] **Sweet spot**: $r_e = 0.5$ AND $E_e = E_x$.
- [ ] **Competency trap** = over-exploitation; **Failure trap** = over-exploration.
- [ ] **Innovation Tax** = $\Delta\eta \cdot C_u$ quantifies efficiency loss during exploration.
- [ ] **WCM benchmarks**: OEE $\ge$ 85%, OTD $\ge$ 98%, NPI $\le$ 6 months.
- [ ] **Structural** suits large firms; **Contextual** suits SMEs; **Temporal** suits project-based; **Network** suits IP-rich firms.
- [ ] **Balanced scorecard** must include all four perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning.
- [ ] AMBITE is *not* the same as Agile, Lean, or Six Sigma — it is the **integrative umbrella**.
- [ ] **McKinsey & WEF Lighthouse** data cite AMBITE as a top-3 enabler of Industry 4.0 success.
- [ ] In 14-mark answers: include **at least one numerical illustration** to score above 9.
- [ ] **Final keyword to memorize**: *“Balance, not Trade-off”* — AMBITE eliminates the false dichotomy between today and tomorrow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRP II - TOPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MRP II - TOPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: MRP II & TOPP — Core Technical Foundation

## 1. Introduction to the Module

This module focuses on two pivotal production-planning paradigms that drive modern **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** enterprises:
1. **MRP II** — a *push-based, computer-integrated planning architecture* rooted in Western manufacturing logic.
2. **TOPP** — the *Toyota Production Planning Process*, a *pull-based, demand-driven planning architecture* that underpins the legendary Toyota Production System (TPS).

Together they represent the two dominant schools of thought for translating customer demand into shop-floor execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **push logic of MRP II** and the **pull logic of TOPP/JIT**. KTU examiners frequently test the comparative logic, not just definitions.

---

## 2. MRP II — Formal Definition

**Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II)** is a computer-integrated, closed-loop information system that extends Material Requirements Planning (MRP) by encompassing **all manufacturing resources** — materials, machine capacity, labor, tooling, and financial implications — to plan, monitor, and control the entire manufacturing enterprise.

It is often represented by the classic acronym coined by Oliver Wight:

$$
\textbf{MRP II} = \text{Money} + \text{Material} + \text{Machine} + \text{Man} + \text{Method}
$$

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, MRP II is positioned as the **bridge between traditional MRP (shop-floor scope) and modern ERP (enterprise scope)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** *MRP* only computes *what* and *when* materials are needed. *MRP II* answers the *whether-it-is-feasible* question by linking the plan to **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** and feedback loops.

---

## 3. TOPP — Formal Definition

**TOPP (Toyota Production Planning Process)** is Toyota's proprietary, hierarchically layered planning methodology that converts long-term demand forecasts into leveled, sequenced, pull-driven shop-floor execution. It operates on three time-horizon tiers:

| Tier | Horizon | Tool |
|------|---------|------|
| Tier 1 | Annual | **Annual Production Plan** (capacity & profit plan) |
| Tier 2 | Monthly | **Monthly Production Plan** (model mix & volume) |
| Tier 3 | Daily | **Heijunka (Production Leveling)** with Kanban |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TOPP is **not identical to TPS** — TPS is the overall production philosophy; **TOPP is the planning brain of TPS** that decides *what* to make and *in what quantity* at each level of granularity.

---

## 4. Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

### MRP II Analogy — The "Master Chef with a Full Pantry"
Imagine a grand hotel kitchen. The Head Chef (MRP II system) has a **detailed recipe book** (Bill of Materials) for every dish. Every morning, the front office submits the day's banquet orders (forecasted demand). The Chef:
1. Computes *exactly* how many tomatoes, flour, eggs, and spices are needed (**Gross Requirements**).
2. Checks the pantry (**On-hand Inventory**).
3. Subtracts one from the other (**Net Requirements**).
4. Verifies whether the kitchen stations (ovens, stoves, prep tables) have the **capacity** to deliver the orders on time.
5. Issues precise purchase orders to suppliers and assigns work to each cook.
6. Tracks actual cost of every dish against the planned cost.

That end-to-end, **closed-loop** orchestration is **MRP II**.

### TOPP Analogy — The "Conveyor-Belt Sushi"
Picture a conveyor-belt sushi restaurant. Instead of guessing how many tuna rolls to make, the kitchen makes only **one tuna roll at a time**, places it on the belt, and only **produces another when a customer picks one up**. Behind the scenes, the manager (TOPP) smooths demand across the day (Heijunka) so the kitchen never has sudden bursts. There is **no big pantry of raw fish** waiting — only small, replenished bins (Kanban). This is **pull-based, level scheduling** — the essence of TOPP.

> [!NOTE]
> MRP II plans **from the top down (push)**. TOPP plans **from the bottom up (pull)**. The KTU 2024 module contrasts these to teach students how to **choose the right paradigm** for a given industry context.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Part A — MRP II Architecture in Depth

### 2.1 The Evolution Hierarchy

The evolution from independent shop-floor scheduling to enterprise planning follows a precise ladder:

$$
\text{Order Point System (OPS)} \rightarrow \text{MRP} \rightarrow \text{Closed-Loop MRP} \rightarrow \text{MRP II} \rightarrow \text{ERP}
$$

- **OPS:** Re-order point systems for independent demand items.
- **MRP (Material Requirements Planning):** Dependent demand, BOM explosion, lot-sizing.
- **Closed-Loop MRP:** Adds capacity feedback and execution control.
- **MRP II:** Adds financial, shop-floor, and sales integration.
- **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning):** Extends beyond manufacturing to HR, CRM, SCM.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of MRP II

The system functions in **four operational phases**:

1. **Demand Planning**
   - Pulls from *Sales Forecasts* + *Customer Orders* → Master Production Schedule (MPS).
   - MPS states *what finished products* are required, *how many*, and *when*.
2. **Material Planning**
   - BOM explosion converts MPS into Gross Requirements for every component.
   - Netting and lot-sizing produce Planned Order Releases.
3. **Capacity Planning**
   - CRP compares load to capacity at each work center using routing data.
   - Output: Rescheduling recommendations to balance load.
4. **Execution & Feedback**
   - Shop Floor Control (SFC) executes the plan.
   - Actual performance is fed back, closing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The word **"closed-loop"** in the KTU answer-script vocabulary refers specifically to the *feedback arrow from execution back into the planning layer*. Always draw this arrow explicitly in diagrams — it is a marker of full credit.

### 2.3 Key Inputs and Outputs of MRP II

| Element | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Master Production Schedule (MPS) | Input | What, how many, when to produce |
| Bill of Materials (BOM) | Input | Product structure tree |
| Inventory Status File | Input | On-hand, on-order, allocated |
| Routing File | Input | Operation sequence & work-center times |
| Gross Requirements | Output | Total demand for an item |
| Net Requirements | Output | After subtracting on-hand inventory |
| Planned Order Releases | Output | Scheduled factory orders |
| Exception Reports | Output | Deviations needing planner attention |

### 2.4 MRP II Lot-Sizing Techniques (KTU Exam Favorites)

| Technique | Formula / Logic | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Order exactly the net requirement | Low holding cost, high setup cost relative to item value |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | Constant, independent demand |
| **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | Order to cover a fixed number of periods' demand | Smooths orders across periods |
| **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** | Balance setup cost with carrying cost | Setup cost is significant |

> Where:
> - $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
> - $S$ = Ordering/setup cost per order (₹/order)
> - $H$ = Holding/carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit-year)

---

## Part B — TOPP Architecture in Depth

### 2.5 The Three-Tier Planning Logic of TOPP

TOPP operates on a **hierarchical decomposition** principle. Each tier refines the previous tier into a more actionable plan.

**Tier 1 — Annual Production Plan:**
- Combines sales forecast, capacity limits, and profit targets.
- Output: Rough monthly volumes per model family.
- Used for long-term capacity decisions (e.g., whether to add a second shift).

**Tier 2 — Monthly Production Plan:**
- Determines the specific model mix each month (e.g., how many sedans vs. hatchbacks).
- Aligns with marketing campaigns and dealer orders.
- Output: A monthly build schedule per line.

**Tier 3 — Daily Plan (Heijunka & Pitch Time):**
- Breaks monthly plan into **leveled daily sequences**.
- Sequence is dictated by **takt time** and **pitch time**.

### 2.6 Core TOPP Calculations (KTU Must-Know Formulas)

$$
\text{Takt Time} \; T_{takt} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}
$$

$$
\text{Cycle Time} \; T_{cycle} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Number of Units Required per Day}}
$$

$$
\text{Pitch Time} \; T_{pitch} = \text{Takt Time} \times \text{Pitch Interval (number of units per batch)}
$$

$$
\text{Lead Time} \; T_{lead} = \text{Pre-Processing Time} + \text{Processing Time} + \text{Waiting Time} + \text{Storage Time} + \text{Post-Processing Time}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trap:** Many students write **Takt Time = Cycle Time**. They are **NOT the same**. Takt Time = *the rate at which a product must be produced to satisfy demand*. Cycle Time = *the rate at which a workstation actually produces*. The relationship is: $T_{cycle} < T_{takt}$ for a feasible system.

### 2.7 Heijunka — The Heart of TOPP

**Heijunka (平準化)** means *production leveling*. It is the practice of **smoothing the production mix and volume over time** to avoid:
- Inventory pile-ups (over-production → muda),
- Starvation of downstream stations (under-production),
- Excessive changeover strain on equipment.

Heijunka is operationalized through a **Heijunka Box (Leveling Board)**, which sequences production orders based on:
- Model variant,
- Quantity per pitch,
- Daily demand pattern.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

| System | Industry Best Suited | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **MRP II** | Discrete batch manufacturing with complex BOMs (aerospace, industrial machinery, electronics) | BOM explosion, dependent demand, finite capacity checks are essential |
| **TOPP** | High-volume repetitive assembly (automotive, consumer electronics, FMCG) | Leveled flow minimizes waste and inventory; Kanban signals are economical |
| **Hybrid** | Job shops with mixed flow (contract manufacturers) | MRP II for materials, Heijunka + Kanban for bottleneck lines |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 MRP II Derivation — Net Requirement Computation

### Setting Up the Logic

Let:
- $GR_t$ = Gross Requirement of item $i$ in period $t$
- $OH_{t-1}$ = On-Hand Inventory at the end of period $t-1$
- $SR_t$ = Scheduled Receipts in period $t$
- $SS$ = Safety Stock (constant for the planning horizon)
- $NR_t$ = Net Requirement in period $t$
- $POR_t$ = Planned Order Receipt in period $t$
- $POC_t$ = Planned Order Release in period $t$
- $LT$ = Lead Time (in periods)

The complete derivation chain in MRP II proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Net Requirement Calculation:**

$$
NR_t = GR_t + SS - OH_{t-1} - SR_t
$$

If $NR_t \le 0$, set $NR_t = 0$ (MRP does not plan negative net requirements — it means we have surplus inventory).

**Step 2 — Planned Order Receipt (POR):**

The POR is sized to the lot-sizing rule. For **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**:

$$
POR_t = NR_t
$$

For **EOQ** lot-sizing, if cumulative demand since last order release reaches $Q^*$, an order of size $Q^*$ is triggered.

**Step 3 — Projected On-Hand Inventory Update:**

$$
OH_t = OH_{t-1} + POR_t - GR_t
$$

**Step 4 — Planned Order Release (POC):**

$$
POC_t = POR_{t+LT}
$$

That is, the release happens $LT$ periods **before** the receipt is needed.

### Worked Numerical Example (L4L Method)

A finished product requires 2 units of component $X$. The BOM is single-level for simplicity. The MPS, inventory, and lead time are:

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement (units of X) | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 30 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Safety Stock | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Lead Time | 1 period |  |  |  |  |
| Lot-Sizing Rule | Lot-for-Lot |  |  |  |  |

Assume $OH_0 = 30$ (on-hand at start of period 1).

**Period 1:**
$$
NR_1 = GR_1 + SS - OH_0 - SR_1 = 0 + 10 - 30 - 0 = -20 \Rightarrow 0
$$
$$
OH_1 = OH_0 + POR_1 - GR_1 = 30 + 0 - 0 = 30
$$

**Period 2:**
$$
NR_2 = 0 + 10 - 30 - 0 = -20 \Rightarrow 0
$$
$$
OH_2 = 30
$$

**Period 3:**
$$
NR_3 = 50 + 10 - 30 - 0 = 30
$$
$$
POR_3 = NR_3 = 30 \quad (\text{L4L})
$$
$$
OH_3 = 30 + 30 - 50 = 10
$$

**Period 4:**
$$
NR_4 = 0 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 0
$$
$$
OH_4 = 10
$$

**Period 5:**
$$
NR_5 = 30 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 30
$$
$$
POR_5 = 30
$$
$$
OH_5 = 10 + 30 - 30 = 10
$$

**Planned Order Releases** (back-shift by $LT = 1$ period):

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Planned Order Release | 0 | **30** | 0 | **30** | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 30 | 0 | 30 |

> [!NOTE]
> The complete MRP II table typically contains **seven rows**: Gross Requirements, Scheduled Receipts, Projected On-Hand, Net Requirements, Planned Order Receipts, Planned Order Releases, and Lead Time. KTU answer scripts lose marks when rows are missing.

---

## 3.2 MRP II — CRP and Capacity Feasibility Derivation

Capacity Requirements Planning ensures the **released orders can be executed** by available work centers.

Let:
- $WC_j$ = Work Center $j$
- $C_j$ = Available capacity of $WC_j$ (hours/day)
- $L_{ij}$ = Load on $WC_j$ from order $i$ (hours) = Operation time per unit × Planned Order Quantity

**Step 1 — Load Calculation:**

$$
L_{ij} = ST_{ij} \times Q_i
$$

where $ST_{ij}$ is standard time for one unit of order $i$ on $WC_j$, and $Q_i$ is the planned order quantity.

**Step 2 — Total Load on Work Center:**

$$
L_j^{total} = \sum_{i} L_{ij}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity Utilization:**

$$
U_j = \frac{L_j^{total}}{C_j} \times 100 \%
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If $U_j \le 100\%$, the plan is **feasible**.
- If $U_j > 100\%$, reschedule orders, add shifts, or subcontract — close the loop back to MRP.

---

## 3.3 TOPP Derivation — Takt, Pitch, and Line Balance

### Problem Setup

A Toyota assembly line operates **480 minutes/day** (two 8-hour shifts minus breaks). Customer demand is **240 cars/day**. The line must produce 4 models in a leveled sequence.

**Step 1 — Takt Time:**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{480 \; \text{min/day}}{240 \; \text{cars/day}} = 2 \; \text{minutes/car}
$$

This means **one car must roll off the line every 2 minutes** to satisfy demand.

**Step 2 — Pitch Time (for a pitch interval of 8 cars per batch):**

$$
T_{pitch} = T_{takt} \times 8 = 2 \times 8 = 16 \; \text{minutes/batch}
$$

Every 16 minutes, a new batch of 8 cars (with a leveled model mix) begins production.

**Step 3 — Daily Production Target (Verification):**

$$
Q_{day} = \frac{480}{2} = 240 \; \text{cars}
$$

Demand matches capacity — the plan is feasible.

**Step 4 — Heijunka Sequence (Example):**

If daily demand is $A = 60$, $B = 80$, $C = 50$, $D = 50$ (total = 240), the sequence must be interleaved so that each pitch interval contains an **equal proportion** of models. A simple leveled pattern for one pitch of 8 cars:

$$
A, B, A, C, B, D, A, B
$$

This repetition (called *repetition pattern* in TOPP) is repeated $\frac{240}{8} = 30$ times throughout the day, achieving **Heijunka**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why level?** Suppose we batched: 60 A's first, then 80 B's, etc. The line would experience:
> - High work-in-process inventory during batch production,
> - Operator over-load at start, idle at end,
> - Quality problems (delayed defect detection).
> Leveling eliminates these muda (waste) categories.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MRP II vs. TOPP

| Dimension | MRP II | TOPP |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Western (USA, 1970s, Oliver Wight) | Japanese (Toyota, 1950s–60s) |
| Logic Direction | **Push** (forecast-driven) | **Pull** (demand-driven) |
| Information Trigger | Time-phased schedule (MPS) | Visual signal (Kanban card / Andon) |
| Lot Size Philosophy | Optimized economic lots | **Small lots, frequent replenishment** |
| Inventory Posture | Inventory is *planned* and *buffered* | Inventory is a *waste* (muda) — minimized |
| Capacity Handling | CRP with finite/infinite capacity | Designed-in balance (line design) |
| Lead Time Stance | Schedule-based (build to forecast) | Reduced via SMED, setup elimination |
| Best Fit | High-variety, low-volume (BOM-heavy) | High-volume, low-variety (repetitive) |
| Weakness | Bullwhip effect, high WIP, slow response | Fragile under high variety or demand spikes |
| KTU Exam Tag | "Discuss MRP II architecture" | "Explain the TOPP model with Heijunka" |

## 4.1 MRP II — Closed-Loop Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of MRP II, showing how inputs, planning engines, and feedback loops interact. (Per KTU protocol, node IDs are alphanumeric only; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SALES FORECAST] --> A2[MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE MPS]
    A3[CUSTOMER ORDERS] --> A2
    A4[BILL OF MATERIALS BOM] --> A5[MRP ENGINE]
    A6[INVENTORY STATUS FILE] --> A5
    A7[ROUTING FILE] --> A8[CAPACITY REQUIREMENTS PLANNING CRP]
    A2 --> A5
    A5 --> A9[PLANNED ORDER RELEASES]
    A5 --> A10[EXCEPTION REPORTS]
    A9 --> A8
    A8 --> A11{CAPACITY FEASIBLE}
    A11 -- YES --> A12[SHOPSHOP FLOOR CONTROL SFC]
    A11 -- NO --> A2
    A12 --> A13[EXECUTION AND FEEDBACK]
    A13 --> A14[PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT]
    A14 --> A15[FINANCIAL INTEGRATION]
    A15 --> A2
    A14 --> A5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from `A14` back to `A2` represents the **closed-loop feedback** — the very feature that distinguishes MRP II from plain MRP. The arrow from `A14` back to `A5` represents the **exception-driven replanning** path.

## 4.2 TOPP — Three-Tier Planning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[ANNUAL DEMAND FORECAST] --> B2[TIER 1 ANNUAL PRODUCTION PLAN]
    B2 --> B3{CAPACITY CHECK}
    B3 -- FEASIBLE --> B4[TIER 2 MONTHLY PRODUCTION PLAN]
    B3 -- NOT FEASIBLE --> B2
    B4 --> B5[MODEL MIX AND VOLUME]
    B5 --> B6[TIER 3 DAILY LEVELING HEIJUNKA]
    B6 --> B7[TAKT TIME COMPUTATION]
    B7 --> B8[PITCH TIME AND REPETITION PATTERN]

    B10 --> B11[ASSEMBLY LINE EXECUTION]

    B12 --> B2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When drawing TOPP in the answer sheet, always show the **descending arrow (forecast → annual → monthly → daily)** and the **ascending feedback arrow (shop floor → monthly)**. Examiners reward diagrams that capture the **bi-directional flow**.

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Block Diagram — MRP II vs. TOPP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
