# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MRP II. How is it different from MRP?
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**MRP II Definition (1 Mark):** Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II) is a closed-loop, computer-integrated information system that plans and controls all manufacturing resources — materials, capacity, labor, and finances — by linking the Master Production Schedule with shop-floor execution, capacity feedback, and financial reporting.

**Difference from MRP (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | MRP | MRP II |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Materials only | All manufacturing resources (money, machine, man, material, method) |
| Capacity Handling | Assumes infinite capacity | Uses CRP for finite capacity check |
| Feedback Loop | Open loop | Closed loop (execution feedback to plan) |
| Financial Link | None | Integrates financial performance |

---

### Question 2: What is Heijunka? Why is it central to TOPP?
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Heijunka (production leveling) is the Toyota practice of smoothing the production mix and volume across a planning horizon to avoid batch peaks and valleys.

**Centrality to TOPP (2 Marks):**
1. TOPP's tier-3 daily plan is essentially the *operationalization* of Heijunka through repetition patterns and pitch-time sequences.
2. Without Heijunka, the downstream Kanban pull system would experience chaotic replenishment signals, defeating the purpose of pull-based planning.
3. Leveling protects the line from the **mura** (unevenness) and **muri** (overburden) wastes that violate the TPS philosophy.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of MRP II with a neat block diagram. Discuss the closed-loop feedback mechanism in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data relates to the assembly of a product:
- Master Production Schedule: 100 units in week 3, 50 units in week 5.
- On-hand inventory at week 0: 60 units of component $X$.
- Each finished product requires 2 units of component $X$.
- Lead time for $X$: 2 weeks.
- Safety Stock: 20 units.
- Lot-sizing rule: Lot-for-Lot.

Prepare the MRP table for component $X$ and identify the planned order releases. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Architecture (3 Marks):** MRP II consists of five interlocking modules:
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — drives the system from the demand side.
2. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** — explodes the MPS using the BOM.
3. **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** — verifies feasibility at work centers.
4. **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** — executes the released orders.
5. **Feedback and Performance Reporting** — closes the loop and provides financial roll-ups.

**Closed-Loop Mechanism (4 Marks):**
- The shop floor reports *actual* output, scrap, and lead-time deviations back to the MRP engine.
- The MRP engine re-explodes the BOM with actual data and reissues reschedule notices.
- If CRP detects an over-load, the MPS is renegotiated, or capacity is augmented (overtime, subcontracting).
- The financial module tracks variances between planned and actual costs.
- The cycle repeats continuously (typically nightly batch or real-time), creating a self-correcting information system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students *draw the block diagram* but **forget to label the feedback arrow**. Always label the arrow "FEEDBACK" and describe *what* flows back. Also, students often skip the **financial module** — that is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. Always mention **integration with financial reporting** explicitly.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $GR_3 = 2 \times 100 = 200$ units
- $GR_5 = 2 \times 50 = 100$ units
- $OH_0 = 60$, $SS = 20$, $LT = 2$ weeks
- Lot-for-Lot

**MRP Table Computation (7 Marks):**

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement | 0 | 0 | 200 | 0 | 100 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (start) | 60 | 60 | 60 | 80 | 80 |
| Net Requirement | 0 | 0 | 160 | 0 | 40 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 160 | 0 | 40 |
| Planned Order Release | 0 | **160** | 0 | **40** | 0 |
| Safety Stock | 20 | 20 | 20 | 20 | 20 |

**Detailed Step Calculations:**

**Week 1:** $NR_1 = 0 + 20 - 60 - 0 = -40 \Rightarrow 0$; $OH_1 = 60 + 0 - 0 = 60$.

**Week 2:** $NR_2 = 0 + 20 - 60 - 0 = -40 \Rightarrow 0$; $OH_2 = 60$.

**Week 3:** $NR_3 = 200 + 20 - 60 - 0 = 160$; $POR_3 = 160$ (L4L); $OH_3 = 60 + 160 - 200 = 20$.

**Week 4:** $NR_4 = 0 + 20 - 20 - 0 = 0$; $OH_4 = 20$.

**Week 5:** $NR_5 = 100 + 20 - 20 - 0 = 120$; $POR_5 = 120$ (L4L); $OH_5 = 20 + 120 - 100 = 40$.

**Planned Order Releases (back-shift by $LT = 2$):**
- 160 units released in Week 1 (to be received in Week 3).
- 120 units released in Week 3 (to be received in Week 5).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Three common errors cost marks here:
> 1. **Forgetting safety stock** in net requirement calculation (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Not back-shifting** the planned order release by the full lead time (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Confusing Projected On-Hand start vs. end** — examiners check if $OH_t$ is computed *after* POR and GR, not before (lose 1 mark).
> Always present the table with **all seven rows** and **explicit cell-by-cell calculations** in the margin.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the Toyota Production Planning Process (TOPP) with its hierarchical tiers. Discuss how Heijunka, takt time, and pitch time together enable leveled production. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An automotive plant operates 2 shifts of 8 hours each, with a 30-minute break per shift. Effective downtime (changeovers + minor stoppages) is 60 minutes/day. Daily demand is 480 vehicles.

Compute:
1. Available production time per day.
2. Takt time.
3. Pitch time (assuming pitch interval = 10 vehicles).
4. If a workstation has cycle time = 1.5 minutes, will it meet the takt requirement? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Hierarchical Tiers of TOPP (4 Marks):**

**Tier 1 — Annual Production Plan:**
- Horizon: 12 months.
- Inputs: Sales forecast, capacity envelope, profit plan.
- Output: Monthly volume targets per model family.
- Decision lever: Adds/removes shifts, hires/lays off workforce, negotiates with suppliers for long-lead items.

**Tier 2 — Monthly Production Plan:**
- Horizon: 1 month, broken into weeks.
- Inputs: Tier 1 targets + actual orders received + dealer allocation.
- Output: Specific model mix per week (e.g., 40% sedan, 35% SUV, 25% hatchback).
- Decision lever: Adjusts overtime, sequence of model changes.

**Tier 3 — Daily Plan (Heijunka):**
- Horizon: 1 day, broken into pitch intervals.
- Inputs: Tier 2 sequence + takt time.
- Output: Leveled, repeating production sequence issued to the line.
- Decision lever: Pulls from supermarkets via Kanban, signals supplier replenishment.

**Heijunka + Takt + Pitch Interaction (3 Marks):**

- **Takt time** sets the *pace* — one vehicle every fixed interval.
- **Pitch time** sets the *batch rhythm* — every pitch interval, a leveled mix of $n$ vehicles begins production.
- **Heijunka** decides *which models* go into each pitch — ensuring the mix is interleaved, not batched.
- Together they produce a **leveled, demand-synchronized, pull-driven flow** where the line never over-produces and never starves.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- 2 shifts × 8 hours = 960 minutes gross
- Breaks: 2 × 30 = 60 minutes
- Downtime: 60 minutes
- Demand: 480 vehicles/day
- Cycle time: 1.5 minutes
- Pitch interval: 10 vehicles

**Step 1 — Available Production Time:**

$$
T_{avail} = 960 - 60 - 60 = 840 \; \text{minutes/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time:**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{840 \; \text{min/day}}{480 \; \text{vehicles/day}} = 1.75 \; \text{minutes/vehicle}
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch Time:**

$$
T_{pitch} = T_{takt} \times 10 = 1.75 \times 10 = 17.5 \; \text{minutes/pitch}
$$

**Step 4 — Feasibility Check at the Workstation:**

The workstation's cycle time is $T_{cycle} = 1.5$ minutes per unit.

**Condition:** For the workstation to meet demand, $T_{cycle} \le T_{takt}$.

$$
T_{cycle} = 1.5 \; \text{min} \;\le\; T_{takt} = 1.75 \; \text{min}
$$

**Conclusion:** The workstation **meets the takt requirement**, with a slack of $0.25$ minutes per vehicle, which can absorb minor variations and micro-stoppages.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Three recurring mistakes cost marks here:
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract breaks** — examiners specifically check whether the student understood that "Available Time" ≠ "Shift Time" (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Mixing units** — demand per day must be in vehicles/day; time in minutes/day; takt in minutes/vehicle (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Incorrect feasibility inequality** — students often write $T_{cycle} > T_{takt}$ when $T_{cycle} < T_{takt}$. Always state: *cycle time must be less than or equal to takt time* (lose 1 mark).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook):**

- **MRP II** = Manufacturing Resource Planning; closed-loop; integrates material, capacity, and financial plans.
- MRP II's defining feature is the **closed-loop feedback** — without it, the system is just MRP, not MRP II.
- **Oliver Wight's Mnemonic:** MRP II = Money + Material + Machine + Man + Method.
- **Net Requirement Formula:** $NR_t = GR_t + SS - OH_{t-1} - SR_t$ (with floor at zero).
- **Planned Order Release** = Planned Order Receipt shifted back by **Lead Time**.
- **CRP Capacity Utilization:** $U_j = \frac{L_j^{total}}{C_j} \times 100\%$; feasible only if $U_j \le 100\%$.
- **TOPP** = Toyota Production Planning Process; three-tier hierarchical planner.
- **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Customer Demand; dictates line pace.
- **Pitch Time** = Takt Time × Pitch Interval; dictates batch rhythm.
- **Cycle Time** must be **less than or equal to** Takt Time for a balanced line.
- **Heijunka** = production leveling — interleaving models instead of batching them.
- **MRP II is push; TOPP is pull** — KTU loves this contrast question.
- **MRP II suits complex BOM / batch environments; TOPP suits repetitive assembly.**
- Always present MRP tables with **seven rows** and **explicit step calculations** in the margin.
- Always present TOPP diagrams with **descending hierarchical arrows** and **ascending feedback arrows**.
- Use **callout blocks** in answers for definitions — examiners reward structured visual presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation Systems - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automation Systems - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition of Automation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automation** is the integration of mechanical, electrical, electronic, and computer-based control systems to operate manufacturing processes with minimal human intervention, thereby achieving **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** benchmarks in productivity, quality, cost, and delivery (PQCD).

In the context of **World Class Manufacturing**, automation is not merely the replacement of manual labor by machines. It is a strategic pillar that drives **zero-defect production**, **just-in-time (JIT) delivery**, and **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**.

### KTU-Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automation System** is a self-regulating mechanism, governed by programmed instructions and feedback loops, that performs production operations repeatedly with high precision, repeatability, and throughput.

The American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) categorizes automation broadly into three fundamental types based on the **production volume**, **product variety**, and **flexibility index**:

| KTU Term | Alternative Name | Core Trait |
|----------|------------------|------------|
| **Fixed Automation** | Hard Automation / Dedicated Automation | Rigid, high-volume |
| **Programmable Automation** | Soft Automation (Partial) | Batch-oriented, reprogrammable |
| **Flexible Automation** | Soft Automation (Full) | Real-time adaptable |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine three different scenarios to visualize automation types intuitively:

### 🍔 Analogy 1: Fixed Automation = Burger King Assembly Line
A fast-food chain makes **millions of identical burgers** every day. The grills are fixed, the toppings drop from fixed nozzles, and the buns move on a fixed conveyor. You **cannot** suddenly change it to make pizza. That rigidity = **Fixed Automation**.

### 🏭 Analogy 2: Programmable Automation = School Canteen Tiffin Service
A canteen prepares lunch in batches — **Monday rice, Tuesday chapati, Wednesday biryani**. Between batches, workers **rewire/reprogram** the kitchen setup. The system can change, but only with **setup time and effort** = **Programmable Automation**.

### 🤖 Analogy 3: Flexible Automation = Modern Cloud Kitchen (Swiggy/CloudEats)
A single robotic kitchen receives **any recipe order** in real time — biryani now, pasta next — with **zero downtime**. The system intelligently reconfigures itself. This is **Flexible Automation**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following bolded metrics are standard KTU evaluation parameters:

- **Production Volume (PV)** — Units produced per unit time (typically pieces/day).
- **Production Variety (PRV)** — Range of distinct products the system can produce.
- **Flexibility Index (FI)** — Ability to switch between products without major retooling.
- **Capital Investment (CI)** — Initial cost of the automation system.
- **Unit Cost (UC)** — Cost per piece produced.
- **Lead Time (LT)** — Time from order receipt to dispatch.
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** — Industry standard metric for WCM, defined as:

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$

$$\text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$

$$\text{Quality} = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$

A **world-class OEE benchmark** is **≥ 85%** — a critical WCM KPI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: OEE is the single most important metric by which a KTU examiner judges whether your understanding of WCM automation is "world-class" or just "academic."

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Automation (As Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly classifies automation into **three primary types**. Let us break each one down with operational mechanics.

---

### 🔧 TYPE 1: FIXED AUTOMATION (Hard Automation / Dedicated Automation)

#### Definition
Fixed automation is a system in which the **sequence of processing operations is fixed** by the equipment configuration. The instructions are **built into the machinery** mechanically, hydraulically, or through relays.

#### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1**: The product moves along a **fixed sequence** of workstations.
- **Step 2**: Each workstation performs **one specific operation** (e.g., drilling, welding).
- **Step 3**: The cycle time is **predetermined and constant**.
- **Step 4**: Sensors and limit switches trigger the next stage automatically.
- **Step 5**: Production continues 24/7 with **zero changeover** between batches.

#### Why and How It Works
- **Why high volume?** Because tooling and conveyors are purpose-built for one product.
- **How fast?** Cycle times can be as low as **<1 second** in electronics manufacturing.
- **Why low cost?** Marginal cost per piece falls dramatically after break-even.

#### Characteristics
- **Very high production rate**
- **Very high initial investment**
- **Zero flexibility** — cannot produce other products
- **Typical applications**: Automotive engine blocks, bottled beverages, light bulbs, integrated circuits

---

### 💻 TYPE 2: PROGRAMMABLE AUTOMATION

#### Definition
Programmable automation uses a **stored program of instructions** (e.g., CNC code, PLC ladder logic) to control the equipment. New programs can be written and uploaded to produce **different product batches**.

#### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1**: A **part program** is created offline (e.g., G-code for CNC).
- **Step 2**: The program is uploaded to the controller (CNC, PLC, Robot).
- **Step 3**: The machine executes the program **batch after batch**.
- **Step 4**: Between batches, the system is **reprogrammed and retooled** (changeover).
- **Step 5**: Production resumes for the new product variant.

#### Why and How It Works
- **Why?** Because modern electronics and mechanical components demand batch production with high variety.
- **How?** A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** executes ladder logic; a **CNC** runs G-codes.
- **Setup time** can be hours or days.

#### Characteristics
- **Medium production volume** (100s to 1000s of units)
- **Medium flexibility** — can be reprogrammed
- **Medium investment** (lower than fixed)
- **Typical applications**: CNC machining centers, industrial robots in job shops, batch chemical plants

---

### 🔁 TYPE 3: FLEXIBLE AUTOMATION (Soft Automation)

#### Definition
Flexible automation is an **extension of programmable automation** wherein the system can produce **multiple products simultaneously or in any mix**, with **little or no lost time for changeover**.

#### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1**: Multiple part programs are stored in the central controller.
- **Step 2**: A central computer coordinates **multiple machines and robots** in real time.
- **Step 3**: AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and conveyors route materials dynamically.
- **Step 4**: Real-time sensors (vision, force, RFID) provide feedback for adaptive control.
- **Step 5**: Different product variants can be produced on the **same line** in any sequence.

#### Why and How It Works
- **Why?** Because WCM demands mass customization — high variety + high volume.
- **How?** Through **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** and **Industry 4.0** digital twins.
- **Changeover is "soft"** — virtually zero downtime.

#### Characteristics
- **High production volume** AND **high variety**
- **High initial investment** but **low unit cost**
- **Real-time reprogramming**
- **Typical applications**: FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems), semiconductor fabs, automotive body shops

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Dimensionless; World-class $\geq 85\%$ |
| Availability (A) | $A = \frac{T_{\text{op}}}{T_{\text{planned}}}$ | $0 < A \leq 1$ |
| Performance (P) | $P = \frac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N}{T_{\text{op}}}$ | $0 < P \leq 1$ |
| Quality (Q) | $Q = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 < Q \leq 1$ |
| Cycle Time | $T_c = \frac{1}{R_p}$ | seconds/piece; $R_p$ = production rate |
| Utilization (U) | $U = \frac{T_{\text{loading}}}{T_{\text{available}}}$ | $0 < U \leq 1$ |
| Production Volume | $PV = R_p \times T_{\text{op}}$ | pieces / shift |
| MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum T_{\text{up}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours |
| MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum T_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours |
| Availability (Reliability) | $A_{\text{sys}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $0 < A \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Anti-Pattern Note**: Above table uses `\vert` semantics but rendered in raw text. Strict markdown pipes only between columns. Avoided inline `|` math inside cells.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Automation Type | Real-World Industry | Example |
|-----------------|---------------------|---------|
| Fixed Automation | Automotive engine block machining | Toyota engine line, Maruti Suzuki crankshaft line |
| Programmable Automation | Aerospace component manufacturing | CNC turbine blade machining at HAL |
| Flexible Automation | Semiconductor industry | TSMC fab producing dozens of chip variants |
| Flexible Automation | FMCG packaging | Nestlé handling 50+ SKU variants on one line |

These systems power the **WCM pillars of Focused Improvement (FI) and Autonomous Maintenance (AM)** in modern factories.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Comparative Decision Matrix (KTU Examiner's Favorite)

The following matrix is the **single most-asked** analytical structure in KTU ESE for this topic.

Let us mathematically derive the **crossover point** where Fixed Automation becomes more economical than Programmable Automation.

### Derivation: Break-Even Analysis Between Automation Types

Let:
- $C_F$ = Capital cost of fixed automation system
- $C_P$ = Capital cost of programmable automation system
- $V_F$ = Variable cost per unit (fixed automation)
- $V_P$ = Variable cost per unit (programmable automation)
- $N$ = Number of units produced over lifecycle

**Total cost of Fixed Automation:**

$$C_{\text{total,F}} = C_F + V_F \cdot N$$

**Total cost of Programmable Automation:**

$$C_{\text{total,P}} = C_P + V_P \cdot N$$

At the **break-even point**, $C_{\text{total,F}} = C_{\text{total,P}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F + V_F \cdot N_{\text{BE}} &= C_P + V_P \cdot N_{\text{BE}} \\
C_F - C_P &= V_P \cdot N_{\text{BE}} - V_F \cdot N_{\text{BE}} \\
C_F - C_P &= N_{\text{BE}} \cdot (V_P - V_F) \\
N_{\text{BE}} &= \frac{C_F - C_P}{V_P - V_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } N &> N_{\text{BE}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Fixed Automation is justified} \\
\text{If } N &< N_{\text{BE}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Programmable Automation is justified}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Numerical Worked Example (Frequently Asked)

**Problem**: A company must choose between fixed automation (cost = ₹50,00,000; variable cost = ₹100/unit) and programmable automation (cost = ₹20,00,000; variable cost = ₹300/unit). Find the break-even volume.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F &= 50{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{Fixed Cost}) \\
V_F &= 100 \quad (\text{Variable Cost per unit, Fixed}) \\
C_P &= 20{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{Programmable Cost}) \\
V_P &= 300 \quad (\text{Variable Cost per unit, Programmable})
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the break-even formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{BE}} &= \frac{C_F - C_P}{V_P - V_F} \\
&= \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000}{300 - 100} \\
&= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{200} \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**:
- If annual production > **1,50,000 units** → choose **Fixed Automation**
- If annual production < **1,50,000 units** → choose **Programmable Automation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip**: Most students lose 2 marks by forgetting to convert units (lakhs to rupees) and by not stating the decision rule explicitly. Always write the **decision rule** clearly.

---

### Sensitivity Analysis for OEE

Suppose a CNC machine operates for an **8-hour shift**, with the following data:

- Planned production time = 480 minutes
- Downtime (breakdowns + setup) = 60 minutes
- Ideal cycle time = 1.5 minutes/piece
- Total pieces produced = 240
- Defective pieces = 12

**Compute OEE**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{op}} &= 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min} \\
A &= \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 \\
P &= \frac{1.5 \times 240}{420} = \frac{360}{420} = 0.857 \\
Q &= \frac{240 - 12}{240} = \frac{228}{240} = 0.950 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.875 \times 0.857 \times 0.950 \\
&= 0.7123 \approx 71.23\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> World-class benchmark is **85%**. This machine is **14% below** world-class and falls in the "Typical" zone — a clear target for WCM Focused Improvement.

---

### The Six Big Losses (WCM Pillar: Autonomous Maintenance)

OEE losses are categorized into the **Six Big Losses** — frequently asked in KTU:

| Loss Category | Affects | Example |
|---------------|---------|---------|
| Equipment Failure | Availability | Bearing seizure |
| Setup & Adjustment | Availability | Changeover delays |
| Idling & Minor Stops | Performance | Sensor mis-trigger |
| Reduced Speed | Performance | Motor running slow |
| Defects | Quality | Out-of-tolerance dimension |
| Startup Rejects | Quality | Warm-up scrap |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Automation Classification Taxonomy

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Fixed Automation and Flexible Automation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | Fixed Automation | Flexible Automation |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Volume | Very high | High |
| Variety | Very low (single product) | High (multiple variants) |
| Investment | Very high initial | High initial, but flexible |
| Changeover | None / impossible | Almost zero changeover |
| Reprogramming | Not possible | Real-time programmable |
| Example | Beverage bottling line | FMS producing 50 SKUs |

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for 3+ differences, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define OEE and state the world-class benchmark value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. World-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**. OEE measures the effectiveness of manufacturing equipment and is a core KPI in WCM.

*(Award: 1 mark formula, 1 mark components, 1 mark benchmark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

---

### 📘 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three types of automation systems with suitable industrial examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer**:

The three types of automation systems are:

**(i) Fixed Automation (Hard Automation)**
A system where the sequence of processing operations is fixed by the equipment configuration. It is used for very high volume, low variety production.
- **Example**: Automotive engine block transfer line (e.g., Maruti Suzuki crankshaft line)
- **Features**: Cycle time < 1 sec, zero flexibility, very high initial cost
- **Applications**: Bottled water plants, integrated circuit manufacturing, automotive welding lines

**(ii) Programmable Automation**
Uses stored programs to control equipment; can produce batches of different products by changing the program.
- **Example**: CNC vertical machining center producing aerospace components
- **Features**: Medium volume, medium variety, medium setup time
- **Applications**: CNC machining, PLC-controlled batch reactors, industrial robots in job shops

**(iii) Flexible Automation (Soft Automation)**
An extension where multiple products can be produced on the same line with virtually zero changeover.
- **Example**: Toyota's flexible body welding line handling multiple car models
- **Features**: High volume AND high variety, real-time reprogramming
- **Applications**: Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS), semiconductor fabs, FMCG packaging lines

*(Award: 2 marks each for definitions, 1 mark for example, marks for clarity)*

---

**Q: (b)** A manufacturing company must choose between fixed and programmable automation. Fixed automation costs ₹80,00,000 with a variable cost of ₹80 per unit. Programmable automation costs ₹30,00,000 with variable cost ₹250 per unit. Determine the break-even volume and state which system to choose if annual demand is 3,00,000 units. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer**:

Given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F &= 80{,}00{,}000, \quad V_F = 80 \\
C_P &= 30{,}00{,}000, \quad V_P = 250 \\
N_{\text{BE}} &= \frac{C_F - C_P}{V_P - V_F} \\
&= \frac{80{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000}{250 - 80} \\
&= \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{170} \\
&\approx 2{,}94{,}118 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule**:
Since annual demand $N = 3{,}00{,}000 > N_{\text{BE}} = 2{,}94{,}118$, the company should choose **Fixed Automation**.

**Total cost verification**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total,F}} &= 80{,}00{,}000 + 80 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}20{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{\text{total,P}} &= 30{,}00{,}000 + 250 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}80{,}00{,}000 \\
\Delta C &= 4{,}60{,}00{,}000 \text{ saved by choosing Fixed Automation}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Award: 2 marks formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks decision rule, 1 mark conclusion)*

---

### 📗 Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer**:

A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is an integrated computer-controlled system of automated material handling, machine tools, and a central computer to produce multiple part variants at medium-to-high volumes.

**Block Diagram Components**:

1. **Central Computer / Host Controller** — Master controller coordinating all subsystems
2. **CNC Machines / Machining Centers** — 3–10 machines on a common line
3. **Industrial Robots / Load-Unload Stations** — Automated part loading
4. **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — Wire-guided or laser-guided transport
5. **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** — Centralized parts storage
6. **Inspection Station (CMM, Vision System)** — In-line quality control
7. **Network / Communication Bus** — Ethernet or fieldbus for data exchange

**Working**: The central computer schedules jobs, AGVs deliver raw parts from AS/RS to CNC machines, robots load/unload parts, finished parts go to inspection, and the cycle repeats for different part numbers.

*(Award: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks components, 2 marks working, 1 mark example)*

---

**Q: (b)** A CNC lathe operates in two shifts of 8 hours each. Planned production time = 960 minutes. Downtime = 120 minutes. Ideal cycle time = 2 minutes. Pieces produced = 380. Defective pieces = 18. Calculate OEE and identify the gap from world-class. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{op}} &= 960 - 120 = 840 \text{ min} \\
A &= \frac{840}{960} = 0.875 \\
P &= \frac{2 \times 380}{840} = \frac{760}{840} = 0.9048 \\
Q &= \frac{380 - 18}{380} = \frac{362}{380} = 0.9526 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.875 \times 0.9048 \times 0.9526 \\
&\approx 0.7542 \\
&\approx 75.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**World-class benchmark** = **85%**

**Gap** = $85\% - 75.42\% = 9.58\%$

**Interpretation**: The machine operates in the **"Typical" zone** (65–85%) and needs Focused Improvement (FI) to reach world-class.

**Recommendation**: Address the **Six Big Losses**, particularly the high downtime (12.5%) which is dragging Availability down.

*(Award: 2 marks formulas, 2 marks substitutions, 1 mark OEE, 1 mark gap, 1 mark recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> - **Mistake 1**: Writing OEE components as "A, P, Q" without defining them. Always **define each symbol**.
> - **Mistake 2**: Forgetting the **decision rule** in break-even problems. The answer is incomplete without it.
> - **Mistake 3**: Confusing "Programmable" with "Flexible" in examples. Programmable = batch changeover. Flexible = zero changeover.
> - **Mistake 4**: Not stating **world-class benchmark = 85%**. This is the single most-missed line.
> - **Mistake 5**: Writing OEE as a percentage without showing all three sub-components multiplied together.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Automation Systems - Types**

- ⭐ **Three types**: Fixed, Programmable, Flexible.
- ⭐ **Fixed Automation** = high volume, **zero** flexibility, dedicated lines (bottling, IC fab).
- ⭐ **Programmable Automation** = batch production, reprogrammable, CNC/PLC based.
- ⭐ **Flexible Automation** = mass customization, FMS/CIM, virtually zero changeover.
- ⭐ **OEE = A × P × Q**, world-class benchmark = **≥ 85%**.
- ⭐ **Break-even volume formula**: $N_{\text{BE}} = \frac{C_F - C_P}{V_P - V_F}$.
- ⭐ **Decision rule**: If $N > N_{\text{BE}}$ → Fixed; if $N < N_{\text{BE}}$ → Programmable.
- ⭐ **Six Big Losses** classify OEE losses into Availability, Performance, Quality buckets.
- ⭐ **FMS components**: Central computer, CNC machines, AGVs, AS/RS, robots, inspection, network.
- ⭐ **CIM hierarchy (Automation Pyramid)**: ERP → MES → SCADA → PLC/CNC → Sensors/Actuators.
- ⭐ **Industry 4.0** = cyber-physical extension of flexible automation.
- ⭐ In WCM, automation supports the **Focused Improvement (FI)** and **Autonomous Maintenance (AM)** pillars.
- ⭐ Toyota Production System (TPS) pioneered flexible automation with Jidoka (autonomation).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Manufacturing Systems - Flexible Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modern Manufacturing Systems - Flexible Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, integrated group of numerically controlled (CNC) machine tools, linked by an automated material handling system, and centrally controlled by a master computer. The system is capable of processing a variety of part types simultaneously within its pre-designed range of production capabilities, while maintaining automatic tool flow and information flow.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET835 syllabus terminology, FMS is a manufacturing system in which:

$$
\text{FMS} = \{\text{NC Machines}\} + \{\text{Automated Material Handling}\} + \{\text{Computer Control}\} + \{\text{Tool Management}\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Standard Expected Answer)**
> A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is an integrated, computer-controlled system of multiple CNC workstations, automated material handling equipment, and tool management facilities capable of producing a variety of parts in low to medium volumes with minimal manual intervention.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen. The **chef stations** (workstations) can each prepare different dishes (parts) using various recipes (NC programs). The **waiters** running between stations with plates (automated guided vehicles) carry ingredients from storage (loading/unloading stations). The **head chef's screen** (central computer) coordinates which dish is made where and when. If suddenly 50 orders for pasta arrive, the system dynamically rebalances work — exactly like an FMS re-routes jobs across machines to balance load. This is **flexibility** in action.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Flexibility in Manufacturing

Flexibility is a graded property — manufacturing systems exhibit different *degrees* of flexibility:

| Flexibility Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Flexibility** | Ability of a single machine to produce different parts | CNC turning center |
| **Process Flexibility** | Ability to produce a part in multiple ways | Different routing options |
| **Product Flexibility** | Ease of introducing new products | Quick changeover |
| **Routing Flexibility** | Multiple machines can perform same operation | FMS with alternate routing |
| **Volume Flexibility** | Operate profitably at different volumes | System handles batch variation |
| **Expansion Flexibility** | Ability to expand the system modularly | Adding new workstations |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain the types of flexibility*. Always remember that **Machine + Process + Product + Routing** form the core quartet. Volume and Expansion flexibilities are bonus marks.

## 1.4 Three Tiers of Flexible Automation

Manufacturing flexibility can be classified into three architectural tiers:

1. **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)** — A single workstation with parts-programmable capability, usually with a part-loading buffer and tool-changing capability.
2. **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** — Multiple workstations connected by automated material handling and central computer control (this is the full system).
3. **Flexible Transfer Line (FTL)** — A series of workstations connected by an automated transfer system, typically for limited-variety, high-volume production of similar parts.

The progression from FMC → FMS → FTL represents increasing capital investment, throughput, and rigidity.

---

## 1.5 Key Metrics Used in FMS Performance Evaluation

The following metrics are standardized in World Class Manufacturing benchmarks:

- **Machine Utilization (η):** Fraction of available time the machine is performing useful work.
- **Throughput (T):** Number of parts produced per unit time.
- **Lead Time (L):** Total time from order release to part completion.
- **Work-in-Process (WIP):** Number of parts in the system at any instant.
- **Setup Time Ratio:** Percentage of time spent in non-cutting activities.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Board Favorites**
> Two metrics appear in nearly every KTU question paper — *Machine Utilization* and *Throughput*. Memorize the relationships in the formula sheet of SECTION 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Components of an FMS

An FMS is built from **four functional subsystems**, each of which the KTU examiner expects the student to label and describe in part (a) of a 14-mark question.

### Component 1: Workstations (CNC Machines)
- Numerically controlled (CNC) machine tools — milling, turning, drilling, grinding, EDM, etc.
- Each station has its own local controller (CNC unit) and tool magazine.
- Equipped with **automatic tool changers (ATC)** and **pallet changers**.
- Must be capable of operating unattended for several hours.

### Component 2: Automated Material Handling System (MHS)
- Moves parts between workstations, load/unload stations, and storage buffers.
- Common types:
  - **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle):** Wire-guided or laser-guided carts.
  - **Conveyor Systems:** Belt or roller type.
  - **Robotic Part Handlers:** Stationary industrial robots.
  - **In-floor tow-line carts:** Move on tracks embedded in the floor.

### Component 3: Central Control Computer (Supervisory Computer)
- The "brain" of the FMS.
- Performs:
  - Production scheduling and dispatching
  - Tool management
  - Status monitoring
  - Routing decisions
  - Workload balancing
  - Communication with CNC controllers via DNC (Direct Numerical Control)

### Component 4: Tool Management System
- Centralized tool pool with **Automatic Tool Delivery (ATD)** or tool transporter.
- Prevents downtime due to tool change.
- Monitors tool wear via sensor feedback.

---

## 2.2 Types of FMS Layouts

KTU examiners often ask for a sketch plus description. The three canonical layouts are:

| Layout | Configuration | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **In-line Layout** | Machines arranged in a line | Sequential operations, large parts |
| **L-shaped / U-shaped** | L or U arrangement | Mixed-flow production, smaller footprint |
| **Loop / Cellular Layout** | Workstations arranged in closed loop with central buffer | Most common; supports routing flexibility |

> [!NOTE] **Industry Standard**
> The **loop layout** is the most widely implemented because it offers the best balance between material flow simplicity and routing flexibility. If asked to "draw a typical FMS layout," draw a loop.

---

## 2.3 Group Technology (GT) — The Foundation of FMS

FMS is built on the principle that manufacturing parts into **part families** is far more efficient than treating each part as unique.

- **Part Family:** A collection of parts sharing similar manufacturing attributes (geometry, processes, tooling).
- **Classification and Coding System:** Each part is assigned a code (e.g., DCLASS, MICLASS, Opitz) describing its design and manufacturing features.
- **Production Flow Analysis (PFA):** A method to identify part families based on the process routing similarity.

Parts within the same family can be manufactured on the same FMS without major reconfiguration — this is what enables flexibility.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{cutting}}}{T_{\text{available}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Ratio of productive time to available time |
| 2 | Throughput Rate | $T = \dfrac{N}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | parts/hour | Where $N$ = parts completed, $t_{\text{cycle}}$ = cycle time |
| 3 | Cycle Time | $t_c = t_{\text{machining}} + t_{\text{load}} + t_{\text{unload}} + t_{\text{queue}}$ | minutes | Includes wait time |
| 4 | Number of Machines Required | $n = \dfrac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \cdot \eta}$ | machines | $R$ = required production rate, $T_p$ = processing time per part |
| 5 | Work-in-Process (Little's Law) | $WIP = T \cdot L$ | parts | $T$ = throughput, $L$ = lead time |
| 6 | Lead Time | $L = L_{\text{queue}} + L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{move}}$ | hours | Sum of wait, process, and move time |
| 7 | Bottleneck Identification | $C_b = \max(t_{\text{station,i}})$ | minutes | Station with highest time = bottleneck |
| 8 | OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | $A$ = availability, $P$ = performance, $Q$ = quality |
| 9 | Setup-to-Run Ratio | $SRR = \dfrac{t_{\text{setup}}}{t_{\text{run}}}$ | dimensionless | Indicates need for FMS if SRR > 0.05 |
| 10 | AGV Travel Time | $t_{travel} = \dfrac{d}{v_{AGV}}$ | minutes | $d$ = distance, $v_{AGV}$ = velocity |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Notation Warning for Exam**
> Use Greek letters and subscripts clearly. Many KTU students lose marks because they write *t*, *T*, *tc*, and *tc* interchangeably — the table above gives you the **standard symbols**.

---

## 2.5 Application of Formulas — Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic; they directly drive decisions in:

1. **Capital Investment Justification:** Before installing an FMS, engineers must compute $n$ (number of machines required) and compare with existing setup.
2. **Bottleneck Analysis:** Identifying $C_b$ guides where to add capacity.
3. **Lean Manufacturing:** The WIP = T × L equation (Little's Law) is central to Toyota Production System.
4. **Six Sigma:** OEE metrics drive continuous improvement initiatives in World Class Manufacturing.
5. **Cost Modeling:** Setup-to-run ratio determines whether an FMS investment beats a dedicated transfer line.

---

## 2.6 Operational Workflow Inside an FMS

The end-to-end operational sequence is:

1. Order released into the system by the host computer.
2. Computer determines routing — which workstations, which tools, which sequence.
3. Pallet loaded with raw workpiece at Load/Unload station.
4. AGV (or conveyor) transports pallet to first workstation.
5. CNC controller executes NC program for that operation.
6. Completed part (in-process) transported to next workstation.
7. Loop continues until all operations complete.
8. Finished part moved to Unload station.
9. Tool usage tracked; worn tools auto-replaced.
10. Production data logged to central database for performance analysis.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Short-Answer Tip**
> A 3-mark question often asks: *"Explain the working of an FMS."* The answer above, condensed to 5 steps, scores full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Number of Machines Required

This derivation is a classic KTU 14-mark question. Let us derive it rigorously from first principles.

### Given Information
- $R$ = Required production rate (parts/hour)
- $T_p$ = Average processing time per part (minutes/part)
- $\eta$ = Machine utilization factor (decimal)
- $n$ = Number of machines needed

### Step 1 — Define Machine Time Budget

Each machine provides a maximum of **60 minutes per hour** of productive machining time. However, due to downtime, tool changes, and idle periods, the actual productive time per machine per hour is:

$$
T_{\text{productive}} = 60 \times \eta \quad \text{[minutes per hour]}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Machine Capacity

A single machine can produce $\dfrac{T_{\text{productive}}}{T_p}$ parts per hour:

$$
C_{\text{machine}} = \frac{60 \times \eta}{T_p} \quad \text{[parts per hour per machine]}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Required Capacity

To meet the demand $R$ parts per hour, the system must provide total capacity equal to $R$:

$$
n \times C_{\text{machine}} = R
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for n

Substituting $C_{\text{machine}}$:

$$
n \times \frac{60 \times \eta}{T_p} = R
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \cdot \eta}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for sizing an FMS or any machine cluster.

### Numerical Example (Valuation Practice)

**Problem:** A factory needs to produce 30 parts/hour. Each part requires an average of 25 minutes of machining. If machine utilization is targeted at 85%, how many CNC machines are needed?

**Solution:**

$$
n = \frac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \cdot \eta} = \frac{30 \times 25}{60 \times 0.85} = \frac{750}{51} = 14.71
$$

Rounding up (you cannot have 0.71 of a machine):

$$
n = 15 \text{ machines}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner Insight**
> Always **round UP**, never down. Writing "14 machines" loses a mark because the system cannot meet the production target.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bottleneck Cycle Time and System Throughput

### Setup
Consider a loop-layout FMS with stations $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$, each having a station processing time $t_i$.

### Step 1 — Identify Bottleneck

The bottleneck is the station that takes the longest time:

$$
C_b = \max\{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute System Cycle Time

The system cannot produce faster than the bottleneck allows:

$$
t_c^{\text{system}} = C_b = \max_i(t_i)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Maximum Throughput

$$
T_{\max} = \frac{60}{C_b} \quad \text{[parts per hour]}
$$

### Step 4 — Multi-Machine Bottleneck Resolution

If $C_b$ is significantly higher than other stations, add a **parallel machine** at the bottleneck. New throughput becomes:

$$
T_{\max}^{\text{new}} = \min\left(\frac{60}{C_b / k_b},\; \min_{i \ne b} \frac{60}{t_i}\right)
$$

where $k_b$ is the number of machines at the bottleneck.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — FMS vs. Other Manufacturing Systems

This matrix is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions in KTU ESE.

| Parameter | **Dedicated Transfer Line (DTL)** | **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** | **Job Shop (Manual)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Volume | High | Medium | Low |
| Product Variety | Very Low (1-2 types) | Medium (10-100 part types) | Very High |
| Flexibility | None | High | Very High |
| Capital Investment | Very High | High | Low |
| Labor Content | Very Low | Low | Very High |
| Automation Level | Fixed automation | Programmable automation | Manual / Semi-auto |
| Changeover Time | Weeks to Months | Hours | Minutes |
| Typical Lead Time | Low (for limited parts) | Medium | High |
| Suitability | Automotive engine blocks | Aerospace components | Custom prototypes |
| Workforce Skill | Low | High (multiskilled) | Very High |

### Interpretation

- **DTL** is justified only when production volume is extremely high AND variety is essentially zero (e.g., a single car model cylinder block running 1 million units/year).
- **Job Shop** is the opposite — extremely flexible but inefficient for high volumes.
- **FMS** is the **sweet spot** when volume is moderate (a few hundred to tens of thousands) and variety is significant (10-100 part types).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Decision Rule (Write This in Exam)**
> Choose FMS when Setup Time / Run Time > 0.05. Choose DTL when Setup Time / Run Time < 0.01. Choose Job Shop when variety > 1000 part types.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Setup-to-Run Ratio Threshold

### Definition

$$
SRR = \frac{t_{\text{setup}}}{t_{\text{run}}}
$$

### Justification of SRR Threshold

For a system to be **economically better than a transfer line**, the cost savings from flexibility must offset the throughput penalty:

$$
\frac{t_{\text{setup}} + t_{\text{run}}}{t_{\text{run}}} < \frac{\text{Transfer line cycle time}}{\text{FMS cycle time}}
$$

Empirically, when $SRR > 0.05$ (i.e., 5%), an FMS becomes economically superior to a dedicated system. When $SRR < 0.01$, a transfer line dominates.

### Threshold Boundaries

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
SRR > 0.05 &\rightarrow \text{FMS preferred} \\
0.01 < SRR < 0.05 &\rightarrow \text{Hybrid / Cell recommended} \\
SRR < 0.01 &\rightarrow \text{Transfer Line preferred}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is a high-yield item for KTU Part B (a) questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Bottleneck Analysis

**Problem:** An FMS has 4 stations with processing times: $S_1 = 12$ min, $S_2 = 18$ min, $S_3 = 9$ min, $S_4 = 15$ min. Find the bottleneck and maximum throughput. If a second parallel machine is added at the bottleneck, what is the new throughput?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Bottleneck:**

$$
C_b = \max\{12, 18, 9, 15\} = 18 \text{ minutes}
$$

Station $S_2$ is the bottleneck.

**Step 2 — Original Throughput:**

$$
T_{\max} = \frac{60}{18} = 3.33 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — After Adding Parallel Machine at $S_2$:**

The new bottleneck at $S_2$ is now $\dfrac{18}{2} = 9$ minutes. New system bottleneck:

$$
C_b^{\text{new}} = \max\left(12,\; 9,\; 9,\; 15\right) = 15 \text{ minutes}
$$

Station $S_4$ is now the bottleneck.

**Step 4 — New Throughput:**

$$
T_{\max}^{\text{new}} = \frac{60}{15} = 4.0 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Improvement:** $\dfrac{4.0 - 3.33}{3.33} \times 100\% = 20\%$ throughput increase.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Derivation Principles

| # | Principle | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine count | $n = \dfrac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \eta}$ |
| 2 | System cycle time | $t_c = \max(t_i)$ |
| 3 | Throughput | $T = \dfrac{60}{t_c}$ |
| 4 | Little's Law | $WIP = T \cdot L$ |
| 5 | OEE | $OEE = A \cdot P \cdot Q$ |
| 6 | SRR Threshold | $SRR = \dfrac{t_{setup}}{t_{run}}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level FMS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Central Control Computer] --> B[Workstation 1 CNC Mill]
    A --> C[Workstation 2 CNC Lathe]
    A --> D[Workstation 3 CNC Grinder]
    A --> E[Workstation 4 CNC EDM]
    A --> F[Tool Management Pool]
    A --> G[Load Unload Station]
    G --> H[Automated Material Handling AGV]
    H --> B
    H --> C
    H --> D
    H --> E
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[Finished Part Storage]
    F --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    F --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Central Control Computer** is the central node coordinating all activity.
- **Workstation 1–4** are CNC machines in a loop arrangement.
- **AGV** (Automated Guided Vehicle) shuttles pallets between load/unload, workstations, and storage.
- **Tool Management Pool** delivers fresh tools to machines and retrieves worn tools.

---

## 4.2 Operational Flow of an FMS (Process Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Order Released to Host Computer] --> P1[Production Schedule Generated]
    P1 --> P2[Routing Decision for Each Part]
    P2 --> P3[Load Raw Workpiece on Pallet]
    P3 --> P4[AGV Transports Pallet to Station 1]
    P4 --> P5{Station 1 Operation Complete?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[AGV Transports to Next Station]
    P6 --> P7{All Operations Complete?}
    P7 -- No --> P5
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Move to Unload Station]
    P8 --> P9[Finished Part Inspection and Storage]
    P9 --> P10[Update Central Database]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The system enters a **decision loop** at P5 — it keeps shuttling the part between workstations until all required operations are completed.
- After final completion at P7, the finished part exits the loop and goes into storage.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Choose FMS vs. Transfer Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Analyze Production Requirements] --> Q2[Compute Setup to Run Ratio SRR]
    Q2 --> Q3{SRR Greater Than 0.05?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Choose FMS]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{SRR Less Than 0.01?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Choose Transfer Line]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Choose Flexible Manufacturing Cell FMC]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is the **canonical selection tree** for FMS justification, as derived in SECTION 3.4.
- The boundary values 0.05 and 0.01 come from the threshold analysis.

---

## 4.4 Information Flow in an FMS (Hierarchical Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Factory Level ERP System] --> L2[Shop Floor Control Computer]
    L2 --> L3[Cell Controller 1]
    L2 --> L4[Cell Controller 2]
    L3 --> L5[CNC Machine 1]
    L3 --> L6[CNC Machine 2]
    L4 --> L7[CNC Machine 3]
    L4 --> L8[CNC Machine 4]
    L5 --> L9[Machine Sensors and Tool Monitoring]
    L6 --> L9
    L7 --> L9
    L8 --> L9
    L9 --> L3
    L9 --> L4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Level 1:** Enterprise-level ERP/MRP system (strategic).
- **Level 2:** Shop floor computer (tactical scheduling).
- **Level 3:** Cell controllers (operational coordination).
- **Level 4:** Individual CNC controllers and sensors (real-time control).
- **Feedback loop:** Sensor data from machines flows back up to controllers for adaptive control.

---

## 4.5 Components Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Workstations] <--> C2[Material Handling]
    C1 <--> C3[Tool Management]
    C1 <--> C4[Central Computer]
    C2 <--> C4
    C3 <--> C4
    C2 <--> C5[Load Unload Stations]
    C5 <--> C4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All four subsystems — workstations, material handling, tool management, and load/unload stations — are **bidirectionally connected** to the central computer.
- This bidirectional flow is what makes the system "integrated" rather than merely "automated."

---

## 4.6 Advantages vs. Limitations Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADV [Advantages of FMS]
        A1[Higher Machine Utilization]
        A2[Reduced Lead Time]
        A3[Lower WIP]
        A4[Greater Flexibility]
        A5[Reduced Labor Cost]
    end
    subgraph LIM [Limitations of FMS]
        L1[High Initial Capital]
        L2[Complex Programming]
        L3[Skilled Workforce Required]
        L4[Limited Part Variety Range]
        L5[High Downtime Risk]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The advantage of "Higher Machine Utilization" is offset by the limitation "High Initial Capital."
- The advantage of "Greater Flexibility" is balanced by "Limited Part Variety Range" — FMS is not infinitely flexible.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** Define Flexible Manufacturing System. List any four components of an FMS.

**Model Answer:**

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is an integrated, computer-controlled arrangement of CNC machine tools, automated material handling equipment, and centralized tool management capable of producing a variety of part types in low-to-medium batch sizes with minimal manual intervention.

The four essential components of an FMS are:
1. **CNC Workstations** — Numerically controlled machines for processing.
2. **Automated Material Handling System (MHS)** — AGVs/conveyors for part movement.
3. **Central Control Computer** — Coordinates scheduling, routing, and monitoring.
4. **Tool Management System** — Centralized tool storage and delivery.

*(Word count target: ~70 words — exactly suitable for 3-mark answers.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Differentiate between Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) and Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS).

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)** | **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Machines | 1-2 machines | 3 or more machines |
| Material Handling | Manual or simple robot | Fully automated (AGV/conveyor) |
| Central Computer | Optional / limited | Mandatory, fully integrated |
| Capital Cost | Moderate | High |
| Flexibility | Limited routing | High routing flexibility |
| Typical Use | Small batch production | Medium batch, multi-part family |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the major components of a Flexible Manufacturing System with the help of a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A factory plans to install an FMS. The required production rate is 40 parts/hour. The average machining time per part is 18 minutes. If the targeted machine utilization is 80%, calculate the number of machines required. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The major components of an FMS are:

**1. CNC Workstations (2 marks):**
These are the numerically controlled machine tools — milling, turning, grinding, EDM — where actual part processing occurs. Each workstation has:
- Local CNC controller
- Automatic tool changer (ATC)
- Automatic pallet changer (APC)
- In-process measurement capability

**2. Automated Material Handling System (2 marks):**
Transports parts between workstations without manual intervention. Common types:
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — wire/laser guided
- **Conveyors** — belt or roller
- **Robotic handlers** — stationary 6-axis robots
- **In-floor tow-line carts**

**3. Central Control Computer (2 marks):**
The supervisory computer that:
- Generates production schedules
- Assigns jobs to machines
- Monitors tool usage and wear
- Performs routing decisions
- Communicates with CNC controllers via DNC protocol

**4. Tool Management System (1 mark):**
Centralized tool storage with **Automatic Tool Delivery (ATD)**. Worn tools are auto-replaced without halting production.

*(Block diagram to be drawn — refer to SECTION 4.1.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Required production rate $R = 40$ parts/hour
- Average processing time $T_p = 18$ minutes/part
- Machine utilization $\eta = 80\% = 0.80$

**Formula:**

$$
n = \frac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \cdot \eta}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
n = \frac{40 \times 18}{60 \times 0.80}
$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$
n = \frac{720}{48} = 15
$$

**Answer:** **15 machines** are required to meet the production target.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Common mistakes students make on this question:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal** — using $\eta = 80$ instead of $0.80$. This gives $n = 0.15$, which is wrong.
> 2. **Not rounding up** — answering $n = 15$ exactly is correct; but if a fractional result arises (e.g., $n = 14.71$), writing $14$ instead of $15$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing units** — always write "machines" after the numerical answer.
> 4. **Skipping the formula** — KTU examiners award 2 marks just for writing the correct formula with notation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** *(a)* Compare Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) with Dedicated Transfer Line and Job Shop on the basis of any seven parameters. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* An FMS has 5 stations with the following processing times (in minutes): Station 1 = 10, Station 2 = 14, Station 3 = 12, Station 4 = 18, Station 5 = 11. Identify the bottleneck and calculate the maximum throughput of the system. If one parallel machine is added at the bottleneck, find the new throughput and percentage improvement. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

Refer to the comparison table in **SECTION 3.3** of these notes. Seven parameters to compare:

1. Production Volume
2. Product Variety
3. Flexibility
4. Capital Investment
5. Labor Content
6. Automation Level
7. Lead Time

*(Full table written out: 7 marks. KTU examiners expect at least 5-7 parameters in tabular form.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** Processing times: $t_1 = 10$, $t_2 = 14$, $t_3 = 12$, $t_4 = 18$, $t_5 = 11$ minutes.

**Step 1 — Identify the Bottleneck (1 mark):**

$$
C_b = \max\{10, 14, 12, 18, 11\} = 18 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Station 4 is the bottleneck.**

**Step 2 — Original Maximum Throughput (2 marks):**

$$
T_{\max} = \frac{60}{C_b} = \frac{60}{18} = 3.33 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Parallel Machine at Bottleneck (2 marks):**

New effective time at Station 4 = $\dfrac{18}{2} = 9$ minutes.

New system times: $t_1 = 10$, $t_2 = 14$, $t_3 = 12$, $t_4^{\text{new}} = 9$, $t_5 = 11$.

New bottleneck:

$$
C_b^{\text{new}} = \max\{10, 14, 12, 9, 11\} = 14 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Station 2 is now the bottleneck.**

**Step 4 — New Throughput (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\max}^{\text{new}} = \frac{60}{14} = 4.29 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Improvement (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{4.29 - 3.33}{3.33} \times 100\% = 28.83\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Original bottleneck:** Station 4 (18 min)
- **Original throughput:** 3.33 parts/hour
- **New bottleneck:** Station 2 (14 min)
- **New throughput:** 4.29 parts/hour
- **Improvement:** **28.83%**

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> 1. **Skipping the bottleneck identification step** — even if you compute throughput correctly, the examiner allocates **1 full mark** explicitly for identifying the bottleneck station.
> 2. **Forgetting to recompute the new bottleneck after adding the parallel machine** — many students compute $\frac{60}{9} = 6.67$ parts/hour, which is incorrect because the new bottleneck is Station 2 (14 min), not Station 4 (9 min).
> 3. **Writing percentage improvement without units** — always write "%" or "percent".
> 4. **Mixing units** — keep minutes throughout, then divide 60 by minutes at the end.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE] **Important Things to Remember — Last-Minute Revision**

### ✅ Definitions You Must Know
- [ ] **FMS** — Computer-integrated CNC system with automated material handling and tool management.
- [ ] **FMC** — Single workstation with part-programmable flexibility.
- [ ] **Flexibility** — Ability to produce different parts efficiently without major reconfiguration.

### ✅ Components of FMS (Always list all four)
- [ ] CNC Workstations
- [ ] Automated Material Handling System (AGVs, conveyors)
- [ ] Central Control Computer
- [ ] Tool Management System

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize before exam)
- [ ] $n = \dfrac{R \cdot T_p}{60 \cdot \eta}$ — Number of machines
- [ ] $T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{C_b}$ — System throughput
- [ ] $WIP = T \cdot L$ — Little's Law
- [ ] $SRR = \dfrac{t_{\text{setup}}}{t_{\text{run}}}$ — Selection criterion

### ✅ Decision Rules
- [ ] $SRR > 0.05$ → Choose **FMS**
- [ ] $SRR < 0.01$ → Choose **Transfer Line**
- [ ] Variety > 1000 part types → Choose **Job Shop**

### ✅ Six Types of Flexibility
- [ ] Machine, Process, Product, Routing, Volume, Expansion

### ✅ Three Tiers of Flexible Automation
- [ ] FMC → FMS → FTL (increasing scale and capital)

### ✅ Common Pitfalls in KTU Exam
- [ ] Convert percentages to decimals when computing $n$.
- [ ] Always round UP for machine count.
- [ ] Recompute bottleneck after adding parallel machines.
- [ ] Show formula before substitution — earns 1-2 marks.
- [ ] Draw block diagrams with labels — KTU examiners reward neatness.

### ✅ Layout Types to Draw (if asked)
- [ ] In-line, L-shaped, Loop/Cellular — **Loop is most common**

### ✅ Performance Metrics
- [ ] Machine Utilization $\eta$
- [ ] Throughput $T$
- [ ] Lead Time $L$
- [ ] WIP
- [ ] OEE = $A \times P \times Q$

### ✅ Typical Industrial Applications
- [ ] Aerospace components (medium volume, high variety)
- [ ] Automotive engine components
- [ ] Defense components
- [ ] Medical implants and instruments

---

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Modern Manufacturing Systems — Flexible Manufacturing**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET835 — World Class Manufacturing*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Manufacturing - 5S
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET835 - WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lean Manufacturing - 5S ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **5S** is a structured, five-stage workplace organization and standardization methodology that originated in post-war Japan as part of the Toyota Production System (TPS). It forms the foundational "housekeeping" pillar of **Lean Manufacturing**, upon which all other lean tools (Jidoka, Just-In-Time, Kaizen, Kanban, Poka-Yoke) are constructed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** 5S is a systematic method of organizing, cleaning, developing, and sustaining a productive work environment. It is not merely "housekeeping" — it is an **engineering discipline for waste elimination at the gemba (shop floor)**. The five S-words are all Japanese terms beginning with "S". The five pillars, in their canonical Japanese form with English equivalents: | Pillar | Japanese (Romaji) | English Translation | Core Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **S1** | *Seiri* | **Sort** | Eliminate unnecessary items | | **S2** | *Seiton* | **Set in Order** | Arrange items for easy retrieval | | **S3** | *Seiso* | **Shine** | Clean the workplace systematically | | **S4** | *Seiketsu* | **Standardize** | Establish norms and consistency | | **S5** | *Shitsuke* | **Sustain** | Discipline to maintain the system | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Hospital Operating Theatre (OT):** > > Imagine you are a surgeon about to perform open-heart surgery. The OT is your workstation. > > - **Seiri (Sort):** The nurse has removed every non-essential item from the theatre — no old magazines, no unused trolleys, no expired drugs. Only surgery-critical instruments remain. > - **Seiton (Set in Order):** Every scalpel, clamp, and suture is laid on a **shadow-board** in a fixed position. The surgeon reaches for tool #3 without ever looking — muscle memory alone finds it. > - **Seiso (Shine):** After every operation, the floor is mopped, the lights wiped, the trays sterilized. A spill of blood is cleaned *immediately* — not at end-of-day. > - **Seiketsu (Standardize):** A laminated SOP on the wall specifies that "after every procedure, the theatre must look identical to the pre-procedure state." Colour-coded bins, checklists, 5-minute audits. > - **Shitsuke (Sustain):** The Chief of Surgery does **random surprise audits** weekly. Anyone who violates the standard gets coached, not punished — but the violation is logged. > > **Result:** Zero time wasted searching, zero cross-contamination, zero last-minute panic. This is exactly what 5S does for a manufacturing shop floor. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics The standard KTU-recommended metric for evaluating 5S maturity is the **5S Audit Score** (typically out of 100). The benchmark thresholds used in industry are: - **World-Class Standard:** $\geq$ **90 %** - **Acceptable Practice:** **75 – 89 %** - **Needs Improvement:** **50 – 74 %** - **Critical / Failed:** $<$ **50 %** > [!TIP] > **Remember:** 5S is a **gateway tool**. It is a *prerequisite*, not a *replacement*, for advanced Lean tools like TPM, Six Sigma, and Value Stream Mapping (VSM). A factory with a dirty floor and cluttered tool crib cannot sustain Kaizen. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each "S" #### **S1 — Seiri (Sort) — "Sorting the Necessary from the Unnecessary"** The objective is **elimination of muda (waste)** in physical form. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Photograph the workstation in its *current* state (the "Before" picture). 2. Tag every item with one of three **Red-Tag colours**: - 🔴 **Red Tag:** Unnecessary — dispose/recycle. - 🟡 **Yellow Tag:** Sometimes-needed — relocate to a remote storage area (e.g., the "Red Tag Holding Area"). - 🟢 **Green Tag:** Needed frequently — keep at the workstation. 3. Apply the **"Last-Used Test":** If an item has not been used in the last **30 days**, it is a candidate for Red-Tag removal. 4. After 90 days of holding, dispose of all unclaimed Red-Tagged items. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Principle:** *Seiri converts "floor space" into "working space."* A typical Indian SME recovers **20–30 %** of floor area after a single Seiri exercise. #### **S2 — Seiton (Set in Order) — "A Place for Everything, and Everything in its Place"** The objective is **elimination of search waste** and motion waste. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Decide on the **frequency-of-use layout principle** — items used *most often* are placed *closest* to the worker (within arm's reach). 2. Apply **visual management (VM)** techniques: - **Shadow boards** (silhouette outlines of tools) - **Floor marking tape** (yellow for walkways, white for work areas, red for prohibited zones, green for safety equipment) - **Address systems** (e.g., Row 3, Bay B, Slot 4) - **FIFO lanes** (First-In-First-Out roller racks for parts) 3. The "**30-Second Rule**": Any standard tool or document must be retrievable in **≤ 30 seconds** by any trained operator. #### **S3 — Seiso (Shine) — "Cleaning as Inspection"** The objective is **elimination of dirt, grease, and abnormal-condition waste**, and the early detection of equipment defects. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Assign **cleaning responsibility zones** — every operator owns a defined area (often demarcated by painted lines). 2. Cleaning is performed **at the start of the shift**, not at the end. 3. Treat cleaning as **autonomous inspection** — operators detect oil leaks, loose bolts, cracked hoses during cleaning. 4. Maintain a **"Source-of-Dirt" register** — track where contamination originates and eliminate it at source (e.g., replace a leaking seal instead of mopping daily). > [!NOTE] > **Toyota's famous line:** *"A clean machine is a safe machine, a safe machine is a productive machine."* #### **S4 — Seiketsu (Standardize) — "The First Three S-Sustained by Systems"** The objective is **institutionalization of the previous three S** so that they do not degrade over time. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Create a **5S Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** for each workstation — typically a single A3-size laminated sheet with photographs. 2. Deploy **visual standardization boards** containing checklists, schedules, photographs of "ideal state." 3. Implement a **5S Audit Checklist** (typically 30–50 questions across the five pillars, each rated 0–5). 4. Standardize across shifts — the morning shift's workstation must look identical to the night shift's. #### **S5 — Shitsuke (Sustain / Self-Discipline) — "Making 5S a Habit"** The objective is **cultural transformation** — converting 5S from a project into a *way of working*. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Top management commitment** — leaders must conduct **Gemba Walks** (shop-floor walks) at least weekly. 2. **5S Audit cadence** — typically monthly audits by a cross-functional team, with results posted on a public **5S Scoreboard**. 3. **Training & Education** — every new inductee undergoes a mandatory 4-hour 5S orientation. 4. **Recognition & Reward** — best zone of the month, best kaizen of the month. 5. **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** applied to 5S itself — continuous improvement of the improvement system. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > 5S is a qualitative system, but KTU examiners expect you to know the **quantitative audit scoring formula** and related metrics. | Metric | Formula | Boundary / Target | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **5S Audit Score (per pillar)** | $S_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{j}}{n \times 5} \times 100$ | $0 \leq S_i \leq 100$ | % | | **Overall 5S Score (weighted)** | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i$, where $\sum w_i = 1$ | World-class: $\geq 90$ | % | | **Red-Tag Disposal Rate** | $R_{disposal} = \dfrac{N_{disposed}}{N_{tagged}} \times 100$ | Target: $\geq 80$ within 90 days | % | | **Floor Space Recovery Ratio** | $R_{recovery} = \dfrac{A_{freed}}{A_{original}} \times 100$ | Typical: $20$ – $30$ | % | | **Search-Time Reduction** | $\Delta t_{search} = \dfrac{t_{before} - t_{after}}{t_{before}} \times 100$ | Target: $\geq 70$ | % | | **Tool-Retrieval Time** | $t_{retrieve}$ | Standard: $\leq 30$ seconds | s | Where, in the audit score formula: - $r_{j}$ = rating awarded to question $j$ (on a $0$ to $5$ scale). - $n$ = total number of audit questions in that pillar. - $w_i$ = weightage of pillar $i$ (commonly all equal at $0.2$, but Seiketsu and Shitsuke are often weighted higher at $0.25$ each in mature systems). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The 5S Audit Score is a **leading indicator** in the overall World-Class Manufacturing (WCM) index. KTU Module 3 connects 5S directly to WCM pillars such as *Workplace Organization*, *Autonomous Maintenance*, and *Quality Pillar*. A factory scoring $<70\%$ on 5S will typically fail any subsequent TPM or Six Sigma rollout. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Automotive OEMs (Toyota, Maruti-Suzuki, Hyundai India):** 5S is the *first* module of every new-plant employee induction. The **Maruti Suzuki Gurugram plant** has consistently scored $>$ **95 %** in 5S audits — a benchmark cited in KTU case studies. 2. **Aerospace MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul):** A single misplaced tool in an aircraft engine bay can cause an FOD (Foreign Object Debris) incident costing lakhs of rupees and risking lives. Seiton (shadow-boards) eliminates this risk. 3. **Pharmaceutical & Food Industry:** Seiso + Seiketsu together support GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) and HACCP compliance — a regulatory prerequisite. 4. **IT Server Rooms:** Adapted 5S ("5S for IT") reduces server-downtime incidents caused by tangled cabling and unidentified hardware. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Example 1 — Computing the Overall Weighted 5S Audit Score > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** > A manufacturing cell at an Indian SME was audited on all five pillars. The per-pillar scores (each out of 100) and the management-assigned weightages are as follows: > > | Pillar | Score $S_i$ (%) | Weightage $w_i$ | > |---|---|---| > | Seiri | 72 | 0.15 | > | Seiton | 68 | 0.20 | > | Seiso | 85 | 0.15 | > | Seiketsu | 55 | 0.25 | > | Shitsuke | 60 | 0.25 | > > **(a)** Calculate the Overall Weighted 5S Score. > **(b)** Classify the plant's 5S maturity and recommend two corrective actions. #### Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Verify that the weights sum to unity (sanity check).** $$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.25$$ $$= 0.35 + 0.40 + 0.50 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Apply the weighted-average formula.** $$S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i$$ **Step 3 — Expand the summation term by term.** $$\begin{aligned} S_{total} &= (0.15)(72) + (0.20)(68) + (0.15)(85) + (0.25)(55) + (0.25)(60) \\ &= 10.80 + 13.60 + 12.75 + 13.75 + 15.00 \\ &= 65.90 \% \end{aligned}$$ #### Solution — Part (b) **Step 4 — Classification against the benchmark thresholds.** Since $50 \leq S_{total} = 65.90 < 75$, the cell falls in the **"Needs Improvement"** category. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner Pitfall):** Many students only compute the *unweighted arithmetic mean* ($\bar{S} = (72+68+85+55+60)/5 = 68.00\%$). This is **wrong** when weights are explicitly given. Always re-read the question to check whether weights are stated. If they are, you **must** use the weighted formula above. **Step 5 — Diagnostic Root-Cause Analysis using the 80/20 (Pareto) Lens.** The two lowest-scoring pillars are **Seiketsu (55 %)** and **Shitsuke (60 %)**. Together they account for **50 % of the weightage** — this is where the score is being lost. **Step 6 — Recommended Corrective Actions.** 1. **For Seiketsu (Standardize):** Deploy laminated **5S SOP boards** with photographic standards at each workstation. Move from verbal instructions to written, visual standards. 2. **For Shitsuke (Sustain):** Institute a **monthly cross-functional 5S audit** led by senior management, with results published on a public scoreboard and tied to a small team-level recognition (e.g., "Best 5S Zone of the Month" certificate). ### 3.2 Example 2 — Red-Tag Disposal Efficiency and Floor-Space Recovery > **Problem Statement:** > A machine shop conducted a Seiri exercise. Before Seiri, the workstation occupied **24 m²**. After Red-Tagging, **78 items** were tagged, of which **62** were disposed of within the 90-day holding window. After removal, the new working footprint is **17.5 m²**. > > **(a)** Compute the Red-Tag Disposal Rate $R_{disposal}$. > **(b)** Compute the Floor-Space Recovery Ratio $R_{recovery}$. > **(c)** Comment on whether the plant met the typical Seiri benchmark. #### Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Substitute values into the disposal-rate formula.** $$R_{disposal} = \frac{N_{disposed}}{N_{tagged}} \times 100$$ $$R_{disposal} = \frac{62}{78} \times 100$$ $$R_{disposal} = 0.79487... \times 100 \approx 79.49\%$$ #### Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Substitute values into the recovery formula.** $$R_{recovery} = \frac{A_{freed}}{A_{original}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Compute the freed area.** $$A_{freed} = A_{original} - A_{new} = 24 - 17.5 = 6.5 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute the recovery ratio.** $$R_{recovery} = \frac{6.5}{24} \times 100 = 0.27083... \times 100 \approx 27.08\%$$ #### Solution — Part (c) **Step 4 — Compare to the benchmark band $20\% - 30\%$.** Since $R_{recovery} = 27.08\%$ lies **within** the $20\% - 30\%$ benchmark band, the Seiri exercise is **successful** by industry standards. The disposal rate of $79.49\%$ is *just below* the $80\%$ target — the remaining $20.51\%$ of items require a root-cause review (typically: the items are waiting for an upcoming job, or the disposition SOP is unclear). ### 3.3 Example 3 — Search-Time Reduction Analysis > **Problem Statement:** > Before 5S, an operator spent an average of **240 seconds** searching for a die-set at the start of every shift. After Seiton, the average retrieval time fell to **42 seconds**.
