# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]

**Q: Define sustainable manufacturing. List any four driving forces that compel modern manufacturers toward sustainability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Sustainable manufacturing is the creation of manufactured products through economically-sound processes that minimize negative environmental impacts while conserving energy and natural resources, while simultaneously protecting employees, communities, and consumers across the entire product life cycle.

**Driving Forces (2 Marks — any four):**
1. **Regulatory Compliance** — Strict environmental laws such as EPA, REACH, RoHS, and Indian CPCB norms, along with ISO 14001 certification requirements.
2. **Market & Customer Pressure** — Growing green consumerism and demand for eco-labeled products.
3. **Resource Scarcity & Cost Volatility** — Rising prices of raw materials, energy, and water push efficiency.
4. **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** — Indian Companies Act 2013 mandates 2% CSR spending, often directed at sustainable operations.
5. **Investor & ESG Pressure** — Environmental, Social, Governance reporting now influences capital access.

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]

**Q: Explain the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) concept. Why is it considered essential to sustainable manufacturing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework, introduced by **John Elkington in 1994**, states that an organization's true performance must be measured across three dimensions:
- **People (Social)** — fair labor, worker safety, community welfare.
- **Planet (Environmental)** — emissions control, waste minimization, resource conservation.
- **Profit (Economic)** — long-term financial viability and growth.

**Why Essential (1 Mark):**
A manufacturing enterprise cannot be called "sustainable" if it is profitable but pollutes the environment and exploits workers. TBL ensures that **all three pillars are satisfied simultaneously**, aligning the firm with the Brundtland Report's definition of sustainable development.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks each and typically have internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Q: (a) Explain the four phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) as per ISO 14040 standard, with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A manufacturing unit consumes the following annually: 60,000 kWh of grid electricity, 5,000 L of diesel, and 3,000 kg of LPG. Compute the total carbon footprint using the emission factors: 0.71 kgCO₂e/kWh for electricity, 2.68 kgCO₂e/L for diesel, and 2.94 kgCO₂e/kg for LPG. Suggest two practical reduction strategies. (7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (Part a) — 7 Marks

The four phases of LCA as per **ISO 14040** are:

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope Definition (2 Marks):**
- Defines the **purpose** of the study (e.g., comparative LCA of two materials).
- Establishes the **functional unit (FU)** — the quantified performance used as reference.
- Sets **system boundaries** — which life cycle stages are included (cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, gate-to-gate).
- Defines the **methodology** and **impact categories** to be assessed.

**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) — Data Collection (2 Marks):**
- Quantifies all **inputs** (raw materials, energy, water) and **outputs** (products, co-products, emissions to air/water/soil, solid waste).
- Builds a complete **mass and energy balance** of the product system.
- Data is collected for every process unit within the defined system boundary.

**Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) (2 Marks):**
- Translates LCI data into **environmental impact scores**.
- Mandatory elements: **Classification** (assigning LCI data to impact categories like global warming, acidification, eutrophication) and **Characterization** (multiplying by equivalence factors to express in a common unit, e.g., kgCO₂e).
- Optional elements: Normalization, Grouping, Weighting.

**Phase 4 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**
- Identifies **significant issues** based on the LCIA results.
- Evaluates **completeness, sensitivity, and consistency** of the study.
- Draws **conclusions and recommendations** for improvement.

*(Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.2 above for the visual flow.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A (Part b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Recall the carbon footprint formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CO₂ from electricity (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{elec} &= 60{,}000 \times 0.71 = 42{,}600 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CO₂ from diesel (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{diesel} &= 5{,}000 \times 2.68 = 13{,}400 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CO₂ from LPG (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{LPG} &= 3{,}000 \times 2.94 = 8{,}820 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total annual carbon footprint (0.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{total} &= 42{,}600 + 13{,}400 + 8{,}820 = 64{,}820 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} \approx 64.82 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Two reduction strategies (1 Mark):**

1. **Install rooftop solar PV** (e.g., 50 kWp system) to displace a portion of grid electricity — the largest emission source (65.7%).
2. **Replace diesel forklift with electric forklift** and improve furnace insulation to reduce LPG consumption — addressing the remaining 34.3%.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: (a) What is the Triple Bottom Line? Discuss in detail how sustainable manufacturing integrates environmental, social, and economic considerations. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Define Design for Environment (DfE). Explain any five DfE strategies that support sustainable manufacturing. (7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction to TBL (2 Marks):**
The Triple Bottom Line, coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, expands traditional accounting (which tracks only financial profit) to include **social** and **environmental** performance. The three "P"s are: **People, Planet, Profit**. A sustainable manufacturer must perform well on all three simultaneously — not just one or two.

**Environmental Pillar (2 Marks):**
Sustainable manufacturing minimizes ecological footprint through:
- Energy efficiency (target SEC reduction of 5–10% annually).
- Renewable energy adoption (solar, wind, biomass).
- Waste minimization at source (pollution prevention hierarchy).
- Water stewardship — recycle process water, rainwater harvesting.
- Compliance with ISO 14001 EMS and ISO 14067 carbon footprint standards.

**Social Pillar (1.5 Marks):**
- Worker occupational health and safety (OHSAS 45001 / ISO 45001).
- Fair wages, no child labor, gender equality (UN Global Compact).
- Community engagement, noise/pollution control beyond the factory fence.
- Ethical sourcing of conflict minerals (3TG — Tin, Tantalum, Tungsten, Gold).

**Economic Pillar (1.5 Marks):**
- Cost reduction through resource efficiency.
- New revenue streams from circular economy (remanufacturing, product-service systems).
- Brand equity from ESG performance attracts investors and customers.
- Risk mitigation against regulatory penalties and resource scarcity shocks.

**Conclusion:** True sustainable manufacturing balances all three pillars — a "green" factory that loses money is not sustainable; a profitable factory that poisons its workers is not sustainable.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (Part b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of DfE (1 Mark):**
**Design for Environment (DfE)** — also called **Eco-Design** — is the systematic integration of environmental considerations into product and process design from the earliest conceptual stage, to minimize the product's total environmental burden throughout its life cycle.

**Five DfE Strategies (1.2 Marks each = 6 Marks):**

1. **Design for Material Efficiency** — Use minimal material via topology optimization, lightweighting (e.g., aluminum replacing cast iron in automotive blocks), and eliminating unnecessary components. **Example:** BMW i3 carbon-fiber-reinforced passenger cell.

2. **Design for Energy Efficiency** — Specify low-power components, standby-mode circuits, efficient motors (IE3/IE4 class), and heat recovery systems. **Example:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on pumps and fans.

3. **Design for Recyclability (DfR)** — Use mono-materials where possible, label plastic parts with resin codes (SPI codes 1–7), avoid permanent adhesives, and design with separable joints. **Example:** Single-polymer packaging replacing multilayer laminates.

4. **Design for Disassembly (DfD)** — Use snap-fits, standard fasteners, modular construction so end-of-life products can be quickly dismantled for parts recovery or remanufacturing. **Example:** Apple iPhone modular design for battery replacement.

5. **Design for Remanufacturing** — Specify wear-resistant surfaces, durable materials, and accessible high-wear components so the product can be restored to "as-new" condition. **Example:** Caterpillar's remanufactured engines carrying same warranty as new.

**Optional 6th Strategy:** Design for Durability/Longevity — extending product life delays the environmental burden of replacement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TBL with sustainability in general.** TBL specifically requires all three pillars to be explicitly named with manufacturing examples. A generic answer about "environment" scores only partial marks.
>
> 2. **Listing LCA phases in wrong order.** The correct sequence is **Goal & Scope → Inventory → Impact Assessment → Interpretation**. Reversing or omitting any phase costs marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Functional Unit (FU).** FU is the cornerstone of any LCA. If your answer does not define FU, the Phase 1 explanation is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Recycling and Remanufacturing.** Recycling breaks products down to material level (value loss 50–70%). Remanufacturing restores products to functional level (value retention 85–95%). Examiners penalize this confusion.
>
> 5. **Using wrong emission factors.** Always cite the **source of the emission factor** (e.g., "CEA 2023 baseline" or "IPCC AR6 default"). Hard-coding a number without attribution loses credibility.
>
> 6. **Not showing mass balance in numerical problems.** When given manufacturing input/output data, ALWAYS verify mass balance first. Showing this verifies your understanding.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units.** Emissions per kWh, per litre, per kg — make sure units cancel correctly to give **kgCO₂e**, not mixed units.
>
> 8. **Skipping the diagram in LCA questions.** A neat flowchart of the four phases is almost mandatory for full marks on a 7-mark question.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam for a quick mental refresh.**

### ✓ Core Definitions to Memorize

- **Sustainable Manufacturing** — economically-sound production that minimizes environmental impact, conserves resources, and protects people throughout the product life cycle.
- **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — John Elkington, 1994: **People, Planet, Profit**.
- **Cradle to Cradle (C2C)** — McDonough & Braungart, 2002: biological and technical nutrients in perpetual cycles.
- **Industrial Ecology** — industrial systems mimicking natural ecosystems; waste of one becomes input of another.
- **Circular Economy** — Ellen MacArthur Foundation: design out waste, keep materials in use, regenerate nature.
- **Functional Unit (FU)** — quantified reference performance enabling fair product comparison in LCA.
- **Lean Manufacturing** — Toyota Production System philosophy of waste (Muda) elimination across the **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)** or **8 Wastes (DOWNTIME)**.

### ✓ Key Standards to Know

- **ISO 14001:2015** — Environmental Management Systems (EMS).
- **ISO 14040 / 14044** — LCA Principles and Guidelines.
- **ISO 14067** — Carbon Footprint of Products.
- **ISO 14046** — Water Footprint.
- **ISO 45001** — Occupational Health & Safety.
- **ISO 50001** — Energy Management Systems.

### ✓ LCA Four Phases (In Order!)

1. **Goal & Scope Definition** (FU, system boundary)
2. **Life Cycle Inventory** (data collection, mass-energy balance)
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment** (classification + characterization)
4. **Interpretation** (recommendations)

### ✓ 5 R's Hierarchy (Most Preferred → Least Preferred)

**Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Rethink**
(or: **Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Refurbish → Remanufacture → Repurpose → Recycle → Recover**)

### ✓ Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)

- Material Utilization: $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output}}{\text{Total Input}} \times 100\%$
- Recycling Rate: $RR = \dfrac{\text{Recycled Mass}}{\text{Total Material}} \times 100\%$
- Specific Energy Consumption: $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Energy Used}}{\text{Mass of Product}}$ [MJ/kg]
- Carbon Footprint: $CF = \sum (AD_i \times EF_i)$ [kgCO₂e]

### ✓ Key Numerical Values for Indian Context

- India grid emission factor (CEA 2023): **0.71 kgCO₂e/kWh**
- Diesel emission factor: **2.68 kgCO₂e/L**
- LPG emission factor: **2.94 kgCO₂e/kg**
- Global average steel: **1.8–2.0 tonnes CO₂/tonne crude steel**
- One cotton T-shirt water footprint: **~2,700 L**

### ✓ Common Board Exam Question Patterns

1. *"Define sustainable manufacturing and explain its driving forces."* (3 marks)
2. *"Explain Triple Bottom Line with examples."* (3–7 marks)
3. *"Explain the four phases of LCA with diagram."* (7–14 marks)
4. *"Define functional unit. Explain its importance."* (3–7 marks)
5. *"Calculate carbon footprint given activity data and emission factors."* (7 marks numerical)
6. *"Differentiate between linear and circular economy."* (7 marks)
7. *"Explain DfE/DfD/DfR strategies."* (7 marks)
8. *"Discuss pollution prevention hierarchy."* (3–7 marks)
9. *"Explain Cradle to Cradle concept."* (7 marks)
10. *"What are the metrics for sustainable manufacturing performance?"* (3–7 marks)

### ✓ Final Mental Anchor

Whenever you see a sustainable manufacturing question, mentally route it through:
**TBL → 5 R's → LCA → Metrics → Improvement Strategy.**
A complete KTU-grade answer touches ALL of these elements in appropriate depth for the marks allotted.
